# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**Q: Define Cross Entropy loss function. Why is it preferred over Mean Squared Error (MSE) for classification tasks?**

**Model Answer:**

Cross Entropy loss between true distribution $p$ and predicted distribution $q$ is defined as:

$$H(p, q) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} p_c \log q_c$$

For a single sample with one-hot true label $\mathbf{y}$ and predicted probability $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{c} y_c \log \hat{y}_c$$

**Why preferred over MSE:**
1. **Stronger gradients:** For softmax + cross entropy, the gradient is $\hat{y} - y$ (linear in error). For MSE with softmax, the gradient contains the term $\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$ which **vanishes** when the model is confidently wrong.
2. **Probabilistic interpretation:** Cross entropy directly measures the divergence between the true and predicted distributions, making it the natural choice for probability outputs.
3. **Better convergence** in practice for multi-class classification.
*(3 marks — full marks if gradient comparison is included.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 3 Marks
**Q: Explain the concept of parameter sharing in Convolutional Neural Networks. How does it reduce the number of learnable parameters compared to a fully connected network?**

**Model Answer:**

**Parameter Sharing** means that the **same set of kernel weights** is used to compute outputs at **every spatial location** of the input. A single $5 \times 5$ filter, for example, has only **25 weights** (plus 1 bias) regardless of the input image size.

**Comparison Example:**
- **Input:** $32 \times 32 \times 3$ image = 3072 inputs
- **Fully Connected to 1000 neurons:** $3072 \times 1000 + 1000 = \mathbf{3{,}073{,}000}$ parameters
- **Conv Layer with 10 filters of size $5 \times 5$:** $10 \times (5 \times 5 \times 3 + 1) = \mathbf{760}$ parameters

The CNN uses **~4000× fewer parameters** while exploiting the fact that the same edge/pattern detector is useful everywhere in the image.

**Benefits:** (1) Drastically reduced memory, (2) Faster training, (3) Built-in **translation equivariance** — a cat detector works whether the cat is in the top-left or bottom-right.
*(3 marks)*

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions feature **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a) Derive the gradient of the Categorical Cross Entropy loss with respect to the softmax inputs (logits). Show that the gradient simplifies to $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the softmax and loss.**

Given logits $\mathbf{z} = [z_1, \ldots, z_C]$, softmax output is:

$$\hat{y}_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$$

Categorical Cross Entropy loss:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$$

**Step 2: Expand the log-softmax.**

$$\log \hat{y}_i = \log\left(\frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}\right) = z_i - \log\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the loss.**

$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} y_i \left(z_i - \log\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}\right) = -\sum_{i} y_i z_i + \log\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}$$

(Used $\sum_i y_i = 1$.)

**Step 4: Differentiate w.r.t. $z_k$.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = -y_k + \frac{\partial}{\partial z_k} \log\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}$$

For the log-sum-exp term:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial z_k} \log\sum_{j} e^{z_j} = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}} = \hat{y}_k$$

**Step 5: Combine.**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k - y_k}$$

In vector form: $\nabla_z \mathcal{L} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$.

**Interpretation:** The gradient is the **simple difference** between the predicted and true probability vectors — elegant and computationally cheap. This is why softmax + cross entropy is the canonical classification head.

**(7 marks — full marks for completing derivation, 5 marks if step 3 simplification is missing, 3 marks if final result is obtained without intermediate steps.)**

---

**(b) Consider a CNN with the following architecture:**
- **Input:** $64 \times 64 \times 3$ image
- **Conv Layer 1:** 16 filters, each $3 \times 3$, stride 1, padding 1 (same)
- **Max Pool 1:** $2 \times 2$, stride 2
- **Conv Layer 2:** 32 filters, each $3 \times 3$, stride 1, padding 1 (same)
- **Max Pool 2:** $2 \times 2$, stride 2
- **Fully Connected:** 128 neurons → 10 output classes (CIFAR-10)

**Calculate for EACH layer: (i) Output spatial dimensions, (ii) Number of parameters, (iii) Receptive field of one neuron at that layer's output. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Conv Layer 1: Input $64 \times 64 \times 3$, $f=3, s=1, p=1, F=16$

**(i) Output dimensions:**
$$H_{out} = \lfloor (64 + 2(1) - 3)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 64$$
Output: $\mathbf{64 \times 64 \times 16}$

**(ii) Parameters:** $(3 \times 3 \times 3 + 1) \times 16 = 28 \times 16 = \mathbf{448}$

**(iii) Receptive field:** $r_1 = 1 + (3-1) = \mathbf{3}$

---

### Max Pool 1: $f=2, s=2$

**(i) Output dimensions:** $64/2 = \mathbf{32 \times 32 \times 16}$

**(ii) Parameters:** $\mathbf{0}$ (pooling has no learnable parameters)

**(iii) Receptive field:** $r_2 = 3 + (2-1) \times 1 = \mathbf{4}$

---

### Conv Layer 2: Input $32 \times 32 \times 16$, $f=3, s=1, p=1, F=32$

**(i) Output dimensions:** $32 \times 32 \times \mathbf{32}$

**(ii) Parameters:** $(3 \times 3 \times 16 + 1) \times 32 = 145 \times 32 = \mathbf{4{,}640}$

**(iii) Receptive field:** $r_3 = 4 + (3-1) \times 2 = \mathbf{8}$

---

### Max Pool 2: $f=2, s=2$

**(i) Output dimensions:** $16 \times 16 \times 32$

**(ii) Parameters:** $\mathbf{0}$

**(iii) Receptive field:** $r_4 = 8 + (2-1) \times 2 = \mathbf{10}$

---

### Flatten: $16 \times 16 \times 32 = 8{,}192$ vector

### FC Layer (128 neurons):

**(i) Output dimensions:** $\mathbf{128}$

**(ii) Parameters:** $(8192 + 1) \times 128 = 8{,}193 \times 128 = \mathbf{1{,}048{,}704}$

**(iii) Receptive field:** **Entire input image** ($64 \times 64$)

---

### Output Layer (10 classes):

**(i) Output dimensions:** $\mathbf{10}$

**(ii) Parameters:** $(128 + 1) \times 10 = \mathbf{1{,}290}$

**(iii) Receptive field:** **Entire input image**

### Summary Table

| Layer | Output Size | Parameters | Receptive Field |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conv 1 | $64 \times 64 \times 16$ | 448 | 3 |
| Pool 1 | $32 \times 32 \times 16$ | 0 | 4 |
| Conv 2 | $32 \times 32 \times 32$ | 4,640 | 8 |
| Pool 2 | $16 \times 16 \times 32$ | 0 | 10 |
| FC | 128 | 1,048,704 | Full image |
| Output | 10 | 1,290 | Full image |
| **Total** | — | **~1.05M** | — |

> **Key Insight:** The **FC layer dominates** the parameter count (~99.7%) — this is why modern architectures (e.g., **Global Average Pooling** in ResNet) eliminate FC layers.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a) Explain in detail the 2D Convolution operation used in CNNs. Discuss the role of stride, padding, and filter size. Derive the formula for the output spatial dimension. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### 1. The 2D Convolution Operation

The 2D convolution slides a small learnable **kernel** $\mathbf{K}$ of size $f \times f \times D$ over an input volume $\mathbf{I}$ of size $H \times W \times D$ and computes the dot product at every location. For a single channel case:

$$S(i, j) = \sum_{m=0}^{f-1} \sum_{n=0}^{f-1} I(i+m, j+n) \cdot K(m, n)$$

This produces one number in the output **feature map** at position $(i, j)$. The kernel is then shifted by **stride** $s$ pixels and the process repeats.

### 2. Role of Filter Size $f$

The filter size determines the **local receptive field** of each output neuron. A $3 \times 3$ filter means each output neuron "sees" a $3 \times 3$ patch of the input. Larger filters capture larger patterns but have **quadratically more parameters**. Modern CNNs prefer **stacked $3 \times 3$ filters** (e.g., two $3 \times 3$ = one $5 \times 5$ receptive field, but with fewer parameters and more non-linearities).

### 3. Role of Stride $s$

Stride is the number of pixels the kernel shifts at each step. Stride 1 = dense sampling (output nearly same size as input). Stride 2 = output halved in each spatial dimension. **Larger strides** = smaller output volumes, **fewer computations**, but loss of fine spatial detail. Stride is one form of **downsampling** (alternative: pooling).

### 4. Role of Padding $p$

Padding adds $p$ rows/columns of zeros (typically) around the input border. **Two purposes:**
1. **Preserve spatial size:** "Same" padding ($p = (f-1)/2$ for $s=1$) keeps output = input size, allowing deeper networks.
2. **Preserve border information:** Without padding, the border pixels are visited fewer times by the kernel, leading to **information loss** at the edges.

### 5. Derivation of Output Dimension

Let the input have height $H_{in}$. After padding, effective height is $H_{in} + 2p$. The kernel can be placed at vertical positions $0, s, 2s, \ldots$ as long as it does not exceed the padded boundary:

$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - f}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$

The floor operation accounts for cases where the kernel does not fit exactly (e.g., $H_{in} = 7, f = 3, s = 2, p = 0$ → $H_{out} = \lfloor 4/2 \rfloor + 1 = 3$, dropping the last 2 columns).

**Numerical example:** Input $224 \times 224 \times 3$, filter $7 \times 7, s=2, p=3$:
$$H_{out} = \lfloor (224 + 6 - 7)/2 \rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 223/2 \rfloor + 1 = 111 + 1 = 112$$

*(7 marks — 2 for convolution operation, 1 for filter role, 1 for stride, 1 for padding, 2 for derivation with example.)*

---

**(b) A CNN is to classify $28 \times 28$ grayscale handwritten digit images (MNIST, 10 classes). The architecture is:**
- **Conv1:** 8 filters, $5 \times 5$, stride 1, no padding
- **MaxPool1:** $2 \times 2$, stride 2
- **Conv2:** 16 filters, $5 \times 5$, stride 1, no padding
- **MaxPool2:** $2 \times 2$, stride 2
- **FC:** 128 neurons → 10 outputs (with softmax)

**Compute: (i) The output feature map size at each layer, (ii) The total number of trainable parameters, (iii) Justify why softmax + categorical cross entropy is used for the final layer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### (i) Output Feature Map Sizes

| Layer | Calculation | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Input | $28 \times 28 \times 1$ | $28 \times 28 \times 1$ |
| Conv1 | $\lfloor (28 - 5)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 24$ | $\mathbf{24 \times 24 \times 8}$ |
| MaxPool1 | $24/2 = 12$ | $\mathbf{12 \times 12 \times 8}$ |
| Conv2 | $\lfloor (12 - 5)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 8$ | $\mathbf{8 \times 8 \times 16}$ |
| MaxPool2 | $8/2 = 4$ | $\mathbf{4 \times 4 \times 16}$ |
| Flatten | $4 \times 4 \times 16$ | $\mathbf{256}$ |
| FC (128) | — | $\mathbf{128}$ |
| Output (10) | — | $\mathbf{10}$ |

### (ii) Total Trainable Parameters

| Layer | Parameters |
|---|---|
| Conv1 | $(5 \times 5 \times 1 + 1) \times 8 = 26 \times 8 = \mathbf{208}$ |
| MaxPool1 | 0 |
| Conv2 | $(5 \times 5 \times 8 + 1) \times 16 = 201 \times 16 = \mathbf{3{,}216}$ |
| MaxPool2 | 0 |
| FC | $(256 + 1) \times 128 = \mathbf{32{,}896}$ |
| Output | $(128 + 1) \times 10 = \mathbf{1{,}290}$ |
| **Total** | $\mathbf{37{,}610}$ |

### (iii) Why Softmax + Categorical Cross Entropy?

1. **Softmax** converts the 10 raw logits into a **valid probability distribution** (all values in $[0,1]$ summing to 1). This is essential for **multi-class classification** where exactly one class is the answer.
2. **Categorical Cross Entropy** is the **maximum likelihood estimator** under a multinomial likelihood model — it is the **principled** probabilistic loss for mutually exclusive classes.
3. **Gradient elegance:** The combined gradient is simply $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$ (proved in Q1), which provides strong, non-vanishing gradients even when the model is confidently wrong.
4. **Probabilistic interpretation:** The output $\hat{y}_c$ can be directly interpreted as "the model's confidence that the image is class $c$."

**Alternative:** For 2-class problems, **sigmoid + binary cross entropy** is used. For regression, **MSE** or **MAE** is appropriate.

*(7 marks — 2 for part (i), 2 for part (ii), 3 for part (iii) with justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing convolution with cross-correlation:** KTU examiners often test whether students know the difference. Always state: "deep learning uses cross-correlation but calls it convolution."
> 2. **Forgetting the +1 bias in parameter count:** $(f \cdot f \cdot D_{in} + 1) \times F$, NOT $(f \cdot f \cdot D_{in}) \times F$. Examiners specifically dock 0.5–1 mark for this.
> 3. **Wrong padding formula:** $p = (f-1)/2$ only works for $s=1$ and **odd** $f$. Don't blindly apply it for $f=4$ or $s=2$.
> 4. **Confusing BCE and CCE:** Binary is for **2-class**; Categorical is for **multi-class** (mutually exclusive). KTU questions often use 10-class MNIST or CIFAR — must use CCE.
> 5. **In receptive field problems, students often forget to multiply by the previous stride.** Use the recursive formula $r_l = r_{l-1} + (f_l - 1) \cdot s_{l-1}$ (note: $s$ of the *previous* layer).
> 6. **Cross entropy gradient:** Many students write the gradient of cross entropy as $-\sum y_c \log \hat{y}_c$ and leave it. The KTU board expects the **final simplified result** $\hat{y} - y$ with full derivation steps. Half marks for incomplete derivation.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Use this as a last-minute revision checklist before the KTU exam.**

### 📌 Cross Entropy — Key Points
- ✅ **Definition:** $H(p, q) = -\sum p(x) \log q(x)$
- ✅ **BCE** for binary; **CCE** for multi-class.
- ✅ **Combined with softmax:** the gradient is just $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$.
- ✅ Preferred over MSE because it doesn't saturate for confidently-wrong predictions.
- ✅ Penalizes divergence from the true distribution, not magnitude errors.

### 📌 Convolution Operation — Key Points
- ✅ **Kernel** = small learnable filter (typically $3 \times 3$ in modern CNNs).
- ✅ **Stride** $s$ = step size when sliding the kernel.
- ✅ **Padding** $p$ = zeros added to the input border (preserves size & border info).
- ✅ **Output size formula:** $H_{out} = \lfloor (H_{in} + 2p - f)/s \rfloor + 1$
- ✅ **Parameter sharing** = same kernel weights used across all spatial positions.
- ✅ **Sparse connectivity** = each output neuron connects to only a small input patch.
- ✅ Technically a **cross-correlation** in deep learning, but called convolution by convention.

### 📌 CNN Convolutional Layer — Key Points
- ✅ Output volume = (Height × Width × Number of filters $F$).
- ✅ Parameter count = $(f \cdot f \cdot D_{in} + 1) \cdot F$ per layer.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** Layer 1 → edges, Layer 2 → textures, Layer 3 → parts, Layer 4 → objects.
- ✅ **Three properties:** local receptive fields, weight sharing, pooling.
- ✅ Modern architectures: LeNet → AlexNet → VGG → ResNet → EfficientNet.
- ✅ **Receptive field** grows with depth and stride — deeper neurons "see" more of the input.
- ✅ FC layers typically dominate the parameter count (>99% in shallow networks).
- ✅ **Total loss = Categorical Cross Entropy** between softmax output and one-hot true label.

### 📌 Must-Memorize Formulas (Top 5 for KTU)
1. $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c$
2. $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial z_k = \hat{y}_k - y_k$
3. $H_{out} = \lfloor (H_{in} + 2p - f)/s \rfloor + 1$
4. $\text{Params} = (f \cdot f \cdot D_{in} + 1) \cdot F$
5. $r_l = r_{l-1} + (f_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i$

> **Final Tip:** In the KTU exam, **always draw a labeled diagram** for CNN architecture questions. Examiners award partial marks for clear diagrams even if the numerical calculation has minor errors. Use proper notation: $H \times W \times D$ for volumes, $F$ for number of filters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kernels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET865 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kernels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Deep Learning and Machine Learning, a **kernel** is a mathematical function $K(x, x')$ that quantifies the similarity between two data points $x$ and $x'$ in some (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space, without the need to explicitly compute the coordinates of the data in that space. Formally, a kernel is a function:

$$K: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

where $\mathcal{X}$ is the input space. A kernel is said to be **valid** (or **Mercer kernel**) if it satisfies **Mercer's Condition** — namely, it is symmetric and positive semi-definite, meaning the Gram matrix $K_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ is positive semi-definite for any finite set of inputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEAET865 Module 1**
> Kernels form a foundational bridge between classical machine learning (SVMs, Gaussian Processes, Kernel Ridge Regression) and modern deep learning (Neural Tangent Kernels, Convolutional Kernels, Attention Kernels). Understanding the *kernel trick* is essential for grasping how implicit feature spaces power many deep architectures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Similarity Judge"**
> Imagine you are a judge at a fruit-similarity contest. Two apples (red, round, sweet) are clearly more similar than an apple and a watermelon. A kernel function is like a calibrated "similarity score machine" — you feed in two items, and it returns a single number indicating how alike they are. Crucially, this machine has been pre-trained to recognize similarities in a *rich, abstract space* (e.g., "sweetness × roundness × color uniformity"), but you never have to know what that space looks like — you only ever see the final score. This is the essence of the **kernel trick**: working in powerful feature spaces *implicitly*.

## 1.3 Why Kernels Matter in Deep Learning

| Aspect | Classical Kernel Methods | Deep Learning Connection |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature Space** | Implicit (via kernel) | Learned representations |
| **Mapping** | Fixed (e.g., RBF, polynomial) | Data-adaptive (hidden layers) |
| **Capacity Control** | Regularization, kernel choice | Architecture, dropout, batch norm |
| **Limit** | $O(n^2)$ or $O(n^3)$ scaling | Scales to big data |

> [!TIP]
> Modern deep learning research has rediscovered kernels: the **Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK)** describes infinitely-wide neural networks as kernel machines, bridging the two paradigms. Understanding classical kernels is therefore *prerequisite* to understanding cutting-edge deep learning theory.

## 1.4 Standard Kernels — Quick Reference

| Kernel Name | Formula $K(x, x')$ | Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| Linear | $x^{\top} x'$ | Raw dot product |
| Polynomial | $(\gamma x^{\top} x' + c)^{d}$ | Captures interactions of degree $d$ |
| RBF / Gaussian | $\exp(-\gamma \vert \vert x - x' \vert \vert^{2})$ | Localized similarity |
| Sigmoid | $\tanh(\gamma x^{\top} x' + c)$ | Neural-net-like |
| Laplacian | $\exp(-\gamma \vert \vert x - x' \vert \vert_{1})$ | Sparse, robust |
| Cosine | $\frac{x^{\top} x'}{\vert \vert x \vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert x' \vert \vert}$ | Angular similarity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Kernel Trick — Operational Breakdown

The **kernel trick** replaces the inner product of feature vectors $\langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$ with a kernel function $K(x, x')$. The key insight: *many algorithms (SVMs, ridge regression, PCA) depend only on inner products of data points — never on the data points themselves.*

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Feature Map:** Define a (possibly nonlinear) map $\phi: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{H}$ from input space $\mathcal{X}$ to a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$.
- **Step 2 — Algorithm Reformulation:** Rewrite the target algorithm (e.g., SVM dual) so that $x$ and $x'$ appear *only* inside inner products $\langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$.
- **Step 3 — Substitution:** Replace every such inner product with the kernel $K(x, x') = \langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$.
- **Step 4 — Implicit Computation:** Evaluate $K$ directly using a closed-form formula — *never* compute $\phi(x)$ explicitly.
- **Step 5 — Complexity Win:** When $\dim(\mathcal{H})$ is very large or infinite (e.g., RBF), the trick avoids intractable explicit mapping.

## 2.2 Mercer's Theorem — The Validity Certificate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mercer's Theorem (1909)**
> A continuous symmetric function $K(x, x')$ admits an expansion
> $$K(x, x') = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \lambda_i \, \phi_i(x) \, \phi_i(x')$$
> with eigenvalues $\lambda_i \geq 0$ if and only if $K$ is a valid (positive semi-definite) kernel. The eigenfunctions $\{\phi_i\}$ form an orthogonal basis of the feature space $\mathcal{H}$.

In matrix form: the **Gram matrix** $G$ with entries $G_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ must satisfy $v^{\top} G v \geq 0$ for all real vectors $v$.

## 2.3 Properties of Valid Kernels

- **Symmetry:** $K(x, x') = K(x', x)$
- **Positive Semi-Definiteness (PSD):** $G \succeq 0$
- **Closure under operations:** Sum, product, scalar multiple, and limit of PSD kernels are PSD.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Kernel | $K(x, x') = x^{\top} x'$ | Simplest, no nonlinearity |
| 2 | Polynomial Kernel | $K(x, x') = (\gamma \, x^{\top} x' + c)^{d}$ | Degree $d \in \mathbb{N}$ |
| 3 | RBF / Gaussian Kernel | $K(x, x') = \exp(-\gamma \cdot \vert \vert x - x' \vert \vert^{2})$ | $\gamma = 1 / (2\sigma^{2})$ |
| 4 | Sigmoid Kernel | $K(x, x') = \tanh(\gamma \, x^{\top} x' + c)$ | Not always PSD |
| 5 | Laplacian Kernel | $K(x, x') = \exp(-\gamma \cdot \vert \vert x - x' \vert \vert_{1})$ | $\ell_1$ norm variant |
| 6 | Cosine Kernel | $K(x, x') = \frac{x^{\top} x'}{\vert \vert x \vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert x' \vert \vert}$ | Scale-invariant |
| 7 | Gram Matrix | $G_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ | Must be PSD for validity |
| 8 | Mercer Expansion | $K(x, x') = \sum_{i} \lambda_i \phi_i(x) \phi_i(x')$ | $\lambda_i \geq 0$ |
| 9 | Kernel Sum Closure | $K_{\text{new}} = K_1 + K_2$ | Both PSD $\Rightarrow$ PSD |
| 10 | Kernel Product Closure | $K_{\text{new}} = K_1 \cdot K_2$ | Both PSD $\Rightarrow$ PSD |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Kernels power **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** for classification, **Gaussian Processes (GP)** for Bayesian regression, **Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR)** for nonlinear prediction, and **Spectral Clustering** for unsupervised grouping. In deep learning, kernels underpin **Convolutional Neural Networks** (where the convolution kernel slides over feature maps) and modern theoretical tools like the **Neural Tangent Kernel**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Feature Map of the Polynomial Kernel

We will show that the polynomial kernel $K(x, x') = (x^{\top} x' + c)^{2}$ corresponds to an *explicit* finite-dimensional feature map $\phi(x)$ — and that direct computation of $\phi(x)$ has cost $O(d^2)$ while the kernel trick costs $O(d)$.

Assume $x, x' \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ with $x = (x_1, x_2)^{\top}$, $x' = (x_1', x_2')^{\top}$, and $c \in \mathbb{R}$ a constant. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
K(x, x') &= (x^{\top} x' + c)^{2} \\
&= (x_1 x_1' + x_2 x_2' + c)^{2} \\
&= (x_1 x_1')^{2} + (x_2 x_2')^{2} + c^{2} + 2 x_1 x_1' x_2 x_2' + 2 c \, x_1 x_1' + 2 c \, x_2 x_2'
\end{aligned}$$

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- **Row 1:** Substitute the definition of the polynomial kernel of degree $d = 2$ with constant $c$.
- **Row 2:** Expand the dot product $x^{\top} x' = x_1 x_1' + x_2 x_2'$.
- **Row 3:** Apply the binomial identity $(a + b + c)^{2} = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2ab + 2ac + 2bc$ with $a = x_1 x_1'$, $b = x_2 x_2'$, $c = c$.

This can be factored as the dot product of two feature vectors:

$$K(x, x') = \phi(x)^{\top} \phi(x')$$

where

$$\phi(x) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1^{2} \\ x_2^{2} \\ c \\ \sqrt{2} \, x_1 x_2 \\ \sqrt{2c} \, x_1 \\ \sqrt{2c} \, x_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \phi(x') = \begin{bmatrix} (x_1')^{2} \\ (x_2')^{2} \\ c \\ \sqrt{2} \, x_1' x_2' \\ \sqrt{2c} \, x_1' \\ \sqrt{2c} \, x_2' \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification by direct dot product:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi(x)^{\top} \phi(x') &= x_1^{2} (x_1')^{2} + x_2^{2} (x_2')^{2} + c^{2} + 2 x_1 x_2 x_1' x_2' + 2c \, x_1 x_1' + 2c \, x_2 x_2' \\
&= (x_1 x_1' + x_2 x_2' + c)^{2} \\
&= K(x, x') \;\; \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**Complexity comparison:**

| Method | Cost | Dim of $\phi(x)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Direct feature map | $O\!\left(\binom{d + n}{n}\right)$ | $\binom{d + n}{n}$ |
| Kernel trick | $O(n \cdot d)$ | $1$ (scalar output) |

For $n = 100$ features and $d = 5$, the explicit map lives in $\mathbb{R}^{96{,}560{,}646}$ — *completely infeasible* without the kernel trick.

## 3.2 Derivation: RBF Kernel as Infinite-Dimensional Feature Map

The Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel is defined as:

$$K(x, x') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \vert x - x' \vert \vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

We can rewrite this in a kernel-trick-friendly form by expanding the squared norm:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert \vert x - x' \vert \vert^{2} &= (x - x')^{\top} (x - x') \\
&= \vert \vert x \vert \vert^{2} - 2 x^{\top} x' + \vert \vert x' \vert \vert^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back:

$$\begin{aligned}
K(x, x') &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \vert x \vert \vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{x^{\top} x'}{\sigma^{2}}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \vert x' \vert \vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \\
&= \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \vert x \vert \vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \vert x' \vert \vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \exp\!\left(\frac{x^{\top} x'}{\sigma^{2}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- **Row 1:** Algebraic expansion of the squared Euclidean distance.
- **Row 2:** Apply the identity $\exp(a + b + c) = \exp(a)\exp(b)\exp(c)$ with $a = -\frac{\vert\vert x \vert\vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}$, $b = \frac{x^{\top} x'}{\sigma^{2}}$, $c = -\frac{\vert\vert x' \vert\vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}$.
- **Row 3:** Reorder the multiplicative factors for clarity.

Now use the **Taylor series expansion** of $\exp(u) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{u^{k}}{k!}$ with $u = \frac{x^{\top} x'}{\sigma^{2}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\exp\!\left(\frac{x^{\top} x'}{\sigma^{2}}\right) &= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k!} \left(\frac{x^{\top} x'}{\sigma^{2}}\right)^{k} \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k! \, \sigma^{2k}} (x^{\top} x')^{k}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $(x^{\top} x')^{k}$ is a degree-$k$ polynomial in the components of $x$ and $x'$, each term corresponds to a *finite-dimensional* feature map $\phi_k(x)$. Therefore:

$$K(x, x') = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k! \, \sigma^{2k}} \langle \phi_k(x), \phi_k(x') \rangle$$

The full feature map $\phi(x)$ is the **infinite concatenation** of all $\phi_k(x)$ — it lives in an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space. This is why the RBF kernel is so powerful: it implicitly maps data to an *infinite-dimensional* space where linear separability becomes almost trivial.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** The RBF kernel is a universal approximator. Under mild conditions, a kernel machine using the RBF kernel can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary precision (Micchelli et al., 2006).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Linear | Polynomial | RBF | Sigmoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PSD always?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Hyperparameters** | 0 | $\gamma, c, d$ | $\gamma$ | $\gamma, c$ |
| **Feature dim** | $n$ | $\binom{n+d}{d}$ | $\infty$ | Undefined |
| **Decision boundary** | Linear | Polynomial | Nonlinear (local) | Nonlinear |
| **Scalability** | Excellent | Good | Poor ($O(n^2)$) | Good |
| **Deep-learning analog** | Linear layer | — | NTK (infinite-width NN) | Single neuron |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Kernel Machine Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Vector x] --> featA[Feature Map phi x]
    inputB[Input Vector x prime] --> featB[Feature Map phi x prime]
    featA --> inner[Inner Product in Feature Space]
    featB --> inner
    inner --> kernel[K x x prime equals scalar]
    inputA -.-> shortcut[Kernel Trick Path]
    inputB -.-> shortcut
    shortcut --> kernel
    kernel --> output[Decision Function f x]
    output --> pred[Predicted Label y hat]
```

**Description of nodes:**
- `inputA` and `inputB`: Original data points $x$ and $x'$ in input space $\mathcal{X}$.
- `featA` and `featB`: The explicit feature map $\phi(\cdot)$ (often intractable).
- `inner`: Inner product computation $\langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$.
- `kernel`: The kernel function $K(x, x')$ — returns a single scalar.
- `shortcut`: The kernel trick bypass — go directly from raw inputs to $K(x, x')$ without ever computing $\phi$.
- `output`: Final decision function, e.g., $f(x) = \sum_i \alpha_i K(x_i, x)$.
- `pred`: Predicted label $\hat{y}$.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Kernel Trick in SVM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw Data in Input Space] --> check{Nonlinearly Separable?}
    check -->|Yes| map[Apply Feature Map phi]
    check -->|No| linear[Train Linear SVM]
    map --> highdim[High Dimensional Feature Space]
    highdim --> linearsep[Now Linearly Separable]
    linearsep --> linear
    linear --> dual[Dual Form Uses Only Inner Products]
    dual --> trick[Replace Inner Product with K x x prime]
    trick --> trained[Trained Kernel SVM]
    trained --> predict[Predict on New Points]
```

**Description of nodes:**
- `raw`: Original 2D data points.
- `check`: Decision point — is the data linearly separable?
- `map`: Apply a (possibly nonlinear) feature map $\phi$.
- `highdim`: The mapped space (e.g., $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ for quadratic features).
- `linearsep`: In the high-dim space, classes become linearly separable.
- `linear`: Standard linear SVM training.
- `dual`: The SVM dual formulation depends only on $\langle \phi(x_i), \phi(x_j) \rangle$.
- `trick`: Substitute the kernel $K(x_i, x_j)$ for the inner product.
- `trained`: Resulting kernel SVM model.
- `predict`: Inference on new points using the trained model.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: From Kernels to Deep Learning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    klassical[Classical Kernel Methods SVM GP KRR] --> bridge[Neural Tangent Kernel NTK]
    bridge --> infinite[Infinite Width Neural Network]
    infinite --> dl[Modern Deep Learning]
    dl --> conv[Convolutional Kernels in CNNs]
    dl --> attn[Attention Kernels in Transformers]
    dl --> gpdl[Deep Gaussian Processes]
    klassical --> dl
```

**Description of nodes:**
- `klassical`: Traditional kernel-based algorithms (SVM, GP, KRR).
- `bridge`: The Neural Tangent Kernel — a theoretical bridge (Jacot et al., 2018).
- `infinite`: Infinitely-wide neural networks behave like kernel machines.
- `dl`: Modern deep learning architectures.
- `conv`: Convolutional kernels/filters in CNNs (e.g., $3 \times 3$ filters).
- `attn`: Attention kernels in Transformers ($Q K^{\top}$).
- `gpdl`: Deep Gaussian Processes — hierarchical kernel models.
- The lower link shows that classical kernels also directly influenced deep learning through regularization theory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`

**Q: Define a kernel function. List any two commonly used kernel functions with their mathematical expressions.**

**Model Answer:**

A *kernel function* $K(x, x')$ is a symmetric, positive semi-definite function that computes the inner product between two data points in a (possibly high-dimensional) feature space, without explicitly computing the feature map.

$$K(x, x') = \langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$$

**Two commonly used kernels:**

- **Polynomial Kernel:** $K(x, x') = (\gamma \, x^{\top} x' + c)^{d}$
- **Radial Basis Function (RBF) Kernel:** $K(x, x') = \exp(-\gamma \cdot \vert \vert x - x' \vert \vert^{2})$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Students who only write the formula without stating the *inner product* interpretation typically lose 1 mark. Always connect the kernel to the feature space.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`

**Q: What is the kernel trick? Why is it computationally advantageous?**

**Model Answer:**

The **kernel trick** is a method in machine learning whereby the inner product $\langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$ in a high-dimensional (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space is replaced by a kernel function $K(x, x')$, computed directly in the input space.

**Computational Advantage:** Computing $\phi(x)$ explicitly is often infeasible — for a degree-$d$ polynomial kernel with $n$-dimensional input, the feature space has dimension $\binom{n + d}{d}$, which grows exponentially. The kernel trick bypasses this by using a closed-form $K(x, x')$, reducing the cost to $O(nd)$ from $O\!\left(\binom{n+d}{d}\right)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing "kernel" with "convolution filter." In Module 1 of this course, *kernel* means the kernel function for similarity. Convolution kernels (filters) in CNNs will be covered in later modules.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Question Paper]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** State and explain Mercer's theorem. Show that the polynomial kernel $K(x, x') = (x^{\top} x' + 1)^{2}$ is a valid kernel by explicitly constructing its feature map for $x, x' \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the properties that a function must satisfy to be a valid (Mercer) kernel. Show that the sum of two valid kernels is also a valid kernel.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Mercer's Theorem & Polynomial Kernel Feature Map

**Mercer's Theorem:** A continuous symmetric function $K(x, x')$ can be expressed as an expansion

$$K(x, x') = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \lambda_i \, \phi_i(x) \, \phi_i(x')$$

where $\lambda_i \geq 0$ and $\{\phi_i\}$ are orthogonal eigenfunctions, **if and only if** $K$ is positive semi-definite. This means the Gram matrix $G$ with $G_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ satisfies $v^{\top} G v \geq 0$ for all vectors $v$.

**Explicit feature map for $K(x, x') = (x^{\top} x' + 1)^{2}$ with $x = (x_1, x_2)^{\top}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K(x, x') &= (x_1 x_1' + x_2 x_2' + 1)^{2} \\
&= x_1^{2} (x_1')^{2} + x_2^{2} (x_2')^{2} + 1 \\
&\quad + 2 x_1 x_2 x_1' x_2' + 2 x_1 x_1' + 2 x_2 x_2'
\end{aligned}$$

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- **Row 1:** Substitute the kernel definition and expand the dot product.
- **Row 2:** Square the trinomial using $(a + b + c)^{2} = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2ab + 2ac + 2bc$.

This equals $\phi(x)^{\top} \phi(x')$ where:

$$\phi(x) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1^{2} \\ x_2^{2} \\ 1 \\ \sqrt{2} \, x_1 x_2 \\ \sqrt{2} \, x_1 \\ \sqrt{2} \, x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Since we have constructed a finite-dimensional feature map $\phi$ such that $K(x, x') = \phi(x)^{\top} \phi(x')$, the kernel is automatically valid (PSD). $\blacksquare$

#### Part (b) — Properties & Sum Closure

**Required Properties of a Valid Kernel:**

1. **Symmetry:** $K(x, x') = K(x', x)$ for all $x, x'$.
2. **Positive Semi-Definiteness (PSD):** For any finite set $\{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$ and any real vector $\alpha = (\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n)^{\top}$:

$$\sum_{i,j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j K(x_i, x_j) \geq 0$$

**Proof that the sum of two valid kernels is valid:**

Let $K_1$ and $K_2$ be valid kernels. Define $K(x, x') = K_1(x, x') + K_2(x, x')$. We verify both properties:

- **Symmetry:**
$$K(x, x') = K_1(x, x') + K_2(x, x') = K_1(x', x) + K_2(x', x) = K(x', x)$$

- **Positive Semi-Definiteness:** For any $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j K(x_i, x_j) &= \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j \big[ K_1(x_i, x_j) + K_2(x_i, x_j) \big] \\
&= \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j K_1(x_i, x_j) + \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j K_2(x_i, x_j) \\
&\geq 0 + 0 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- **Row 1:** Substitute the definition $K = K_1 + K_2$.
- **Row 2:** Distribute the sum.
- **Row 3:** Each sum is non-negative because $K_1$ and $K_2$ are individually PSD.

Hence $K$ is a valid kernel. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) Pitfall:**
> A common mistake is to claim "sum of PSD matrices is PSD" without invoking the specific property $\sum \alpha_i \alpha_j G_{ij} \geq 0$. Always show the explicit sum and cite the validity of each constituent kernel. *Also: do not confuse this with "product is PSD" — that is a separate, also-true theorem which the question does not ask for.*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Question Paper]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the RBF (Gaussian) kernel. Show via Taylor expansion that it corresponds to an infinite-dimensional feature map.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the linear, polynomial, and RBF kernels with respect to: (i) feature space dimensionality, (ii) decision boundary complexity, and (iii) computational cost. State one real-world application where each would be preferred.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — RBF Kernel & Infinite-Dimensional Feature Map

The **RBF (Radial Basis Function) kernel**, also called the **Gaussian kernel**, is defined as:

$$K(x, x') = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \vert x - x' \vert \vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

It measures similarity as a decreasing function of Euclidean distance — points close together receive high similarity scores, distant points receive near-zero scores. The bandwidth parameter $\sigma$ controls the locality: small $\sigma$ gives very local influence, large $\sigma$ gives smoother, more global influence.

**Derivation of the infinite-dimensional feature map:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K(x, x') &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \vert x - x' \vert \vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \\
&= \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \vert x \vert \vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \exp\!\left(\frac{x^{\top} x'}{\sigma^{2}}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert \vert x' \vert \vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- **Row 1:** Definition of the RBF kernel.
- **Row 2:** Expand $\vert\vert x - x' \vert\vert^{2}$ and apply $\exp(a + b + c) = \exp(a)\exp(b)\exp(c)$.

Applying the Taylor series $\exp(u) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{u^{k}}{k!}$ with $u = \frac{x^{\top} x'}{\sigma^{2}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\exp\!\left(\frac{x^{\top} x'}{\sigma^{2}}\right) &= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(x^{\top} x')^{k}}{k! \, \sigma^{2k}} \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k! \, \sigma^{2k}} \sum_{\vert \alpha \vert = k} \binom{k}{\alpha} x^{\alpha} (x')^{\alpha}
\end{aligned}$$

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- **Row 1:** Direct application of the Taylor expansion.
- **Row 2:** Expand $(x^{\top} x')^{k}$ as a sum of monomials $x^{\alpha} = x_1^{\alpha_1} x_2^{\alpha_2} \cdots x_n^{\alpha_n}$ with multi-index $\alpha$ and $\vert \alpha \vert = k$.

Each monomial $x^{\alpha}$ can be viewed as one dimension of an infinite-dimensional feature vector. Therefore:

$$K(x, x') = \langle \phi_{\infty}(x), \phi_{\infty}(x') \rangle_{\mathcal{H}}$$

where $\phi_{\infty}$ lives in a Hilbert space of *infinite* dimension. This is why the RBF kernel is so expressive: it effectively maps data into an infinitely rich feature space where any continuous function can be approximated (universal approximation property). $\blacksquare$

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis

| Aspect | Linear | Polynomial | RBF |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Feature dim** | $n$ (input dim) | $\binom{n + d}{d}$ | $\infty$ |
| **(ii) Decision boundary** | Hyperplane | Polynomial curve of degree $d$ | Arbitrarily curved (universal) |
| **(iii) Computational cost** | $O(nd)$ | $O(nd)$ | $O(nd)$ training, $O(n^2)$ storage |

**Real-world applications:**

- **Linear Kernel — Preferred for:** Text classification with high-dimensional sparse features (e.g., bag-of-words). The large feature dimension and approximate linear separability make the linear kernel both sufficient and extremely fast. *Example:* Spam detection with logistic regression on TF-IDF features.
- **Polynomial Kernel — Preferred for:** Image processing tasks where moderate nonlinear interactions (e.g., pixel-pair correlations) matter. *Example:* Early hand-crafted feature classification in computer vision.
- **RBF Kernel — Preferred for:** Nonlinear regression, time-series forecasting, and any problem where the relationship is highly nonlinear but training data is limited. *Example:* Medical diagnosis, financial market prediction with Gaussian Processes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) Pitfall:**
> Students often write generic statements like "RBF is best." This will *lose marks*. Examiners expect a *nuanced* comparison grounded in the three sub-criteria given in the question. Always tie your application example to the *strength* of the kernel (e.g., "RBF is preferred for medical diagnosis *because* of its universal approximation property with limited data").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Kernels in Deep Learning (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** A kernel is a function $K(x, x') = \langle \phi(x), \phi(x') \rangle$ that computes inner products in a (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space.
- **Kernel Trick:** Substitute inner products in the algorithm with a closed-form $K$ — avoid computing $\phi$ explicitly.
- **Mercer's Theorem:** A continuous symmetric kernel is valid (PSD) iff all eigenvalues of the associated integral operator are non-negative.
- **Validity Conditions:** (1) Symmetry, (2) Positive Semi-Definiteness of the Gram matrix.
- **Closure Properties:** Valid kernels are closed under sum, product, scalar multiple, and pointwise limit.
- **Standard Kernels:**
  - *Linear:* $K(x, x') = x^{\top} x'$
  - *Polynomial:* $K(x, x') = (\gamma \, x^{\top} x' + c)^{d}$
  - *RBF / Gaussian:* $K(x, x') = \exp(-\gamma \cdot \vert \vert x - x' \vert \vert^{2})$
  - *Sigmoid:* $K(x, x') = \tanh(\gamma \, x^{\top} x' + c)$ — *not always PSD*
- **Cost-Benefit:** Polynomial feature maps scale as $\binom{n + d}{d}$ — kernel trick reduces to $O(nd)$. RBF maps to infinite dimensions — kernel trick still costs only $O(nd)$.
- **Universal Approximation:** RBF kernel can approximate any continuous function on compact domains to arbitrary precision.
- **Deep Learning Bridge:** Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) describes infinitely-wide neural networks as kernel machines — connects classical kernels to deep learning theory.
- **Common Pitfall:** Do not confuse "kernel function" (similarity measure) with "convolution kernel/filter" (used in CNNs) — they are different concepts.
- **Mark-Winning Tip:** Always show both the *feature map intuition* and the *closed-form kernel* when describing a kernel — examiners reward dual perspectives.
- **PSD Check (Exam Trick):** To quickly verify kernel validity, pick 3 sample points, build the $3 \times 3$ Gram matrix, and check that all eigenvalues are $\geq 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Padding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET865 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Padding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Padding** in the context of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Deep Learning is a spatial preprocessing operation applied to a tensor (typically a 2D input image or 1D/3D feature map) in which extra elements — usually **zero-valued entries** — are appended symmetrically (or asymmetrically) along the borders of the tensor **before** a convolution, pooling, or kernel-based operation is performed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Padding is the process of adding extra pixels (most commonly zeros, but sometimes reflected or replicated values) around the boundary of the input feature map to preserve spatial resolution, control the size of output feature maps, and allow kernel filters to operate fully on the edge pixels of the input.*

Formally, given an input tensor $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$, a padding value $p$ produces a padded tensor $I_p \in \mathbb{R}^{(H+2p) \times (W+2p) \times C}$ such that:

$$
I_p(i+p, j+p, c) = I(i, j, c) \quad \forall \; 0 \le i < H, \; 0 \le j < W
$$

The original input occupies the *interior* of the padded tensor, and the added boundary region of thickness $p$ is filled according to the chosen **padding mode**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Picture Frame:**
> Imagine you are holding a beautiful photograph and want to fit it into a slightly larger ornate **picture frame** (a wooden border). The photograph is your *input feature map*, and the frame is the *padding*. Before the photograph goes into the frame, you attach a border of empty cardboard around it so that it fills the frame perfectly. Similarly, in deep learning, the convolution kernel (a small sliding window) "needs room to move" near the edges of an image. Without padding, the kernel would have to stop early or produce shrunken outputs. Padding gives the kernel the extra "border" it needs to process edge pixels properly, just like the cardboard gives the photograph the room to fit in its frame.

**Another simple analogy:** Think of a **rubber stamp** sliding over a piece of paper. If you want the stamp to imprint right up to the paper's edge, you would need to extend the paper slightly beyond the original boundary — that extension is the **padding**.

## 1.3 Why Padding is Critical in Deep Learning

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Preserve Spatial Dimensions** | Without padding, every convolution shrinks the feature map. Stacking many layers would rapidly reduce the image to a uselessly small size. |
| **Process Edge Pixels Equitably** | Without padding, border pixels are visited far fewer times by the kernel than central pixels, biasing the network. |
| **Enable Deeper Architectures** | "Same" padding allows construction of very deep networks (ResNet, VGG) without spatial collapse. |
| **Control Receptive Field Growth** | Different padding modes control how quickly the receptive field of neurons expands. |
| **Boundary Information Preservation** | Useful in segmentation (U-Net), image generation (GANs), and signal processing tasks. |

## 1.4 Standard Padding Modes Used in Industry

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *Name the different types of padding used in CNNs.*

1. **Valid Padding (No Padding):** $p = 0$. Output shrinks.
2. **Same Padding (Zero Padding):** $p = \lfloor k/2 \rfloor$ (for stride 1). Output size equals input size.
3. **Full Padding:** $p = k - 1$. Kernel starts completely outside the input.
4. **Causal Padding:** Used in autoregressive models (e.g., WaveNet); pads only on the left for 1D signals.
5. **Reflective (Mirror) Padding:** Reflects pixel values at the boundary ($ \text{bcd} \rightarrow \text{dcbabcda}$).
6. **Replicate (Edge) Padding:** Repeats the edge pixel value outward.
7. **Constant Padding:** Fills with a constant (default is 0 — most common in CNNs).
8. **Circular Padding:** Wraps around (e.g., $a b c d \rightarrow d a b c d a b c$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Convolution Operation with Padding — Step-by-Step

Let us deconstruct what happens mathematically when padding is applied.

### Step 1: Take the Original Input Feature Map
A 2D image (or hidden layer feature map) of height $H$ and width $W$:

$$
I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W}
$$

### Step 2: Construct the Padded Tensor
Insert $p$ rows/columns on each of the four sides (top, bottom, left, right). The new spatial dimensions become:

$$
H_p = H + 2p, \qquad W_p = W + 2p
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Convolution
A kernel $K$ of size $k \times k$ slides over the padded tensor with stride $s$. The output spatial dimension $O$ is given by the **canonical CNN output formula:**

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

### Step 4: Apply Activation
The convolution output is then passed through a non-linear activation function $f(\cdot)$ (e.g., ReLU) to produce the final feature map of the layer.

## 2.2 Deriving the Padding Required for "Same" Output Size

A common KTU exam question: *What value of padding is needed so that the output feature map has the same size as the input?*

**Setting up the equation:** We want $O = H$. For a stride of $s = 1$:

$$
H = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - k}{1} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

Ignoring the floor (assuming exact division):

$$
H = H + 2p - k + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2p = k - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \frac{k-1}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** For an **odd-sized kernel** $k$ (e.g., 3, 5, 7), choosing $p = (k-1)/2$ with stride 1 gives an output **exactly equal** to the input dimension. This is the principle behind **"Same" Padding** in TensorFlow/Keras.

For **even-sized kernels**, "same" padding is asymmetric: $p_{\text{top}} = \lfloor k/2 \rfloor$ and $p_{\text{bottom}} = \lceil k/2 \rceil$ (and similarly for left/right).

## 2.3 The Receptive Field Equation with Padding

The **effective receptive field (ERF)** of a neuron after $L$ convolutional layers, each with kernel size $k_l$ and padding $p_l$, is:

$$
r_L = r_{L-1} + (k_L - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{L-1} s_i
$$

with $r_0 = 1$. Padding **does not** change the receptive field size, but it **changes which input pixels can actually be reached** by the network.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize These.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Padded Input Height | $H_p = H + 2p$ | Same for width |
| 2 | Output Size (General) | $O = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Canonical CNN formula |
| 3 | Padding for "Same" (s=1, odd k) | $p = \frac{k-1}{2}$ | Output = Input size |
| 4 | Total Padded Pixels | $2p(H + W) + 4p^2$ | For 2D feature map |
| 5 | Number of Output Channels | $C_{out} = \text{number of filters}$ | Independent of padding |
| 6 | Parameter Count per Layer | $(k \cdot k \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out}$ | Padding adds no learnable parameters |
| 7 | Output Volume Shape | $H_{out} \times W_{out} \times C_{out}$ | Standard CNN notation |
| 8 | Receptive Field (Layer $\ell$) | $r_\ell = r_{\ell-1} + (k_\ell - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{\ell-1} s_i$ | Padding does not affect $r$ |
| 9 | Asymmetric "Same" Padding (even k) | $p_{\text{top}}=\lfloor k/2 \rfloor, \; p_{\text{bottom}}=\lceil k/2 \rceil$ | Avoids checkerboard artifacts |
| 10 | Transposed Conv Padding | $p_{\text{out}} = k - p_{\text{in}} - 1$ | For upsampling / decoder paths |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bars inside the table above** — absolute value / norm symbols are written using $\vert \cdot \vert$ syntax in text to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Padding

| Field | Application of Padding |
|---|---|
| **Medical Imaging** | Tumor segmentation with U-Net uses **same padding** to preserve lesion edges. |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Semantic segmentation of road scenes relies on padding to keep small distant objects (pedestrians, signs) detectable. |
| **Audio / Speech (WaveNet)** | **Causal padding** ensures the model does not "see the future" during real-time waveform generation. |
| **Generative Models (GANs, Diffusion)** | Padding strategies directly affect generation artifacts (checkerboard patterns). |
| **Satellite / Remote Sensing** | Reflective padding is preferred for terrain classification to avoid introducing artificial zero-region noise. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Output Dimension with Padding, Stride, and Dilation

**Problem Setup:** We derive the exact output dimension $O$ of a single convolutional layer for an input of height $H$, padded by $p$ on top and bottom, convolved with a kernel of size $k$ and stride $s$. We include the case of **dilation $d$** (KTU 2024 Module 1 includes dilated/atrous convolutions in many syllabi).

### Step 1 — Write the Effective Kernel Size with Dilation

A dilated kernel has its elements spaced apart by $d$ positions. The **effective** kernel size becomes:

$$
k_{\text{eff}} = k + (k - 1)(d - 1) = k \cdot d - (d - 1) - 1 + 1 = d \cdot (k - 1) + 1
$$

**Logic:** With dilation $d$, the kernel "stretches" so that consecutive elements are $d$ indices apart. The number of gaps added is $(k-1)(d-1)$, so total span is original size + gaps.

### Step 2 — Substitute $k_{\text{eff}}$ into the Standard Output Formula

The general output formula with stride is:

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{\text{eff}} - k_{\text{eff}}}{s} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

where $H_{\text{eff}} = H + 2p$ is the padded input height.

### Step 3 — Insert All Quantities

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - \big[d(k-1) + 1\big]}{s} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Numerator

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - d \cdot (k-1) - 1}{s} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

### Step 5 — Final General Formula

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - d(k-1) - 1}{s} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

This is the **most general** output dimension formula in CNNs. KTU examiners frequently ask students to derive or apply this.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Standard Convolution

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Input $H = 32$, kernel $k = 5$, stride $s = 1$, dilation $d = 1$, padding $p = 2$. Find $O$.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $d(k-1) + 1 = 1 \cdot 4 + 1 = 5$.

**Step 2:** Padded input $H + 2p = 32 + 4 = 36$.

**Step 3:** Apply the formula:

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{36 - 5}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 31 \rfloor + 1 = 32
$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion — Output height = **32**, identical to input. This confirms that $p = 2 = (k-1)/2 = (5-1)/2$ gives "same" padding for $k=5$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Stride > 1

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Input $H = 224$ (e.g., ImageNet), kernel $k = 3$, stride $s = 2$, padding $p = 1$. Find $O$.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1:** $d(k-1) + 1 = 1 \cdot 2 + 1 = 3$.

**Step 2:** Padded input $= 224 + 2(1) = 226$.

**Step 3:** Apply formula:

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{226 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 111.5 \rfloor + 1 = 111 + 1 = 112
$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion — Output height = **112** (exactly half of 224, as expected for stride 2 with same padding).

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Effect of Zero Padding on Pixel Visit Count

**Question:** A $3 \times 3$ kernel is applied with stride 1 to a $5 \times 5$ image.

**(a)** Without padding, how many times is the **center pixel** visited vs. the **corner pixel**?

**(b)** With padding $p=1$, recompute these counts.

### Solution (a) — No Padding

**Center pixel visits:** The kernel can be centered at the center pixel exactly **once** (position (3,3) in 1-indexed coordinates).

**Corner pixel visits:** The corner pixel is included in the kernel only when the kernel's top-left is at position (1,1). So the corner is visited **once** as well — but only for 1 of the 9 kernel positions. The center is the "focus" of 1 position out of 9. Let's count total visits:

**Total number of kernel placements (valid conv):** $(5-3+1) \times (5-3+1) = 3 \times 3 = 9$ positions.

**Center pixel visit count:** The center pixel (3,3) appears in the kernel output for every kernel position where (3,3) is within the $3 \times 3$ window. Counting: kernel top-left $i \in \{1, 2, 3\}$, $j \in \{1, 2, 3\}$. Center (3,3) is in window iff $i \le 3 \le i+2$ and $j \le 3 \le j+2$, i.e., $i \in \{1,2,3\}$, $j \in \{1,2,3\}$. So **9 visits** (in fact, all 9 windows contain the center).

**Corner pixel visit count:** Pixel (1,1) is in window iff $i \le 1 \le i+2$ and $j \le 1 \le j+2$, i.e., $i = 1$, $j = 1$. So **1 visit**.

**Ratio:** 9 : 1 — strong bias towards center, edges are neglected.

### Solution (b) — With $p=1$

**Padded image size:** $(5 + 2) \times (5 + 2) = 7 \times 7$.

**Number of kernel placements:** $(7 - 3 + 1) \times (7 - 3 + 1) = 5 \times 5 = 25$ positions.

**Center pixel visit count:** Pixel (3,3) (the original center) is now contained in windows where $i \le 3 \le i+2$ and $j \le 3 \le j+2$, with $i, j \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$. That gives $i \in \{1,2,3\}$, $j \in \{1,2,3\}$, so still **9 visits**.

**Corner pixel visit count:** Pixel (1,1) is in window iff $i = 1$ and $j = 1$. Still **1 visit**. So padding alone does **not equalize** visit counts for image corners — but it does increase the **overall** information captured from edges.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often think padding "fixes" the edge-pixel under-representation problem. It does **not** fully equalize it. The visit count of corner pixels relative to central pixels is *not* changed by valid-vs-same padding for a single layer; what padding does is allow the edge to be processed at all (without it, edge pixels are dropped from the output entirely). True equalization requires **architectural** choices like **reflective** or **circular** padding in deeper layers.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Padding Modes

| Padding Mode | Boundary Fill | Output Size | Preserves Edge Pixels | Adds Noise | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Valid (No Padding)** | None | Shrinks | ❌ No | None | Output layers, pooling |
| **Same (Zero)** | Zeros | Same as input | ✅ Yes | Some (zero artifacts) | General CNNs (VGG, ResNet) |
| **Full** | Zeros (more) | Expands | ✅ Yes | More zeros | Inverting convolutions |
| **Causal** | Zeros (left only, 1D) | Same (1D) | ✅ Yes | Minimal | WaveNet, autoregressive |
| **Reflective** | Mirrored values | Same | ✅ Yes | None | Image inpainting, segmentation |
| **Replicate / Edge** | Edge value repeated | Same | ✅ Yes | None | Medical imaging |
| **Circular** | Wrap-around | Same | ✅ Yes | None | Spherical / periodic data |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Padding Operation Block-Level Flow

The following diagram describes the *functional architecture* of how padding integrates into a convolutional layer, which is the diagram most often expected in KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature Map H x W x C] --> B{Padding Mode?}
    B -- Valid --> C1[p = 0 : No padding applied]
    B -- Same --> C2[p = floor k minus 1 over 2 : Zero padding]
    B -- Full --> C3[p = k minus 1 : Zero padding expanded]
    B -- Reflective --> C4[Mirror boundary pixels]
    B -- Replicate --> C5[Repeat edge pixel value]
    B -- Causal --> C6[Asymmetric zero padding 1D]

    C1 --> D[Padded Tensor H plus 2p x W plus 2p x C]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D

    D --> E[Apply Convolution with k x k Kernel and Stride s]
    E --> F[Add Bias Term]
    F --> G[Apply Activation Function ReLU Sigmoid etc]
    G --> H[Output Feature Map O x O x C out]

    H --> I{Receptive Field Update}
    I --> J[Pass to Next Layer or Output]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style D fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
    style E fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style H fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style J fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Spatial Geometry of "Same" Padding (3×3 Kernel, 5×5 Input)

This diagram shows the spatial mechanics of "same" padding for a 5×5 input convolved with a 3×3 kernel, padding $p=1$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Original Input 5 x 5]
        I1[1,1] --- I2[1,2] --- I3[1,3] --- I4[1,4] --- I5[1,5]
        I1 --- J1[2,1]
        I2 --- J2[2,2]
        I3 --- J3[2,3]
        I4 --- J4[2,4]
        I5 --- J5[2,5]
    end

    subgraph PAD[Padded Tensor 7 x 7 with p = 1]
        P0A[0,0] --- P0B[0,1] --- P0C[0,2] --- P0D[0,3] --- P0E[0,4] --- P0F[0,5] --- P0G[0,6]
        P1A[1,0] --- P1B[1,1] --- P1C[1,2] --- P1D[1,3] --- P1E[1,4] --- P1F[1,5] --- P1G[1,6]
        P2A[2,0] --- P2B[2,1] --- P2C[2,2] --- P2D[2,3] --- P2E[2,4] --- P2F[2,5] --- P2G[2,6]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Feature Map 5 x 5]
        O1[1,1] --- O2[1,2] --- O3[1,3] --- O4[1,4] --- O5[1,5]
    end

    IN --> PAD
    PAD --> OUT
```

**Reading the diagram:** Red cells = added padding (zeros). Green cells = original input image. After padding, the 5×5 input becomes a 7×7 tensor, and applying the 3×3 kernel with stride 1 produces an output of size **5×5** (same as input).

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Choosing Padding in CNN Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Designing a CNN Layer] --> Q1{Is output dimension preservation critical?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the task edge-sensitive like segmentation?}
    Q1 -- No --> VP[Use Valid Padding p = 0]
    Q2 -- Yes --> RP[Use Reflective or Replicate Padding]
    Q2 -- No --> SP[Use Same Zero Padding p = floor k minus 1 over 2]

    START --> Q3{Is this a 1D sequential or autoregressive task?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> CP[Use Causal Padding]

    START --> Q4{Is this a transposed convolution or generator?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> FP[Use Full or carefully designed asymmetric padding]

    VP --> END[Layer Configured]
    SP --> END
    RP --> END
    CP --> END
    FP --> END

    style START fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B
    style VP fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
    style SP fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style RP fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E
    style CP fill:#F0F4C3,stroke:#827717
    style FP fill:#FFECB3,stroke:#FF6F00
    style END fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1.** *Define padding in the context of Convolutional Neural Networks. List any two types of padding used in CNNs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Padding is the technique of adding extra pixel values (commonly zeros) around the boundary of an input feature map before performing a convolution operation. It is primarily used to **(1)** preserve the spatial dimensions of the input through the convolution, and **(2)** ensure that the kernel processes edge pixels of the input rather than ignoring them.

Two common types of padding are:
1. **Valid Padding (No Padding):** No pixels are added; output size shrinks.
2. **Same Padding (Zero Padding):** Pixels are added so that the output size equals the input size; for kernel $k$ and stride 1, $p = (k-1)/2$.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q2.** *For a convolutional layer with input size $32 \times 32$, kernel size $5 \times 5$, and stride $1$, calculate the padding required to keep the output size equal to the input size.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

To keep the output size equal to the input size, we use the "same" padding formula:

$$
p = \frac{k - 1}{2} = \frac{5 - 1}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2
$$

Therefore, the padding required is **$p = 2$** on each side (top, bottom, left, right). This expands the input to $36 \times 36$, and convolving with a $5 \times 5$ kernel with stride 1 gives an output of $32 \times 32$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *Explain the concept of padding in CNNs. Discuss the difference between "valid" and "same" padding with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For a CNN layer with input feature map of size $64 \times 64 \times 16$, kernel size $3 \times 3$, stride $2$, and padding $1$, compute the output feature map dimensions and the number of trainable parameters in the layer (assuming 32 filters). (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Padding (1 mark):**
Padding is the addition of extra values (usually zeros) around the input feature map border before convolution. It serves to control the output spatial dimensions and to allow kernels to operate on edge pixels of the input.

**Valid Padding (2 marks):**
In valid padding, $p = 0$. No extra pixels are added. The output dimension is:

$$
O = H - k + 1
$$

For input $H = 32$ and kernel $k = 5$: $O = 32 - 5 + 1 = 28$. The output shrinks, and edge pixels of the input are visited fewer times by the kernel.

**Same Padding (2 marks):**
In same padding, padding is chosen so that $O = H$. For odd $k$ and stride 1, $p = (k-1)/2$. For input $H = 32$, $k = 5$, $p = 2$:

$$
O = 32 + 2(2) - 5 + 1 = 32
$$

The output dimensions equal the input dimensions, making it suitable for building very deep networks.

**Diagram (2 marks):**
*Refer to the schematic in Section 4.2 — the same-padded 7×7 tensor over a 5×5 input.* A textual diagram should show:
- 5×5 input grid
- Surrounded by a 1-cell-thick border of zeros (for $k=3$)
- Resulting in 7×7 padded tensor
- 3×3 kernel sliding over the padded tensor
- Producing 5×5 output

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Input $H = W = 64$, $C_{in} = 16$
- Kernel $k = 3$, stride $s = 2$, padding $p = 1$
- Number of filters $C_{out} = 32$

**Step 1 — Compute output height (2 marks):**

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{64 + 2(1) - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{64 + 2 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{63}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \lfloor 31.5 \rfloor + 1 = 31 + 1 = 32
$$

**Step 2 — Compute output width (1 mark):** By symmetry, $W_{out} = 32$.

**Step 3 — Output volume (1 mark):** $32 \times 32 \times 32$.

**Step 4 — Number of trainable parameters (3 marks):**

Each filter has $k \cdot k \cdot C_{in} + 1$ parameters (the +1 is for the bias term).

Parameters per filter:

$$
(3 \times 3 \times 16) + 1 = 144 + 1 = 145
$$

Total parameters:

$$
145 \times 32 = 4640
$$

**Final Answer:** Output feature map is $32 \times 32 \times 32$, with **4640 trainable parameters**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** *Derive the general formula for the output dimension of a convolutional layer with input size $H$, kernel size $k$, stride $s$, and padding $p$. Show how the formula simplifies for the special case of "same" padding with stride 1 and odd kernel size. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Compare and contrast zero padding, reflective padding, and replicate padding. For which type of CNN application would each be most suitable, and why? (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**General Formula Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider an input of height $H$ padded by $p$ on top and bottom. The padded input has height:

$$
H_p = H + 2p
$$

A kernel of size $k$ is applied with stride $s$. The number of times the kernel fits vertically into the padded input is the number of distinct starting positions for the top of the kernel:

$$
\text{Number of positions} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

Substituting $H_p$:

$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Special Case: Same Padding (2 marks):**

For "same" padding we want $O = H$, with $s = 1$ and odd $k$:

$$
H = \left\lfloor \frac{H + 2p - k}{1} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

Ignoring the floor (assuming divisibility):

$$
H = H + 2p - k + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2p = k - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \frac{k-1}{2}
$$

For example, with $k = 3$, $p = 1$; with $k = 5$, $p = 2$; with $k = 7$, $p = 3$.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Zero Padding | Reflective Padding | Replicate Padding |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fill Values | Constant 0 | Mirror of edge | Repeated edge value |
| Introduces Artificial Values | Yes (zeros) | No | No |
| Computational Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Low |
| Boundary Discontinuity | High (zero edge) | None | None (constant edge) |
| Information Loss | None (added info is zero) | None | None |

**Suitability Analysis (4 marks):**

1. **Zero Padding** is the most computationally efficient and is the **default** choice in standard classification CNNs such as VGG, ResNet, and AlexNet. It is suitable when the network is deep enough that boundary artifacts are smoothed out by subsequent layers. However, it can introduce a slight bias towards lower activation magnitudes at the image edges, which may be problematic in tasks like segmentation where boundary accuracy matters.

2. **Reflective Padding** is preferred in **image segmentation** (e.g., U-Net) and **image generation** (GANs, diffusion models). Since it mirrors pixel values, it does not introduce artificial zero regions, leading to cleaner feature maps near boundaries. It is also used in **image inpainting** tasks where the boundary must blend naturally with the interior.

3. **Replicate Padding** is most suitable in **medical imaging** and **satellite imagery analysis**, where the boundary of the image often represents an *actual physical edge* (e.g., organ boundary, terrain edge) and extending this edge outward is a more natural assumption than zeroing it out. It is also used in **deep image prior** and **super-resolution** networks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Padding Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the floor function:** Students often write $O = (H + 2p - k)/s + 1$ without the floor brackets, leading to non-integer outputs in cases where the numerator is not exactly divisible by the stride. Always wrap with $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$.
> 2. **Confusing the +1 term:** The "+1" comes from the fact that the first kernel position is at index 0 (or 1 in 1-indexed math), not from the padding. Removing the +1 gives wrong answers.
> 3. **Asymmetric padding for even kernels:** When $k$ is even (e.g., $k=4$), the formula $p = (k-1)/2$ gives a non-integer. The correct approach is to use asymmetric padding: $p_{\text{top}} = \lfloor k/2 \rfloor = 2$, $p_{\text{bottom}} = k - 1 - p_{\text{top}} = 1$. This is a very common KTU trap question.
> 4. **Confusing padding with stride:** Some students mix up the roles. Padding is a *boundary expansion*; stride is a *step size* of the kernel. They are independent operations and appear as separate terms in the output formula.
> 5. **Forgetting bias in parameter count:** When asked about parameters, always add +1 per filter for the bias term.
> 6. **Zero-padding in test-time vs. training-time:** In some networks (e.g., Fully Convolutional Networks), padding behavior can differ between training (with random crops) and inference (full image). Mention this if the question is about deployment.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Padding (KTU PEAET865, Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Padding adds values around the input border before convolution; primary purpose is **dimension preservation** and **edge pixel processing**.
- **Output Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** $O = \lfloor (H + 2p - k)/s \rfloor + 1$.
- **"Same" Padding Condition:** $p = (k-1)/2$ works only for **odd** $k$ and **stride 1**. For even $k$, use **asymmetric** padding.
- **"Valid" Padding:** $p = 0$ → output shrinks by $k - 1$ in each spatial dimension.
- **"Full" Padding:** $p = k - 1$ → output expands.
- **Zero Padding** is the **default** in most modern CNN frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch, Keras).
- **Reflective and Replicate padding** are used in segmentation, generation, and medical imaging to avoid zero-boundary artifacts.
- **Causal Padding** is critical for **autoregressive** 1D models (WaveNet, time-series forecasting).
- **Padding adds NO learnable parameters** — it is a purely structural / data-level operation.
- **Padding does NOT change the receptive field size** — it only changes *which* input pixels contribute to the output.
- **Number of trainable parameters per layer:** $(k \cdot k \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out}$ — independent of $p$.
- **For even kernel size:** use asymmetric padding $(p_{\text{top}}, p_{\text{bottom}}) = (\lfloor k/2 \rfloor, \lceil k/2 \rceil)$ to avoid checkerboard artifacts (a KTU-favorite trick question).
- **General formula with dilation:** $O = \lfloor (H + 2p - d(k-1) - 1)/s \rfloor + 1$.
- **Transposed convolution padding formula:** $p_{\text{out}} = k - p_{\text{in}} - 1$ — used in encoder–decoder architectures.
- **KTU 14-mark standard structure:** Definition (2) → Types & Diagram (3) → Formula derivation (5) → Numerical problem (4). Always answer in this structure for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
