topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET865 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pooling Layers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pooling Layer (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **Pooling Layer** is a downsampling operation in a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) that progressively reduces the spatial dimensions (height and width) of its input feature maps while preserving the depth (number of channels). It operates independently on every depth slice of the input and replaces each non-overlapping (or overlapping) sub-region with a single statistical summary value.

Mathematically, given an input feature map of size $W_{in} \times H_{in} \times D$, a pooling layer with a spatial filter of size $F \times F$ and stride $S$ produces an output feature map of size $W_{out} \times H_{out} \times D$, where the depth $D$ remains unchanged.

$$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} - F}{S} \right\rfloor + 1, \quad H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} - F}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Pooling layers contain **zero learnable parameters** (no weights or biases). They perform a fixed, deterministic mathematical aggregation, which makes them computationally cheap and a primary tool for **dimensionality reduction** in deep networks.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are looking at a high-resolution satellite image of a football stadium, but your phone screen is too small to display every pixel. What do you do?

- You **shrink** the image by grouping every $2 \times 2$ block of pixels into **one representative pixel**.
- If you pick the **brightest** pixel in each $2 \times 2$ block, you are performing **Max Pooling** (keeping the strongest signal).
- If you take the **average color** of each $2 \times 2$ block, you are performing **Average Pooling** (keeping the smooth average).

**In a CNN, pooling does exactly this on the "image" produced by a previous convolutional layer.** It asks: *"Out of all the activations in this small neighborhood, what is the most important summary?"* The network keeps the answer and throws away the rest. This makes the network:
1. **Faster** (less data to process in deeper layers).
2. **Smarter** (forces the network to care about *whether* a feature is present, not *exactly where* it is — this is called **translation invariance**).
3. **Robust** (small shifts in the input do not drastically change the output).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hyperparameters of a Pooling Layer (Bold = Standard Defaults in KTU Lab/Exams):**
> - **Filter size (F) = 2**
> - **Stride (S) = 2**
> - **Padding (P) = 0** (almost never used)
> - Most common architecture: **$2 \times 2$ filter with stride 2**, which divides both height and width by exactly **2** (a 75% reduction in spatial data).

---

## 1.4 The "Why" Behind Pooling — Three Engineering Reasons

| # | Engineering Reason | Plain English Explanation |
|---|--------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Dimensionality Reduction** | Shrinks large feature maps, saving memory and compute. |
| 2 | **Translation Invariance** | The network stops being obsessed with exact pixel positions. |
| 3 | **Larger Receptive Field** | Deeper neurons effectively "see" a bigger portion of the original image. |
| 4 | **Overfitting Control** | Fewer spatial activations mean fewer downstream parameters. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Pooling Operations

Pooling layers are classified based on the **aggregation function** applied within each spatial window. Below is the complete taxonomy as per the KTU 2024 Deep Learning syllabus.

### 2.1.1 Max Pooling
- Selects the **maximum** value from each pooling window.
- Most widely used in classification networks (e.g., AlexNet, VGG, ResNet).
- Preserves the **strongest activation** — i.e., the most prominent detected feature.

$$y_{i,j,d} = \max_{(p,q) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} x_{p,q,d}$$

where $\mathcal{R}_{i,j}$ is the pooling window centered at output coordinate $(i,j)$ and $d$ is the depth/channel index.

### 2.1.2 Average (Mean) Pooling
- Computes the **arithmetic mean** of all values in the pooling window.
- Used in networks like GoogLeNet, ResNet (final layer), and in many segmentation architectures.
- Preserves the **smoothed background information**.

$$y_{i,j,d} = \frac{1}{F^2} \sum_{(p,q) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} x_{p,q,d}$$

### 2.1.3 Min Pooling
- Selects the **minimum** value in each window.
- Rare in practice; used in certain signal-processing oriented networks and dark-feature detection.

### 2.1.4 Global Pooling
- A special case where the **pool size equals the input feature map size**.
- Replaces each entire feature map with a **single number**.
- Two variants: **Global Average Pooling (GAP)** and **Global Max Pooling (GMP)**.
- Used as a replacement for the fully connected layer in modern architectures (e.g., ResNet, MobileNet).

### 2.1.5 Lp Pooling (Generalized Pooling)
- A generalized form parametrized by a positive integer $p$:

$$y_{i,j,d} = \left( \frac{1}{F^2} \sum_{(p,q) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} x_{p,q,d}^{\,p} \right)^{1/p}$$

- When $p = 1$: Average Pooling.
- When $p \to \infty$: Max Pooling.

### 2.1.6 Stochastic Pooling
- Probabilistically selects an activation based on its relative magnitude within the window.
- Probability of picking activation $x_k$ is:

$$P(x_k) = \frac{x_k}{\sum_{(p,q) \in \mathcal{R}} x_{p,q}}$$

- Reduces overfitting but adds randomness — less common in standard pipelines.

### 2.1.7 Spatial Pyramid Pooling (SPP)
- Applies pooling at **multiple scales** and concatenates the results.
- Allows the network to accept **variable-size inputs** (used in SPP-Net, R-CNN family).

### 2.1.8 Adaptive Pooling
- The user specifies the **desired output size**, and the layer automatically computes the kernel size and stride.
- Available in PyTorch as `nn.AdaptiveMaxPool2d`, `nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d`.
- Useful for connecting convolutional backbones to fully connected heads.

---

## 2.2 Operational Walkthrough — Step-by-Step Logic

A standard $2 \times 2$ max-pooling operation with stride 2 on a $4 \times 4$ feature map proceeds as follows:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four-Step Pipeline of Any Pooling Operation:**
> 1. **Sliding Window:** Place an $F \times F$ window on the input feature map.
> 2. **Stride Jump:** Move the window by $S$ pixels (horizontally then vertically).
> 3. **Aggregation:** Apply the chosen function (max / average / min) to all values inside the window.
> 4. **Write to Output:** Store the aggregated value in the corresponding location of the output map.
> Repeat until the entire input is covered.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Must Memorize for ESE**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Output Width | $W_{out} = \left\lfloor (W_{in} - F) / S \right\rfloor + 1$ | Pixels |
| 2 | Output Height | $H_{out} = \left\lfloor (H_{in} - F) / S \right\rfloor + 1$ | Pixels |
| 3 | Output Depth | $D_{out} = D_{in}$ (unchanged) | Channels |
| 4 | Learnable Parameters | **0** | None |
| 5 | Hyperparameters | $F$ (filter), $S$ (stride), $P$ (padding, optional) | Integers $\geq 1$ |
| 6 | Max Pooling | $y = \max(x)$ in window | Activation-preserving |
| 7 | Average Pooling | $y = \frac{1}{F^2}\sum x$ | Smoothed |
| 8 | Lp Pooling | $y = \left( \frac{1}{F^2}\sum x^p \right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized form |
| 9 | Stochastic Pooling Prob. | $P(x_k) = x_k / \sum x_j$ | Normalized activations |
| 10 | Standard $2\times2$, $S=2$ | Output is exactly $W_{in}/2 \times H_{in}/2$ | 75% size reduction |

> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When $W_{in} - F$ is **not divisible by $S$**, the floor function $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ is applied, and the rightmost / bottommost columns may be **discarded** (this is the "VALID" pooling convention, the default in most frameworks).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pooling is not just an academic exercise. It powers:
- **Medical Imaging:** Tumor detection in MRI/CT scans (VGG, ResNet backbones).
- **Autonomous Driving:** Real-time object detection in Tesla / Waymo perception stacks.
- **Smartphones:** Face unlock, photo classification — all use GAP in MobileNet/EfficientNet.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Defect detection on assembly lines using transfer-learned CNNs.
- **Satellite Imagery:** Land-use classification using dilated CNNs with global pooling heads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Max Pooling and Average Pooling

**Given:** An input feature map of size $4 \times 4$ (a single depth slice, $D=1$).
**Task:** Apply $2 \times 2$ pooling with stride $S = 2$, padding $P = 0$.
**Compute:** Both the max-pooled and average-pooled outputs.

### Input Feature Map

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 2 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 & 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 7 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 & 8 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### Step 1 — Output Dimension Calculation

Apply the formula $W_{out} = \lfloor (W_{in} - F) / S \rfloor + 1$:

$$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{4 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 1 \right\rfloor + 1 = 2$$

$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{4 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 2$$

So the output is a **$2 \times 2$ matrix**.

---

### Step 2 — Identify the Four Non-Overlapping Windows

Since $F=2$ and $S=2$, the windows do not overlap. They partition the $4 \times 4$ input into four disjoint $2 \times 2$ blocks.

- **Window (0,0):** Rows 0–1, Cols 0–1 → $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$
- **Window (0,1):** Rows 0–1, Cols 2–3 → $\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$
- **Window (1,0):** Rows 2–3, Cols 0–1 → $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 7 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$
- **Window (1,1):** Rows 2–3, Cols 2–3 → $\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 8 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$

---

### Step 3 — Apply Max Pooling

For each window, select the maximum element:

- Window (0,0): $\max(1, 3, 5, 6) = 6$
- Window (0,1): $\max(2, 4, 1, 2) = 4$
- Window (1,0): $\max(0, 7, 2, 4) = 7$
- Window (1,1): $\max(3, 1, 8, 5) = 8$

$$Y_{max} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 4 \\ 7 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### Step 4 — Apply Average Pooling

For each window, compute the arithmetic mean $y = \frac{1}{4}\sum x$:

- Window (0,0): $\frac{1+3+5+6}{4} = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75$
- Window (0,1): $\frac{2+4+1+2}{4} = \frac{9}{4} = 2.25$
- Window (1,0): $\frac{0+7+2+4}{4} = \frac{13}{4} = 3.25$
- Window (1,1): $\frac{3+1+8+5}{4} = \frac{17}{4} = 4.25$

$$Y_{avg} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.75 & 2.25 \\ 3.25 & 4.25 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### Step 5 — Backward Pass (Gradient Flow) for Max Pooling

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU Theory Exam:** A common question asks how gradients flow backward through a pooling layer.

For **max pooling**, the gradient is routed **only to the position that had the maximum value** in the forward pass. All other positions receive a gradient of zero.

Using a switch mask $M$ where $M_{p,q} = 1$ if $x_{p,q}$ was the max, else $0$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_{p,q}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y_{i,j}} \cdot M_{p,q}$$

For the four windows in our example, the switch masks are:

- Window (0,0): $M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ (since $x_{1,1}=6$ was max)
- Window (0,1): $M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (since $x_{0,3}=4$ was max)
- Window (1,0): $M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (since $x_{2,1}=7$ was max)
- Window (1,1): $M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (since $x_{3,2}=8$ was max)

For **average pooling**, the gradient is **distributed equally** to all four positions in the window:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_{p,q}} = \frac{1}{F^2} \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial y_{i,j}}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Pooling Variants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Matrix (High-Yield for ESE 14-mark questions):**

| Pooling Type | Information Preserved | Best Use Case | Computational Cost | Drawback |
|--------------|----------------------|----------------|---------------------|----------|
| **Max Pooling** | Strongest feature | Classification, edge/texture detection | Very Low | Throws away weak signals |
| **Average Pooling** | Smoothed aggregate | Segmentation, GANs (e.g., PatchGAN) | Very Low | May dilute the dominant feature |
| **Min Pooling** | Weakest signal | Dark-region detection, anomaly detection | Low | Rarely useful in vision |
| **Global Avg. Pooling** | Whole-map average | Replacing FC layers (ResNet, MobileNet) | Low | Loses spatial info entirely |
| **Lp Pooling** | Tunable via $p$ | Research, flexible aggregation | Moderate | Extra hyperparameter |
| **Stochastic Pooling** | Probabilistic | Regularization, dropout-like effect | High | Non-deterministic, hard to reproduce |
| **SPP** | Multi-scale features | Variable input size, detection | High | Memory intensive |
| **Adaptive Pooling** | Fixed output size | Bridging CNNs to FC heads | Low | Less control over receptive field |

---

## 3.3 Dimensionality Reduction Quantification

For a CNN with $L$ pooling layers, each halving the spatial dimensions, the **total data reduction** in the feature maps is:

$$\text{Compression Ratio} = \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{2L} = \frac{1}{4^{L}}$$

For example:
- $L = 1$: 75% reduction (quartering the data).
- $L = 2$: 93.75% reduction.
- $L = 3$: 98.44% reduction.
- $L = 5$ (VGG-16 depth): 99.998% reduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting that pooling affects **height and width simultaneously**, so the total compression is **quadratic** in the stride, not linear. A $2\times2$ pool with $S=2$ reduces the **number of activations by 4×**, not 2×.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — CNN with Pooling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image 224x224x3] --> B[Conv2D + ReLU 224x224x64]
    B --> C[MaxPool 2x2 stride 2 112x112x64]
    C --> D[Conv2D + ReLU 112x112x128]
    D --> E[MaxPool 2x2 stride 2 56x56x128]
    E --> F[Conv2D + ReLU 56x56x256]
    F --> G[MaxPool 2x2 stride 2 28x28x256]
    G --> H[Conv2D + ReLU 28x28x512]
    H --> I[MaxPool 2x2 stride 2 14x14x512]
    I --> J[Flatten / GlobalAvgPool 1x1x512]
    J --> K[Dense 4096]
    K --> L[Dense 1000 Softmax]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each blue-ish pool node halves the spatial dimensions (H, W) while preserving depth. Notice how the spatial volume shrinks from $224 \times 224$ down to $14 \times 14$, then a Global Pool collapses it to $1 \times 1 \times 512$ before the dense classifier.

---

## 4.2 Operational Block Diagram — Inside a Max Pooling Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Feature Map 4x4] --> B[Sliding Window 2x2]
    B --> C{Stride = 2}
    C --> D[Extract Window 1: Top-Left]
    C --> E[Extract Window 2: Top-Right]
    C --> F[Extract Window 3: Bottom-Left]
    C --> G[Extract Window 4: Bottom-Right]
    D --> H[Max Operation]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Output Feature Map 2x2]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparison Flowchart — Choosing the Right Pooling Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need a Pooling Layer?] --> Q1{Variable input size?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> SPP[Use Spatial Pyramid Pooling]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Need to preserve strongest feature?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Replace fully connected layer?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> GMP[Global Max Pooling]
    Q3 -- No --> MaxP[Max Pooling 2x2, S=2]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Need smooth aggregation?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Replace fully connected layer?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> GAP[Global Average Pooling]
    Q5 -- No --> AvgP[Average Pooling 2x2, S=2]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6{Probabilistic regularization needed?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> StochP[Stochastic Pooling]
    Q6 -- No --> LP[Lp Pooling with tuned p]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Role of Pooling in a CNN Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StageA[Stage 1: Feature Extraction]
        direction TB
        I1[Input Image] --> C1[Conv Layer]
        C1 --> A1[Activation ReLU]
        A1 --> P1[Pooling Layer]
    end
    subgraph StageB[Stage 2: Spatial Reduction]
        direction TB
        P1 --> C2[Conv Layer]
        C2 --> A2[Activation ReLU]
        A2 --> P2[Pooling Layer]
    end
    subgraph StageC[Stage 3: Classification Head]
        direction TB
        P2 --> Flat[Flatten / Global Pool]
        Flat --> FC[Dense Layer]
        FC --> Soft[Softmax Output]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Strategy for KTU Exams:** In a typical CNN block diagram, pooling layers are the **nodes labeled "Pool"** placed *between* convolution blocks. They are drawn as **slightly smaller rectangles** to visually represent the spatial shrinking. Examiners often give a CNN architecture diagram and ask students to compute the size of the feature map after each pool — practice this skill.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define a *Pooling Layer* in a CNN. List any **two** types of pooling operations.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A pooling layer is a downsampling operation in a CNN that reduces the spatial dimensions (height and width) of feature maps while preserving depth. It operates on each depth slice independently using a fixed-size window and a deterministic aggregation function. **No learnable parameters** exist in a pooling layer.
Two common types are:
1. **Max Pooling** — selects the maximum value in each window.
2. **Average Pooling** — computes the mean of all values in each window.

*(Marks distribution: 1 for definition, 1 for "no parameters" property, 1 for naming the two types.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2.** State **three** advantages of using pooling layers in a CNN.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Dimensionality reduction:** Decreases the number of activations, lowering memory and computation in deeper layers. (1 mark)
2. **Translation invariance:** Makes the network robust to small shifts and distortions in the input. (1 mark)
3. **Larger receptive field:** Each neuron in deeper layers effectively "sees" a larger portion of the original input image. (1 mark)

*(An additional valid point: helps control overfitting.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | Internal Choice Question]**

### ✦ OR — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q (a).** With a neat diagram, explain the **Max Pooling** operation in a CNN. Compute the output of a $2 \times 2$ max-pooling layer (stride 2) applied to the following $4 \times 4$ feature map. **(7 marks)**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 & 1 & 3 \\ 5 & 1 & 7 & 2 \\ 0 & 6 & 4 & 8 \\ 3 & 2 & 5 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Model Solution:**

Max pooling slides a $2 \times 2$ window over the input with stride 2, partitioning the input into four non-overlapping blocks, and selects the **maximum** value from each block.

- Block (top-left): $\max(2, 4, 5, 1) = 5$
- Block (top-right): $\max(1, 3, 7, 2) = 7$
- Block (bottom-left): $\max(0, 6, 3, 2) = 6$
- Block (bottom-right): $\max(4, 8, 5, 1) = 8$

**Output:**
$$Y_{max} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 7 \\ 6 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Diagram Description (to be drawn in exam):**
Draw a $4 \times 4$ grid, partition it into four $2 \times 2$ sub-grids, and label the maximum value picked from each.

---

**Q (b).** Differentiate between **Max Pooling** and **Average Pooling**. Discuss the **gradient flow** through both during backpropagation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Max Pooling | Average Pooling |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| Aggregation | $\max$ over window | $\frac{1}{F^2}\sum$ over window |
| Information kept | Strongest activation | Smoothed average |
| Typical use | Classification (VGG, ResNet middle) | Segmentation, GANs, final layer |
| Forward output | Sparse, sharp | Dense, smooth |

**Gradient Flow:**

For **Max Pooling**, the gradient is routed **only to the position that held the maximum** in the forward pass. A switch mask $M$ (1 at max position, 0 elsewhere) is used:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_{p,q}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y_{i,j}} \cdot M_{p,q}$$

For **Average Pooling**, the upstream gradient is **distributed equally** to all $F^2$ positions in the window:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_{p,q}} = \frac{1}{F^2} \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial y_{i,j}}$$

Hence, max pooling creates a *selective* gradient path, while average pooling creates a *uniform* one.

---

### ✦ OR — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q (a).** Explain **Global Average Pooling (GAP)**. How does it differ from a **Fully Connected (FC) layer**, and why do modern architectures like ResNet and MobileNet prefer it? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Global Average Pooling (GAP):**
GAP is a pooling operation where the **pool size equals the entire feature map size**. For an input of size $H \times W \times D$, GAP produces an output of size $1 \times 1 \times D$ by averaging each feature map:

$$y_d = \frac{1}{H \cdot W} \sum_{i=1}^{H} \sum_{j=1}^{W} x_{i,j,d}$$

**Comparison with FC Layer:**

| Property | Fully Connected Layer | Global Average Pooling |
|----------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Parameters | $H \cdot W \cdot D \times N$ (very large) | **0** |
| Spatial info | Thrown away after flattening | Aggregated, summarization preserved |
| Overfitting risk | High (millions of parameters) | Low |
| Input size constraint | Must be fixed | Can accept variable input size |

**Why modern architectures prefer GAP:**
1. **No parameters → less overfitting**, especially on small datasets.
2. **Native translation invariance** built in.
3. **Native handling of variable input sizes** (FC requires fixed flatten size).
4. **Empirically equivalent accuracy** with much lower model size — proven in the original ResNet and MobileNet papers.

---

**Q (b).** For an input feature map of size $32 \times 32 \times 16$, compute the output dimensions after applying:
- (i) Max Pooling with $F=2$, $S=2$
- (ii) Average Pooling with $F=3$, $S=2$
- (iii) Global Average Pooling

Show all calculations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Max Pooling, $F=2$, $S=2$:**

$$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 2}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 15 \right\rfloor + 1 = 16$$

$$H_{out} = 16, \quad D_{out} = 16$$

**Output = $16 \times 16 \times 16$** (2 marks)

**(ii) Average Pooling, $F=3$, $S=2$:**

$$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 14.5 \right\rfloor + 1 = 14 + 1 = 15$$

$$H_{out} = 15, \quad D_{out} = 16$$

**Output = $15 \times 15 \times 16$** (2 marks)

**(iii) Global Average Pooling:**

Pool size = entire feature map = $32 \times 32$. Stride is implicit (no overlap).

$$W_{out} = 1, \quad H_{out} = 1, \quad D_{out} = 16$$

**Output = $1 \times 1 \times 16$** (3 marks — must explicitly state the depth is preserved)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Read Before Exam Day):**
> 1. **Confusing pooling with convolution:** Pooling has **0 parameters**; convolution has $F \times F \times D_{in} \times D_{out}$ weights. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the floor function $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$:** When $(W_{in} - F)$ is not divisible by $S$, students often write a non-integer or decimal output dimension. Always apply $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$.
> 3. **Depth confusion:** Some students believe pooling changes the depth (number of channels). It does not. $D_{out} = D_{in}$ — always.
> 4. **Forgetting to back-substitute:** When asked for output size, students often write the formula but forget to evaluate the final numerical answer. KTU examiners award marks only for the **final boxed answer**.
> 5. **Mixing up Max vs. Average gradient flow:** A common 7-mark question. Memorize that **max pooling** routes gradient to **one position**, while **average pooling** distributes it **uniformly** across the window.
> 6. **Stride confusion with filter size:** Pooling with $F=3$ and $S=3$ is overlapping; with $F=3$ and $S=2$ it is non-overlapping. State which one the question is about.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before ESE):**
> - [ ] Pooling = **downsampling**, **no learnable parameters**, depth preserved.
> - [ ] Standard config: **$2 \times 2$ filter, stride 2, no padding** → output is $W/2 \times H/2$.
> - [ ] Output dimension formula: $\lfloor (W_{in} - F)/S \rfloor + 1$.
> - [ ] **Max Pooling** = keep the brightest signal (most prominent feature).
> - [ ] **Average Pooling** = keep the smoothed average (preserves background).
> - [ ] **Global Average Pooling** = collapses $H \times W \times D$ to $1 \times 1 \times D$; used in place of FC layers in modern CNNs.
> - [ ] **Lp Pooling** generalizes to $p = 1$ (average) and $p \to \infty$ (max).
> - [ ] **Stochastic Pooling** uses probabilities proportional to activation magnitudes.
> - [ ] **Gradient flow:** Max → switch mask (one-hot), Average → uniform distribution ($1/F^2$ each).
> - [ ] **Three engineering reasons:** dimensionality reduction, translation invariance, larger receptive field.
> - [ ] **Spatial Pyramid Pooling** allows variable input sizes via multi-scale pooling.
> - [ ] **Adaptive Pooling** auto-computes kernel and stride to hit a target output size.
> - [ ] **Compression ratio** after $L$ halving pools: $1/4^L$. For $L=5$ (VGG-16), the spatial data is reduced by **99.998%**.
> - [ ] Pooling is **channel-wise independent** — no cross-channel information mixing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fully Connected Layers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET865 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fully Connected Layers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fully Connected (FC) Layer (also known as Dense Layer):** A neural network layer in which **every neuron** in the current layer is connected to **every neuron** in the subsequent layer via a unique learnable weight parameter. Mathematically, it implements a linear affine transformation followed by a non-linear activation function.

Formally, given an input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, a Fully Connected Layer with $m$ output neurons computes:

$$\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})$$

where:
- $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **weight matrix**
- $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the **bias vector**
- $f(\cdot)$ is the element-wise **activation function**
- $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the output vector

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a class of 100 students taking a final exam composed of 10 questions. A *fully connected* assessment is one where **every student is graded on every question** — no student is skipped for any question, and no question is hidden from any student. The teacher's "weight" (importance) given to each student-question pair is unique and learned over time.

Another intuitive view: think of a **company voting board**. Every board member (neuron in layer $L$) casts a weighted opinion on every agenda item (neuron in layer $L+1$). The final decision for each agenda item is the sum of all members' weighted opinions, plus a personal bias, then filtered through a decision threshold (activation function).

## 1.3 Position in a Deep Network

Fully Connected Layers are typically placed:
1. At the **classifier head** of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs).
2. As the **core building block** of Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs).
3. At the **final regression/classification layer** of nearly all deep architectures.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 KTU scheme explicitly identifies FC layers as the *universal approximator building block* — a single hidden layer with sufficient neurons can approximate **any** continuous function on a compact domain (Universal Approximation Theorem).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown

A Fully Connected Layer performs **four sequential operations**:

1. **Linear Transformation (Weighted Sum):** Each input $x_i$ is multiplied by a corresponding weight $w_{ji}$, where $j$ indexes the output neuron and $i$ indexes the input.
2. **Bias Addition:** A learnable bias $b_j$ is added to the pre-activation of each output neuron — this allows the activation function to shift left/right.
3. **Summation (Pre-activation):** All weighted inputs plus the bias are summed to form the pre-activation $z_j$.
4. **Non-linear Activation:** The pre-activation is passed through $f(\cdot)$ to produce the final output $a_j$.

### 2.1.1 Why the Bias Term Matters

Without a bias, the hyperplane of decision must pass through the origin — a serious restriction. The bias gives the model **translational自由度 (translational freedom)**, allowing the separating hyperplane to be located anywhere in the input space.

### 2.1.2 Why Non-linearity is Mandatory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If we stacked only linear FC layers, the entire network would collapse into a single linear transformation (composition of linear maps is linear). Activation functions like **ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh** inject the non-linearity that allows deep networks to model complex, curved decision boundaries.

## 2.2 Vectorized Form (Batch Processing)

For a mini-batch of $B$ samples stacked as matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times n}$:

$$\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^{T} + \mathbf{b}$$

$$\mathbf{A} = f(\mathbf{Z})$$

where broadcasting adds the bias $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times m}$ to every row of the pre-activation matrix.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Shape / Dimensions | Purpose |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|---------|
| 1 | Forward pass (single sample) | $\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})$ | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}, \mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}, \mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ | Output computation |
| 2 | Forward pass (batch) | $\mathbf{A} = f(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^{T} + \mathbf{b})$ | $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times n}, \mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ | Parallel training |
| 3 | Pre-activation | $z_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ji} x_i + b_j$ | Scalar per neuron | Sum before activation |
| 4 | Activation output | $a_j = f(z_j)$ | Scalar per neuron | Non-linear mapping |
| 5 | Total parameters | $P = (n \times m) + m$ | $n$ = input, $m$ = output | Capacity measure |
| 6 | Gradient w.r.t. weights | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \frac{1}{B} \mathbf{X}^{T} \delta$ | $\mathbf{X}^{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times B}, \delta \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times m}$ | Weight update |
| 7 | Gradient w.r.t. bias | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \delta_i$ | Vector of length $m$ | Bias update |
| 8 | Gradient w.r.t. input | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{X}} = \delta \mathbf{W}$ | Shape $(B \times n)$ | Backprop to previous layer |
| 9 | Output error signal | $\delta = (\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{Y}) \odot f'(Z)$ | Shape $(B \times m)$ | Chain rule application |
| 10 | ReLU activation | $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Scalar | Hidden layer default |
| 11 | Sigmoid activation | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Scalar | Output (binary) |
| 12 | Softmax activation | $p_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | Probabilities | Output (multi-class) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Notice:** In the table above, all vertical separators are markdown table pipes — not mathematical absolute-value bars. For absolute value, use $\lvert x \rvert$ in your own notes.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fully Connected Layers are deployed in:
- **Tabular data regression/classification** (loan default, house price prediction)
- **Feature aggregation in CNNs** (final layers of ResNet, VGG, AlexNet)
- **Transformer feed-forward blocks** (the "position-wise" FFN sublayer)
- **Reinforcement learning value heads** (Deep Q-Networks)
- **Generative models** (the latter half of VAE decoders, GAN output layers)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Parameter Count in a Fully Connected Layer

**Given:** An FC layer receiving $n$ inputs and producing $m$ outputs.

**Step 1:** Count the weight connections.

Every one of the $n$ input neurons connects to every one of the $m$ output neurons. This is a complete bipartite connection.

$$N_{\text{weights}} = n \times m$$

*Textual Logic:* Think of an $n \times m$ grid where each cell represents one weight — there are exactly $n$ rows and $m$ columns, giving $n \cdot m$ cells.

**Step 2:** Count the bias terms.

Each output neuron has exactly one bias term added before the activation.

$$N_{\text{bias}} = m$$

*Textual Logic:* One bias per output neuron — hence $m$ total.

**Step 3:** Total learnable parameters.

$$P_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{weights}} + N_{\text{bias}} = (n \times m) + m$$

**Step 4 (Worked Example):** A layer with $n = 1024$ inputs and $m = 512$ outputs.

$$P = (1024 \times 512) + 512 = 524{,}288 + 512 = 524{,}800 \text{ parameters}$$

*Textual Logic:* This is why FC layers are *parameter-heavy* — they account for the bulk of memory in many architectures.

## 3.2 Derivation: Forward Pass for a Single Neuron

Consider output neuron $j$ receiving inputs $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ with weights $w_{j1}, w_{j2}, \ldots, w_{jn}$ and bias $b_j$.

**Step 1:** Weighted sum of all inputs.

$$z_j = w_{j1} x_1 + w_{j2} x_2 + \cdots + w_{jn} x_n$$

*Textual Logic:* Each input contributes proportionally to its associated weight.

**Step 2:** Add the bias term.

$$z_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ji} x_i + b_j$$

*Textual Logic:* The bias acts as an offset, independent of the input magnitude.

**Step 3:** Apply the activation function.

$$a_j = f(z_j) = f\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ji} x_i + b_j\right)$$

*Textual Logic:* The activation squashes, thresholds, or transforms the linear combination into a non-linear output.

## 3.3 Derivation: Backpropagation Through an FC Layer

**Given:** Loss $L$ and the post-activation output $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times m}$ of the current FC layer; the layer receives input $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times n}$, has weights $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, and bias $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times m}$.

**Step 1:** Recall the forward equations.

$$\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^{T} + \mathbf{b}, \qquad \mathbf{A} = f(\mathbf{Z})$$

**Step 2:** Define the upstream gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{A}}$ (received from the next layer). The local error signal at the pre-activation is:

$$\delta = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{Z}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{A}} \odot f'(\mathbf{Z})$$

*Textual Logic:* Element-wise multiplication applies the derivative of the activation function to chain the gradient through $f$.

**Step 3:** Compute the gradient w.r.t. the bias.

Since $\mathbf{Z}$ depends on $\mathbf{b}$ as an additive constant broadcast across the batch:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \delta_i$$

*Textual Logic:* The bias contributes identically to every sample, so we sum the per-sample gradients and average over the batch.

**Step 4:** Compute the gradient w.r.t. the weights.

For a single sample, $z_j = \sum_k w_{jk} x_k + b_j$, hence $\frac{\partial z_j}{\partial w_{jk}} = x_k$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \frac{1}{B} \mathbf{X}^{T} \delta$$

*Textual Logic:* Each weight $w_{jk}$ is updated in proportion to the input $x_k$ that flowed through it and the resulting error $\delta_j$.

**Step 5:** Compute the gradient w.r.t. the input (to pass backward).

Since $z_j = \sum_k w_{jk} x_k + b_j$, we have $\frac{\partial z_j}{\partial x_k} = w_{jk}$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{X}} = \delta \mathbf{W}$$

*Textual Logic:* This is the upstream gradient for the previous layer in the network.

**Step 6 (Summary Block):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{A}} \odot f'(\mathbf{Z}) \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} &= \frac{1}{B} \mathbf{X}^{T} \delta \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}} &= \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \delta_i \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{X}} &= \delta \mathbf{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Universal Approximation Intuition

A single hidden-layer FC network with $H$ hidden neurons approximates any continuous function $g: [0,1]^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ as:

$$g(\mathbf{x}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{H} \alpha_i \, f(\mathbf{w}_i \cdot \mathbf{x} + b_i)$$

*Textual Logic:* Each hidden neuron carves out a localized "bump" in the input space; the output layer performs a weighted sum of these bumps — like a Fourier-style basis expansion.

> [!NOTE]
> The theorem guarantees *existence* of such a network but gives **no constructive algorithm** — finding the right weights is exactly what gradient descent solves.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | FC Layer | Convolutional Layer | Recurrent Layer |
|----------|----------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Connectivity | Full (all-to-all) | Local + weight sharing | Sequential + hidden state |
| Parameter count | $(n \times m) + m$ | $k_h \times k_w \times C_{in} \times C_{out}$ | $(n \times n) + n$ per gate |
| Spatial structure | Ignored | Preserved | Sequential |
| Typical role | Classifier / regressor | Feature extractor | Sequence modeler |
| Translation invariance | No | Yes (via weight sharing) | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Architecture: Multi-Layer FC Network

```mermaid
graph LR
    in1((x1))
    in2((x2))
    in3((x3))
    in4((x4))
    h1((h1))
    h2((h2))
    h3((h3))
    out1((y1))
    out2((y2))
    in1 --> h1
    in1 --> h2
    in1 --> h3
    in2 --> h1
    in2 --> h2
    in2 --> h3
    in3 --> h1
    in3 --> h2
    in3 --> h3
    in4 --> h1
    in4 --> h2
    in4 --> h3
    h1 --> out1
    h1 --> out2
    h2 --> out1
    h2 --> out2
    h3 --> out1
    h3 --> out2
```

*Interpretation:* A 3-layer fully connected network: 4 input neurons, 3 hidden neurons, 2 output neurons. Every input-to-hidden and hidden-to-output connection carries a unique weight.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Forward Pass Computational Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Vector X shape B by n] --> B[Matrix Multiply with W transpose]
    P[Parameter W shape m by n] --> B
    B --> C[Pre Activation Z]
    Q[Parameter b shape 1 by m] --> C
    C --> D[Apply Activation f elementwise]
    D --> E[Output A shape B by m]
    E --> F[Loss Computation vs Ground Truth]
    F --> G[Backprop Compute delta]
    G --> H[Compute Gradients dL dW and dL db]
    H --> I[Optimizer Step W and b]
    I --> B
```

*Interpretation:* Closed-loop block diagram of one FC layer showing the forward compute path (top) and the backward gradient path (bottom-right), with the optimizer arrow looping back to update the parameters used in the next forward pass.

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: FC Layer Within a Deep CNN Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Image HxWxC] --> B[Convolution Block 1]
    B --> C[Convolution Block 2]
    C --> D[Convolution Block 3]
    D --> E[Flatten Layer]
    E --> F[FC Layer 1 with ReLU]
    F --> G[FC Layer 2 with ReLU]
    G --> H[FC Output Layer with Softmax]
    H --> I[Class Probabilities]
```

*Interpretation:* Illustrates the canonical role of FC layers as the classifier head sitting atop convolutional feature extractors. The flatten layer converts the 3-D feature map into a 1-D vector compatible with FC input.

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic: Tensor Shape Tracking

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Tensor X shape B by n] --> S2[Multiply by W transpose shape n by m]
    S2 --> S3[Result Z shape B by m]
    S3 --> S4[Add bias b shape 1 by m broadcast]
    S4 --> S5[Activate f elementwise]
    S5 --> S6[Output A shape B by m]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Loss scalar]
    S7 --> S8[delta shape B by m]
    S8 --> S9[dL dW shape n by m via X transpose dot delta]
    S8 --> S10[dL dX shape B by n via delta dot W]
```

*Interpretation:* Shape-tracking diagram used during backpropagation derivation. The shapes must match exactly at every operation — this is a primary exam-validation technique.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Part A = 3 marks each (short answer); Part B = 14 marks each (choice-based, with sub-parts a=7 marks and b=7 marks). All questions below are aligned to the **Module 1** outcome and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L2–L4.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Fully Connected Layer. Write its mathematical formulation and state the role of the bias term.**

**Model Answer:**

A Fully Connected (FC) Layer is a neural network layer where each neuron in one layer is connected to every neuron in the adjacent layer, with each connection governed by a unique learnable weight.

$$\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})$$

- $\mathbf{W}$ = weight matrix
- $\mathbf{b}$ = bias vector
- $f(\cdot)$ = activation function

**Role of bias:** The bias term provides **translational freedom** to the affine transformation, allowing the hyperplane of decision to shift away from the origin. Without bias, the model is restricted to functions passing through the origin, severely limiting its representational power.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Calculate the total number of trainable parameters in an FC layer with 128 input neurons and 64 output neurons.**

**Model Answer:**

Number of weight parameters:

$$N_w = 128 \times 64 = 8{,}192$$

Number of bias parameters:

$$N_b = 64$$

Total trainable parameters:

$$P = N_w + N_b = 8{,}192 + 64 = \boxed{8{,}256 \text{ parameters}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the forward propagation of input through a Fully Connected Layer in a deep network. Derive the vectorized formulation for a mini-batch of $B$ samples and discuss the role of the activation function with at least two examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Single-Sample Forward Pass:**

For an input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and an FC layer with $m$ output neurons:

$$\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}, \qquad \mathbf{a} = f(\mathbf{z})$$

**Step 2 — Mini-Batch Vectorization:**

For $B$ samples stacked row-wise as $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times n}$:

$$\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^{T} + \mathbf{b}$$

$$\mathbf{A} = f(\mathbf{Z})$$

The bias $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times m}$ is broadcast across all $B$ rows.

**Step 3 — Role of the Activation Function:**

Activation injects non-linearity. Two key examples:

- **ReLU:** $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ — default for hidden layers; computationally cheap and mitigates vanishing gradient.
- **Softmax:** $p_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ — used at the output for multi-class classification, producing a valid probability distribution.

Without $f(\cdot)$, stacking FC layers collapses to a single linear map regardless of depth.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the backpropagation gradient expressions for the weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$, the bias vector $\mathbf{b}$, and the upstream input gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{X}}$ for an FC layer. Clearly state the chain-rule steps.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Local Error Signal:**

Given upstream $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{A}}$ and activation derivative $f'(\mathbf{Z})$:

$$\delta = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{Z}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{A}} \odot f'(\mathbf{Z})$$

**Step 2 — Gradient w.r.t. Bias (chain rule over additive broadcast):**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \delta_i$$

**Step 3 — Gradient w.r.t. Weights:**

Since $z_{ij} = \sum_k w_{jk} x_{ik}$, we have $\frac{\partial z_{ij}}{\partial w_{jk}} = x_{ik}$. Applying the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \frac{1}{B} \mathbf{X}^{T} \delta$$

**Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. Input (upstream message):**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{X}} = \delta \mathbf{W}$$

**Step 5 — Final Compiled Box:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{A}} \odot f'(\mathbf{Z}) \\[3pt]
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} &= \frac{1}{B} \mathbf{X}^{T} \delta \\[3pt]
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}} &= \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i=1}^{B} \delta_i \\[3pt]
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{X}} &= \delta \mathbf{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**For a 4-layer MLP with input dimension 5, two hidden layers of sizes 10 and 8, and an output layer of size 3, compute: (i) the total number of trainable parameters, (ii) the output shape for a batch of 16 samples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Network Architecture:**

- Layer 1 (Input → Hidden 1): $5 \to 10$
- Layer 2 (Hidden 1 → Hidden 2): $10 \to 8$
- Layer 3 (Hidden 2 → Output): $8 \to 3$

**Step 2 — Per-Layer Parameter Count:**

| Layer | Weights | Biases | Total |
|-------|---------|--------|-------|
| 1 | $5 \times 10 = 50$ | $10$ | $60$ |
| 2 | $10 \times 8 = 80$ | $8$ | $88$ |
| 3 | $8 \times 3 = 24$ | $3$ | $27$ |

**Step 3 — Sum the Totals:**

$$P_{\text{total}} = 60 + 88 + 27 = \boxed{175 \text{ parameters}}$$

**Step 4 — Output Shape for $B = 16$:**

The input to Layer 1 is $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{16 \times 5}$. After successive transformations:

$$\mathbf{A}_1 \in \mathbb{R}^{16 \times 10}, \quad \mathbf{A}_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{16 \times 8}, \quad \mathbf{A}_3 \in \mathbb{R}^{16 \times 3}$$

**Final Output Shape:** $\mathbb{R}^{16 \times 3}$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Fully Connected Layers with Convolutional Layers in terms of connectivity, parameter efficiency, translation invariance, and typical use-case. Why are FC layers unsuitable as the first layer in image classification networks?**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | FC Layer | Convolutional Layer |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| Connectivity | All-to-all (dense) | Local receptive field |
| Parameters | $(n \times m) + m$ | $k_h \times k_w \times C_{in} \times C_{out}$ |
| Translation invariance | No | Yes (via weight sharing) |
| Spatial structure | Lost | Preserved |
| Use case | Tabular data, classifier head | Feature extraction from images |

**Why FC is unsuitable as the first image layer:**

1. **Parameter explosion:** A $224 \times 224$ RGB image flattened yields a $150{,}528$-dim vector. Even a modest 1024-neuron FC layer would require $150{,}528 \times 1024 \approx 154$ million parameters — intractable and prone to overfitting.

2. **Loss of spatial structure:** FC layers treat each pixel as an independent feature, destroying the local 2-D neighborhood relationships critical for visual recognition.

3. **No translation invariance:** A cat shifted 5 pixels right would produce a completely different FC representation, while a CNN with weight sharing recognizes it identically.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias count.** Many students compute only $n \times m$ and miss the $+m$ bias terms. This costs 1–2 marks in parameter-count problems.
> 2. **Transposed weight matrix confusion.** In the batched formulation, the multiplication is $\mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^{T}$, not $\mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}$. Drawing the shape diagram clearly avoids this error.
> 3. **Missing the activation derivative in backprop.** Students often write $\delta = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{A}}$ and forget the $\odot f'(\mathbf{Z})$ factor. The full chain rule application is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **Confusing FC with "fully convolutional" terminology.** "Fully Connected" means *dense* connections; "Fully Convolutional" means *all layers are convolutions*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Not averaging gradients over the batch.** When asked to write the gradient, include the $\frac{1}{B}$ factor — it represents the mean loss over the mini-batch.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Definition:** FC layer = dense all-to-all connectivity between adjacent layers.
- **Core equation:** $\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b})$.
- **Batched form:** $\mathbf{A} = f(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^{T} + \mathbf{b})$ with bias broadcast.
- **Parameter formula:** $P = (n \times m) + m$ — always include the bias!
- **Activation is non-negotiable** — without it, depth is meaningless.
- **Backprop equations** (memorize all four):
  * $\delta = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{A}} \odot f'(\mathbf{Z})$
  * $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \frac{1}{B} \mathbf{X}^{T} \delta$
  * $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i} \delta_i$
  * $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{X}} = \delta \mathbf{W}$
- **Shape tracking is your best friend** — always annotate $(\text{rows} \times \text{cols})$ for every tensor in derivations.
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A single hidden FC layer with enough neurons can approximate any continuous function.
- **Position in pipeline:** FC layers are typically the **classifier head**, not the feature extractor.
- **Parameter explosion risk:** FC layers are the dominant memory consumer in many networks; prefer convolutions for image data.
- **Distinguish FC from Fully Convolutional Networks (FCN).** Same acronym root, completely different concepts.
- **Common activations:** ReLU (hidden), Sigmoid (binary output), Softmax (multi-class output), Linear (regression output).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training CNNs - Backpropagation and Initialization, Optimization Algorithms - SGD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET865 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Training CNNs - Backpropagation and Initialization, Optimization Algorithms - SGD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Training CNNs & Optimization

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What Does "Training a CNN" Actually Mean?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Training a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is the iterative process of minimizing a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ with respect to the network's learnable parameters $\theta$ (filters $W$, biases $b$, and fully-connected weights), by propagating prediction errors backward through the network (Backpropagation) and updating parameters using a gradient-based Optimization Algorithm.*

In simple words — when a CNN classifies an image (e.g., "cat" vs. "dog"), it makes a **prediction**. The prediction is almost never perfect on the first try. Training is the act of:

1. **Measuring the mistake** (Loss function).
2. **Figuring out which weight in which layer is responsible** for the mistake (Backpropagation).
3. **Nudging those weights in the right direction** (Optimization — e.g., SGD).

> [!NOTE]
> This is the same three-step recipe for *every* neural network. What makes CNNs special is **how** the backward step behaves inside convolution and pooling layers.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Mountain Climber

Imagine you are standing on a foggy mountain (the loss landscape $\mathcal{L}$). You want to reach the valley (minimum loss). You cannot see the ground, but you can **feel the slope of the ground under your feet** (the gradient $\nabla \mathcal{L}$).

- **Backpropagation** = the act of computing the slope under your feet. In a CNN, the "feet" are spread across millions of filter weights in many layers, so computing this slope is non-trivial — we use the **chain rule of calculus** layer-by-layer backward.
- **Optimization (SGD)** = the act of taking a small step downhill based on that slope. The *step size* is the **learning rate $\eta$**.
- **Initialization** = where you are dropped on the mountain at the start. Drop yourself on a sheer cliff and you might fall into a bad valley (poor local minimum). Initialization strategies are about choosing a sensible starting position.

---

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of This Topic

| Pillar | What It Does | Key Math Object |
|---|---|---|
| **Backpropagation** | Computes $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$, $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b}$ for every layer | Chain rule, Convolution gradients |
| **Initialization** | Sets the initial values of $W$, $b$ before training begins | Variance-controlled sampling |
| **Optimization (SGD)** | Uses the gradients to update weights iteratively | Update rule $W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** Questions are frequently asked (a) on the backward pass through a **convolution layer** and (b) comparing **SGD with its variants (Momentum, Nesterov, Adam)**. Memorize the update equations — they appear almost every semester.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Backpropagation in a CNN — Layer-by-Layer Logic

A standard CNN forward pass has this structure:

$$X \;\xrightarrow{\text{Conv}}\; Z^{[l]} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Activation}}\; A^{[l]} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Pool}}\; P^{[l]} \;\to \;\dots \;\to\; \hat{Y}$$

To backpropagate, we need $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{[l]}}$ and $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A^{[l-1]}}$ for every layer $l$.

### 2.1.1 Backward Pass Through a Fully-Connected (Dense) Layer

This is the standard MLP backprop you already know:

$$dZ^{[l]} = dA^{[l]} \odot g'^{[l]}(Z^{[l]})$$

$$dW^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} dZ^{[l]} \, A^{[l-1]T}$$

$$dA^{[l-1]} = W^{[l]T} \, dZ^{[l]}$$

$$db^{[l]} = \frac{1}{m} \sum dZ^{[l]}$$

where $dA^{[L]}$ (the gradient at the output) is initialized using the loss derivative — e.g., for softmax + cross-entropy:

$$dZ^{[L]} = \hat{Y} - Y$$

---

### 2.1.2 Backward Pass Through a Pooling Layer

Pooling has **no learnable parameters**, so its backward pass only routes the gradient correctly.

- **Max Pooling:** The gradient $dA$ from the next layer is a matrix of the same shape as the pooled output. We scatter each value back to the **exact position that held the maximum** during the forward pass; all other positions receive **zero**. A "mask" $M$ records these positions.

$$dA^{[l-1]} = M \odot dP^{[l]}$$

- **Average Pooling:** Each value in $dP$ is **distributed equally** to all cells of the corresponding pooling window.

$$dA^{[l-1]}[i,j] = \frac{1}{k^2}\, dP^{[l]}\left[\left\lfloor i/k \right\rfloor,\, \left\lfloor j/k \right\rfloor\right]$$

---

### 2.1.3 Backward Pass Through a Convolution Layer — The Heart of CNN Training

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the highest-weightage derivation in KTU Module 2. Master it.**

Let the forward convolution be:
$$Z^{[l]} = W^{[l]} * A^{[l-1]} + b^{[l]}$$

where $*$ denotes the discrete convolution operation. We need two gradients.

#### Gradient w.r.t. the filter $W^{[l]}$
The gradient of the loss w.r.t. each filter is the **convolution of the input volume $A^{[l-1]}$ with the output gradient $dZ^{[l]}$** (where $dZ^{[l]}$ is flipped spatially — implementation note).

$$dW^{[l]} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{[l]}} = \text{conv}\!\left(A^{[l-1]},\; dZ^{[l]}\right)$$

**Intuition:** *Pixels of the input that produced large errors (large $dZ$) cause large updates in the filter weights that detected them.*

#### Gradient w.r.t. the input $A^{[l-1]}$
To backpropagate further to earlier layers, we need the gradient w.r.t. the input. This is the **full convolution** (valid padding flipped) of the output gradient with the filter:

$$dA^{[l-1]} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A^{[l-1]}} = \text{full-conv}\!\left(dZ^{[l]},\; W^{[l]}\right)$$

**Intuition:** *Every filter "projects" its error back onto the input region it looked at.*

#### Gradient w.r.t. the bias
$$db^{[l]} = \sum_{i,j} dZ^{[l]} \quad \text{(sum over all spatial locations)}$$

---

### 2.2 Weight Initialization

| Initialization | Formula (variance) | When to Use | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zero Initialization** | $W=0$ | ❌ Never | **Symmetry problem** — every neuron learns the same feature |
| **Random Small** | $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 0.01)$ | Shallow nets | **Vanishing/exploding activations** in deep nets |
| **Xavier (Glorot)** | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \dfrac{1}{n_{in}}\right)$ or $\dfrac{2}{n_{in}+n_{out}}$ | tanh, sigmoid, softmax | Sub-optimal for ReLU |
| **He Initialization** | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \dfrac{2}{n_{in}}\right)$ | **ReLU, Leaky ReLU** | None for typical CNNs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (He, 2015):** For a ReLU network of depth $L$, the variance of activations is preserved when $\text{Var}(W) = \dfrac{2}{n_{in}}$. This is the **default for modern CNNs**.

---

### 2.3 Optimization Algorithms — Gradient Descent Family

#### 2.3.1 Batch Gradient Descent (BGD)
Uses the **entire training set** to compute one gradient per update.

$$W := W - \eta \, \nabla_{W} \mathcal{L}(W;\, X_{\text{full}}, Y_{\text{full}})$$

- ✅ Stable convergence.
- ❌ Very slow for large $N$; cannot fit dataset in memory.

#### 2.3.2 Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
Uses **one sample** $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ per update.

$$W := W - \eta \, \nabla_{W} \mathcal{L}(W;\, x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

- ✅ Fast per update, can escape shallow local minima via noise.
- ❌ Noisy trajectory, never settles at the minimum.

#### 2.3.3 Mini-Batch SGD (The Practical Default)
Uses a **batch of $m$ samples** $\{x^{(i:i+m)}, y^{(i:i+m)}\}$.

$$W := W - \eta \, \nabla_{W} \mathcal{L}\!\left(W;\, X^{[i:i+m]}, Y^{[i:i+m]}\right)$$

- ✅ Best of both worlds: stable *and* fast. Vectorized GPU computation.
- Typical $m \in \{32, 64, 128, 256\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In KTU parlance and in modern literature, "SGD" almost always means Mini-Batch SGD.** Be careful in exams to clarify which you mean.

#### 2.3.4 SGD with Momentum
Adds a "velocity" term that accumulates past gradients, smoothing the trajectory.

$$v_{t} = \beta\, v_{t-1} + \eta\, \nabla_{W} \mathcal{L}(W_t)$$

$$W_{t+1} = W_t - v_t$$

- $\beta$ is the momentum coefficient, typically $\beta = 0.9$.
- **Physical analogy:** A heavy ball rolling downhill — it builds up speed in consistent directions and dampens oscillations across ravines.

#### 2.3.5 Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG)
Looks ahead before computing the gradient.

$$v_{t} = \beta\, v_{t-1} + \eta\, \nabla_{W} \mathcal{L}(W_t - \beta\, v_{t-1})$$

$$W_{t+1} = W_t - v_t$$

- **Intuition:** "Calculate the gradient as if you had already taken the momentum step." Gives a corrective response, faster convergence on convex problems.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conv forward | $Z = W * A + b$ | $W$ is the filter |
| 2 | $dW$ for Conv | $dW = A_{\text{prev}} * dZ$ | Cross-correlation form |
| 3 | $dA$ for Conv | $dA = dZ *_{\text{full}} W$ | Full convolution with flipped $W$ |
| 4 | Max-pool backward | $dA_{\text{prev}} = M \odot dP$ | $M$ stores argmax positions |
| 5 | Avg-pool backward | $dA_{\text{prev}}[i,j] = \dfrac{1}{k^2} dP$ | Uniform distribution |
| 6 | Xavier init | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \dfrac{1}{n_{in}}\right)$ | tanh / sigmoid |
| 7 | He init | $W \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \dfrac{2}{n_{in}}\right)$ | **ReLU** |
| 8 | BGD update | $W := W - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_{\text{full}}$ | Full batch |
| 9 | SGD update | $W := W - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}^{(i)}$ | One sample |
| 10 | Mini-batch SGD | $W := W - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}^{\{i:i+m\}}$ | **Default in practice** |
| 11 | Momentum | $v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$, $W := W - v_t$ | $\beta \approx 0.9$ |
| 12 | Nesterov | $v_t = \beta v_{t-1} + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(W - \beta v_{t-1})$ | Lookahead |
| 13 | Softmax+CE grad | $dZ = \hat{Y} - Y$ | Direct, no $g'$ needed |
| 14 | Bias gradient | $db = \sum dZ$ over spatial dims | Per filter |

> **Notation key:** $*$ = convolution, $*_{\text{full}}$ = full-padding convolution, $dZ = \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial Z$, $\odot$ = element-wise product, $n_{in}$ = number of inputs to a neuron, $n_{out}$ = number of outputs.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Directive:** These derivations are *high-yield* in KTU ESE. Understand each algebraic step — partial credit is awarded for the chain of logic, not just the final answer.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient w.r.t. Filter Weights in a Convolution Layer

### Setup
We define the forward convolution (in the *cross-correlation* form used by all deep learning libraries) for a single filter $W$ on input $A$:

$$Z_{i,j} = \sum_{u=0}^{f-1} \sum_{v=0}^{f-1} W_{u,v}\, A_{i+u,\, j+v} + b$$

where $f \times f$ is the filter size.

### Step 1 — Loss Decomposition via the Chain Rule
The total loss $\mathcal{L}$ depends on every output pixel $Z_{i,j}$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{u,v}} = \sum_{i} \sum_{j} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z_{i,j}} \cdot \frac{\partial Z_{i,j}}{\partial W_{u,v}}$$

*Logic:* A single weight $W_{u,v}$ contributes to *every* output cell $Z_{i,j}$ in the feature map, so we must sum over all such contributions.

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Local Derivative
From the forward equation:

$$\frac{\partial Z_{i,j}}{\partial W_{u,v}} = A_{i+u,\, j+v}$$

because $W_{u,v}$ multiplies $A_{i+u,j+v}$ directly (and $b$ has no dependence on $W$).

### Step 3 — Substitute and Recognize the Convolution
Substituting back:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{u,v}} = \sum_{i} \sum_{j} \underbrace{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z_{i,j}}}_{dZ_{i,j}} \cdot A_{i+u,\, j+v}$$

This is *exactly* the (cross-correlation) convolution of $dZ$ with $A$:

$$dW = A * dZ \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Conclusion:** To update the filter, convolve the input activation with the output-error map.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Gradient w.r.t. Input Activations

### Step 1 — Identify the dependence
A single input activation $A_{i,j}$ contributes to every output cell $Z_{i-u, j-v}$ where the filter overlaps it. So:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A_{i,j}} = \sum_{u} \sum_{v} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Z_{i-u,\, j-v}} \cdot \frac{\partial Z_{i-u,\, j-v}}{\partial A_{i,j}}$$

### Step 2 — Local derivative
$$\frac{\partial Z_{i-u, j-v}}{\partial A_{i,j}} = W_{u,v}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial A_{i,j}} = \sum_{u} \sum_{v} dZ_{i-u,\, j-v} \cdot W_{u,v}$$

This is the **full convolution** of the gradient map $dZ$ with the (spatially-flipped) filter $W$:

$$dA = dZ *_{\text{full}} W_{\text{flipped}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The full convolution enlarges the output spatially, matching the shape of $A$. This is why we can chain it with the next layer's gradient.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — SGD on a Toy Loss

Suppose we have a single scalar weight $W$ and a one-sample squared-error loss:

$$\mathcal{L}(W) = \tfrac{1}{2}(y - W x)^2$$

Let $y = 2$, $x = 3$, and current $W = 0.5$, learning rate $\eta = 0.1$.

### Step 1 — Forward pass
$$\hat{y} = W x = 0.5 \times 3 = 1.5$$

### Step 2 — Compute loss
$$\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2}(2 - 1.5)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.5)^2 = 0.125$$

### Step 3 — Compute gradient
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = -(y - \hat{y})\, x = -(0.5)(3) = -1.5$$

### Step 4 — SGD update
$$W_{\text{new}} = W - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = 0.5 - 0.1(-1.5) = 0.65$$

### Step 5 — Verify the loss decreased
$$\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = 0.65 \times 3 = 1.95, \quad \mathcal{L}_{\text{new}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.05)^2 = 0.00125 \;\downarrow$$

✅ The update moved $W$ in the correct direction, reducing loss from $0.125$ to $0.00125$ in a single step.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Optimizers

| Property | BGD | SGD | Mini-Batch SGD | SGD + Momentum | Nesterov |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Samples per update | $N$ | $1$ | $m$ | $m$ | $m$ |
| Gradient noise | None | High | Low–Med | Low–Med | Low–Med |
| Convergence speed | Slow | Fast (per step) | **Fastest (wall-clock)** | Faster | Fastest (convex) |
| Memory needed | Very high | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Hyperparameter added | — | — | batch size $m$ | $\beta$ | $\beta$ |
| Escapes local minima? | Poorly | Yes (via noise) | Yes | Yes | Yes (better) |
| Used in production? | Rarely | Noisy baseline | ✅ **Most common** | ✅ Very common | ✅ Common |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Momentum SGD

Same toy problem: $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2}(y - Wx)^2$, $y=2$, $x=3$, $\eta = 0.1$, $\beta = 0.9$. Start $W_0 = 0.5$, $v_0 = 0$.

### Iteration 1
$$g_1 = -1.5, \quad v_1 = (0.9)(0) + (0.1)(-1.5) = -0.15$$
$$W_1 = 0.5 - (-0.15) = 0.65$$

### Iteration 2
$$\hat{y}_1 = 1.95, \quad g_2 = -(0.05)(3) = -0.15$$
$$v_2 = (0.9)(-0.15) + (0.1)(-0.15) = -0.135 - 0.015 = -0.15$$
$$W_2 = 0.65 - (-0.15) = 0.80$$

> **Observation:** The velocity $v$ is *larger in magnitude* than the raw gradient $g$ alone would give, because the momentum term carries over previous direction. This accelerates convergence along consistent directions.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNN Training — Forward and Backward Pass Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input Image X] --> C1[Conv Layer L1]
    C1 --> A1[ReLU Activation]
    A1 --> P1[Max Pooling]
    P1 --> C2[Conv Layer L2]
    C2 --> A2[ReLU Activation]
    A2 --> P2[Max Pooling]
    P2 --> F[Flatten]
    F --> FC[Fully Connected]
    FC --> SM[Softmax]
    SM --> Yhat[Prediction Yhat]
    Yhat --> L[Compute Loss L]
    L -- Backward Pass --> BP[Backpropagation]
    BP --> BP1[Gradient dZ at Output]
    BP1 --> BP2[Backprop through FC]
    BP2 --> BP3[Backprop through Pool 2]
    BP3 --> BP4[Backprop through Conv 2]
    BP4 --> BP5[Backprop through Pool 1]
    BP5 --> BP6[Backprop through Conv 1]
    BP6 --> OPT[Optimizer SGD Updates W and b]
    OPT -.Update.-> C1
    OPT -.Update.-> C2
    OPT -.Update.-> FC
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of Gradient Flow Through a Convolution Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Aprev[A_prev - Input Activation Map] --> FWD[Forward Convolution with Filter W]
    W[Filter W and bias b] --> FWD
    FWD --> Z[Z - Pre-activation Feature Map]
    Z --> ACT[ReLU Activation]
    ACT --> A[A - Output of Layer]
    A --> NEXT[Pass to Next Layer]
    A -. dA arrives.-> ROUTE[Route gradient dA]
    ROUTE --> DRELU[Multiply by ReLU derivative g prime Z]
    DRELU --> DZ[dZ - Gradient w.r.t. Z]
    DZ --> CALCW[Compute dW via conv of A_prev and dZ]
    DZ --> CALCA[Compute dA_prev via full conv of dZ and W]
    DZ --> CALCB[Compute db by summing dZ spatially]
    CALCW --> UPDATE[Parameter Update via SGD]
    CALCB --> UPDATE
    CALCA --> BACK[Pass dA_prev to previous layer]
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing an Optimizer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Need to train a CNN] --> Q1{Dataset fits in memory?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> BGD[Batch Gradient Descent]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Need stable, fast convergence?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> MB[Use Mini-Batch SGD]
    Q2 -- No, research --> SGDpure[Pure SGD]
    MB --> Q3{Is the loss surface ravine-shaped?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> MOM[SGD plus Momentum beta 0.9]
    Q3 -- No --> ADAM[Adam or RMSprop]
    Q3 -- Uncertain --> NEST[Nesterov Accelerated Gradient]
    MOM --> DONE[Train CNN]
    NEST --> DONE
    ADAM --> DONE
    BGD --> DONE
    SGDpure --> DONE
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic of a Single SGD Iteration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Sample mini-batch from dataset] --> S2[Step 2: Forward pass compute Yhat]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute loss L Y Yhat]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Backward pass compute dW and db for all layers]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute velocity v if using momentum]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Update parameters W and b]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Repeat for next mini-batch or stop if converged]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
