**(a)** [7 Marks] Draw and explain the architecture of a basic RNN cell. Derive the forward pass equations for an unrolled RNN with sequence length $T = 3$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **vanishing and exploding gradient problems** in RNNs with mathematical justification. List at least **two** solutions for each problem.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RNN Cell Architecture and Forward Pass:**

The basic RNN cell consists of three weight matrices: $W_{xh}$ (input-to-hidden), $W_{hh}$ (hidden-to-hidden), and $W_{hy}$ (hidden-to-output). The same matrices are reused at every time step.

**Unrolled forward pass for $T = 3$:**

$$a_1 = W_{xh} x_1 + W_{hh} h_0 + b_h, \quad h_1 = \tanh(a_1), \quad \hat{y}_1 = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_1 + b_y)$$

$$a_2 = W_{xh} x_2 + W_{hh} h_1 + b_h, \quad h_2 = \tanh(a_2), \quad \hat{y}_2 = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_2 + b_y)$$

$$a_3 = W_{xh} x_3 + W_{hh} h_2 + b_h, \quad h_3 = \tanh(a_3), \quad \hat{y}_3 = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_3 + b_y)$$

The total loss is $L = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$.

**(b) Vanishing and Exploding Gradients:**

**Vanishing gradients:** During BPTT, the gradient involves a product of Jacobians: $\prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$. Since each $\tanh$ derivative is bounded in $(0, 1]$ and spectral norm of $W_{hh}$ may be $< 1$, the product decays **exponentially** with sequence length. As a result, $\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_k} \to 0$ for distant $k$, and the network cannot learn long-range dependencies.

**Exploding gradients:** If the spectral norm of $W_{hh}$ exceeds 1, the product grows exponentially, causing gradient values to overflow (NaN losses) and destabilize training.

**Solutions:**

| **Problem** | **Solutions** |
|---|---|
| Vanishing | (1) Use **LSTM** or **GRU** with gating mechanisms. (2) Use **orthogonal initialization** of $W_{hh}$ with $\sigma_{\max} = 1$. (3) Use **skip connections** (ResNet-style) across time. |
| Exploding | (1) **Gradient clipping** — rescale gradient if norm exceeds threshold. (2) **Truncated BPTT** — limit backpropagation window. |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **four major RNN architecture types** (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many) with one real-world example for each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe **Truncated BPTT** and **Gradient Clipping** in detail. Why are they necessary in practice?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four RNN Architecture Types:**

1. **One-to-One:** Single input, single output. Example: Standard image classification (technically a vanilla feedforward network, included for completeness).
2. **One-to-Many:** Single input produces a sequence. Example: **Image captioning** — input is one image, output is a sequence of words describing it.
3. **Many-to-One:** Sequence input produces a single output. Example: **Sentiment classification** — input is a sentence, output is a positive/negative label.
4. **Many-to-Many (synced):** Each input step produces an output. Example: **Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging** — each word is tagged with its grammatical role.
5. **Many-to-Many (delayed / Encoder-Decoder):** Input and output sequences have different lengths. Example: **Machine translation** — English sentence (10 words) → French sentence (12 words).

**(b) Truncated BPTT and Gradient Clipping:**

**Truncated BPTT:** Instead of backpropagating through the entire sequence, we split the sequence into chunks of length $k$ (e.g., $k = 50$). The forward pass still runs through all chunks, but the backward pass only unrolls $k$ steps. This **reduces computation** and **mitigates exploding gradients** at the cost of ignoring very long-range dependencies. Mathematically:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} \approx \sum_{\text{chunks}} \sum_{t \in \text{chunk}} \sum_{k=t-k_{\max}}^{t} (\text{truncated gradient})$$

**Gradient Clipping:** When the gradient norm exceeds a threshold $c$ (commonly $c = 5.0$), the gradient is rescaled:

$$g \leftarrow \begin{cases} g & \text{if } \|g\| \leq c \\ \dfrac{c}{\|g\|} \cdot g & \text{if } \|g\| > c \end{cases}$$

This prevents the update step from overshooting catastrophically and stabilizes training.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term** in the forward pass equation — students often write only $W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1}$ and forget $b_h$. Loss of **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing the activation** — the hidden state uses $\tanh$ or ReLU, but the output uses softmax (for classification). Mixing them up costs marks.
> 3. **Not summing losses** — the total loss is $L = \sum L_t$, not just the loss at the final time step. Many students compute only $L_T$ and lose **2 marks**.
> 4. **Vanishing vs. Exploding distinction** — students often claim "the gradient becomes 0" for both problems. Vanishing is bounded by 1 from above (and decays); exploding is unbounded and grows. Be precise.
> 5. **BPTT formula** — in the product $\prod_{i=k+1}^{t}$, the indices are subtle. Off-by-one errors are common; show the expansion explicitly.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **RNN core equation:** $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$ — memorize this.
- ✅ **Output equation:** $y_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y$ followed by softmax for classification.
- ✅ **Weight sharing:** The matrices $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are **shared across all time steps** — this is the key parameter-saving trick.
- ✅ **BPTT** = unroll the RNN $T$ times → apply backprop on the unrolled feedforward graph.
- ✅ **Total loss:** $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$ (sum over all time steps).
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient** occurs when $\left\| W_{hh} \right\|_\sigma < 1$ — the product of Jacobians decays exponentially.
- ✅ **Exploding gradient** occurs when $\left\| W_{hh} \right\|_\sigma > 1$ — solved by **gradient clipping** (threshold ~5.0).
- ✅ **LSTM/GRU** are the practical solutions for vanishing gradients due to **gating mechanisms**.
- ✅ **5 architecture types:** one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many (synced), many-to-many (delayed/encoder-decoder).
- ✅ **Truncated BPTT** uses chunks of length $k$ to limit computation and stabilize training.
- ✅ **Standard dimensions:** $W_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_x}$, $W_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h \times d_h}$, $W_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_y \times d_h}$.
- ✅ **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Explain vanishing gradient with mathematical justification" or "Compare LSTM vs RNN" — both are high-yield.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backpropagation Through Time, Vanishing and Exploding Gradients, Architecture of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Architecture of Gated Recurrent Units (GRU)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET865 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backpropagation Through Time, Vanishing and Exploding Gradients, Architecture of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Architecture of Gated Recurrent Units (GRU)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Backpropagation Through Time is the gradient-based learning algorithm applied to **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)** by unrolling the network across all time steps and applying the standard backpropagation algorithm on this unrolled computational graph to compute gradients of the loss with respect to the network parameters.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a teacher grading a student's essay that was written **one word at a time, over 100 days**. To know which words were bad, you must trace the grade back through **every single day** to see how each day's writing was influenced by all previous days. BPTT does exactly this: it "unrolls" the RNN through time so the error from the final prediction can flow backward step-by-step, telling each weight how much it contributed to the mistake.

### Key Engineering Insight
> [!IMPORTANT]
> BPTT converts a **temporal/sequential learning problem** into a **very deep feedforward network** problem, where depth equals the number of time steps. The total loss is summed across all time steps before backpropagation begins.

---

## 1.2 Vanishing and Exploding Gradients

> [!NOTE]
> **Vanishing Gradient Problem:** During BPTT, gradients shrink **exponentially** as they are propagated backward through many time steps, causing earlier layers/time steps to receive negligible updates and the network to fail to learn **long-term dependencies**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exploding Gradient Problem:** During BPTT, gradients grow **exponentially** as they are propagated backward through many time steps, causing unstable, extremely large weight updates that lead to numerical overflow (`NaN` values) and divergence of training.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a chain of 1000 whispering friends. If each friend whispers **half** of what they heard (vanishing), the original message never reaches the start. If each friend **doubles** the volume (exploding), the last person is deafened — and the message becomes meaningless noise.

---

## 1.3 Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) Network

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** LSTM is a specialized RNN architecture introduced by **Hochreiter and Schmidhuber (1997)** that uses a **memory cell** along with three gating mechanisms — **forget gate, input gate, and output gate** — to regulate the flow of information and mitigate the vanishing gradient problem.

### Conceptual Analogy
An LSTM cell is like a **smart conveyor belt in a factory**. Three control levers (gates) decide: (1) what to **throw away** from the belt (forget gate), (2) what **new items to load** onto the belt (input gate), and (3) what to **unload** as the final output (output gate). The belt itself (cell state) carries information safely across long distances.

---

## 1.4 Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** GRU is a simplified variant of the LSTM proposed by **Cho et al. (2014)** that combines the forget and input gates into a single **update gate** and merges the cell state and hidden state, resulting in a more computationally efficient architecture with fewer parameters.

### Conceptual Analogy
GRU is like an **LSTM that has been put on a diet** — fewer moving parts (2 gates instead of 3, no separate cell state), making it faster and lighter, while still solving the vanishing gradient problem. It is the **economy class** version of LSTM.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Module 3:** Time step index $\tau$ or $t$, hidden state dimension $n_h$, batch size $m$, sequence length $T$, sigmoid activation $\sigma$, hyperbolic tangent $\tanh$, and Hadamard (element-wise) product $\odot$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Recurrent Neural Network — Forward Pass Recap

A vanilla RNN cell at time step $t$ performs the following computations:

$$
h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + W_{xh} \, x_t + b_h)
$$

$$
\hat{y}_t = W_{hy} \, h_t + b_y
$$

Where:
- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the input at time $t$.
- $h_t \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}$ is the hidden state.
- $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are **shared** weight matrices across all time steps.
- $\hat{y}_t$ is the predicted output.

The total loss for an entire sequence of length $T$ is the sum of per-time-step losses:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)
$$

---

## 2.2 Why Standard Backpropagation Fails on RNNs
- Standard backpropagation assumes a **fixed acyclic graph**.
- RNNs have **cyclic connections** ($h_t$ depends on $h_{t-1}$), so the graph is a **cycle**.
- BPTT solves this by **unrolling** the cycle into $T$ copies of the same network, producing a deep acyclic graph.

---

## 2.3 The Core Mathematics of BPTT

The gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}_t$ at time $t$ with respect to a parameter $W$ (e.g., $W_{hh}$) requires applying the **multivariate chain rule** across all intermediate time steps. For the hidden-to-hidden weight $W_{hh}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

The total gradient for the entire sequence is:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **product of Jacobians** $\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$ is the **mathematical origin** of the vanishing and exploding gradient problems. If this product is repeatedly multiplied by values less than 1, it collapses to zero; if greater than 1, it explodes to infinity.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Explanation** |
|---|---|---|
| RNN Hidden State | $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b_h)$ | Standard RNN cell forward pass |
| Total Sequence Loss | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$ | Sum of losses across all $T$ steps |
| BPTT Gradient | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}$ | Chain rule across time |
| Jacobian Element | $\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot)\right) W_{hh}^T$ | Bounded by $[-1, 1]$ for $\tanh$ |
| Vanishing Condition | $\Vert \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \Vert < 1$ (repeated) | Product → 0 as $T \to \infty$ |
| Exploding Condition | $\Vert \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \Vert > 1$ (repeated) | Product → $\infty$ as $T \to \infty$ |
| LSTM Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Decides what to discard |
| LSTM Input Gate | $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | Decides what new info to store |
| LSTM Candidate | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | New candidate values |
| LSTM Cell State | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Long-term memory update |
| LSTM Output Gate | $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Controls output exposure |
| LSTM Hidden State | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | Final cell output |
| GRU Update Gate | $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | Balances past vs. present |
| GRU Reset Gate | $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | Decides how much past to forget |
| GRU Candidate | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$ | Candidate new hidden state |
| GRU Hidden State | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ | Linear interpolation of past & new |

> [!WARNING]
> The Hadamard product $\odot$ is **element-wise multiplication**, NOT matrix multiplication. This is a critical distinction that KTU examiners frequently test.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Speech Recognition** (Google Assistant, Siri): LSTMs model audio as time series.
- **Machine Translation** (Google Translate): Encoder-decoder with LSTM/GRU.
- **Stock Price Forecasting**: Captures long-term financial dependencies.
- **Music Generation, Chatbots, DNA Sequence Analysis**: All rely on gated RNNs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of BPTT Gradient for $W_{hh}$

We will now derive, step by step, the gradient of the loss at time $t$ with respect to the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$.

### Step 1 — Define the forward pass
The pre-activation at time step $j$ is given by:

$$
a_j = W_{hh} \, h_{j-1} + W_{xh} \, x_j + b_h
$$

The hidden state is the activation:

$$
h_j = \tanh(a_j)
$$

The predicted output is:

$$
\hat{y}_j = W_{hy} \, h_j + b_y
$$

The per-step loss (e.g., cross-entropy) is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_j = \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}_j, y_j)
$$

The total loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_j
$$

---

### Step 2 — Identify the chain of dependencies
The hidden state $h_t$ at time $t$ depends on $h_{t-1}$, which depends on $h_{t-2}$, ..., which depends on $h_0$. The chain rule across all these time steps gives:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

But $h_t$ itself depends on $W_{hh}$ through $h_{t-1}, h_{t-2}, \ldots$, so we must apply the chain rule **recursively**:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Substitute and simplify
Combining the two equations:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

When the convention "empty product = 1" is used, the term with $k = t$ becomes:

$$
\left( \prod_{j=t+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) = 1
$$

So the $k = t$ term is simply:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

which is the standard backpropagation result for the **last time step**.

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$
From the forward equation $h_j = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{j-1} + W_{xh} x_j + b_h)$:

$$
\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(a_j)\right) \cdot W_{hh}^T
$$

Let $\text{diag}_j = \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a_j))$. Then the product becomes:

$$
\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}_j \cdot W_{hh}^T
$$

---

### Step 5 — Analyze the gradient's behavior
Define the spectral radius $\rho$ of the matrix $\text{diag}_j \cdot W_{hh}^T$ as its largest absolute eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$. Then:

$$
\left\Vert \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}_j \cdot W_{hh}^T \right\Vert \approx \lambda_{\max}^{(t - k)}
$$

- **If $\lambda_{\max} < 1$:** $\lambda_{\max}^{(t - k)} \to 0$ exponentially as $t - k$ grows. → **Vanishing Gradient**.
- **If $\lambda_{\max} > 1$:** $\lambda_{\max}^{(t - k)} \to \infty$ exponentially as $t - k$ grows. → **Exploding Gradient**.
- **If $\lambda_{\max} \approx 1$:** Gradient is preserved. → This is what LSTM's cell-state additive update achieves.

---

## 3.2 Derivations of LSTM Gate Equations

The LSTM cell maintains a **cell state** $C_t$ in addition to the hidden state $h_t$. We derive each gate's role.

### Step 1 — Forget Gate
The forget gate $f_t$ decides what fraction of the **previous cell state** $C_{t-1}$ to retain. It is a sigmoid layer that squashes values to $[0, 1]$:

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)
$$

where $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ denotes vector concatenation. Value $f_t = 0$ means "forget everything", value $f_t = 1$ means "keep everything".

### Step 2 — Input Gate and Candidate
The input gate $i_t$ decides which candidate values to **update**:

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)
$$

The candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ creates new values that **could be added** to the state:

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)
$$

### Step 3 — Cell State Update
The new cell state is a **linear interpolation** (using the forget and input gates as weights):

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **additive** structure $f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$. The cell state update is **linear** in $C_{t-1}$, so the gradient flowing through $C$ does not get repeatedly squashed by a nonlinearity. This is the **mathematical key** to LSTM solving the vanishing gradient problem.

### Step 4 — Output Gate and Hidden State
The output gate $o_t$ decides what part of the cell state to expose as the hidden state:

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)
$$

$$
h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivations of GRU Equations

### Step 1 — Update Gate
The update gate $z_t$ is analogous to LSTM's forget + input gates combined. It decides how much of the past hidden state to keep:

$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)
$$

### Step 2 — Reset Gate
The reset gate $r_t$ controls how much of the past to "reset" when computing the candidate:

$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)
$$

### Step 3 — Candidate Hidden State
The candidate $\tilde{h}_t$ is computed using the reset-gated previous hidden state:

$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)
$$

If $r_t \approx 0$, the previous hidden state is ignored; if $r_t \approx 1$, it is fully used.

### Step 4 — Final Hidden State (Linear Interpolation)
The new hidden state is a **convex combination** of the old hidden state and the candidate:

$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
$$

> [!TIP]
> When $z_t = 0$, $h_t = h_{t-1}$ (pure copy, no update). When $z_t = 1$, $h_t = \tilde{h}_t$ (complete update, ignore past). This convex combination is a **constant error carousel** (CEC) mechanism analogous to LSTM's cell state.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Vanilla RNN** | **LSTM** | **GRU** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of Gates | 0 | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Cell State | No (only $h_t$) | Yes ($C_t$ separate from $h_t$) | No (merged with $h_t$) |
| Hidden State Update | Non-linear in $h_{t-1}$ | Additive (linear in $C_{t-1}$) | Additive (linear in $h_{t-1}$) |
| Parameter Count | Lowest | Highest | Intermediate |
| Vanishing Gradient Mitigation | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Training Speed | Fastest | Slowest | Faster than LSTM |
| Long-Term Memory | Poor | Strong | Strong |
| Use Case Recommendation | Short sequences | Long sequences, complex patterns | Moderate sequences, fast prototyping |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Unrolled RNN with BPTT Flow

This diagram illustrates how a recurrent network is unrolled across $T$ time steps and how the gradient propagates backward through the unrolled graph.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1["x1 input"] --> A1["h1 = tanh Wxh x1 + Whh h0 + bh"]
    A1 --> X2["x2 input"]
    X2 --> A2["h2 = tanh Wxh x2 + Whh h1 + bh"]
    A2 --> X3["x3 input"]
    X3 --> A3["h3 = tanh Wxh x3 + Whh h2 + bh"]
    A3 --> X4["xT input"]
    X4 --> A4["hT = tanh Wxh xT + Whh hT-1 + bh"]
    A1 --> Y1["y1 hat output"]
    A2 --> Y2["y2 hat output"]
    A3 --> Y3["y3 hat output"]
    A4 --> YT["yT hat output"]
    Y1 --> L1["L1 loss"]
    Y2 --> L2["L2 loss"]
    Y3 --> L3["L3 loss"]
    YT --> LT["LT loss"]
    L1 --> SUM["L total = sum L1 to LT"]
    L2 --> SUM
    L3 --> SUM
    LT --> SUM
    SUM --> BPTT["BPTT backward pass"]
    BPTT -. gradient flow .-> A4
    BPTT -. gradient flow .-> A3
    BPTT -. gradient flow .-> A2
    BPTT -. gradient flow .-> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed arrows represent the **backward flow of gradients** during BPTT. Notice that gradient information must travel through **every** time step, which is where vanishing/exploding occurs.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: LSTM Cell Architecture

This diagram shows the internal structure of a single LSTM cell with its three gates and the cell state pathway.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    XT["xt input"] --> CONC1["concat ht-1, xt"]
    HTM1["ht-1 prev hidden"] --> CONC1
    CONC1 --> FT["ft = sigma Wf concat + bf forget gate"]
    CONC1 --> IT["it = sigma Wi concat + bi input gate"]
    CONC1 --> CT["Ctilde t = tanh WC concat + bC candidate"]
    CONC1 --> OT["ot = sigma Wo concat + bo output gate"]
    CTM["Ct-1 prev cell state"] --> MUL1["ft elementwise Ct-1 forget"]
    FT --> MUL1
    IT --> MUL2["it elementwise Ctilde t input"]
    CT --> MUL2
    MUL1 --> ADD["Ct = ft Ct-1 + it Ctilde t"]
    MUL2 --> ADD
    ADD --> TANH["tanh Ct"]
    TANH --> MUL3["ot elementwise tanh Ct"]
    OT --> MUL3
    MUL3 --> HT["ht new hidden state"]
    ADD --> OUTC["Ct new cell state"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: GRU Cell Architecture

This diagram shows the simplified GRU cell with its two gates and merged hidden/cell state.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    XT["xt input"] --> CONC["concat ht-1, xt"]
    HTM["ht-1 prev hidden"] --> CONC
    CONC --> ZT["zt = sigma Wz concat + bz update gate"]
    CONC --> RT["rt = sigma Wr concat + br reset gate"]
    RT --> MULR["rt elementwise ht-1 reset gate"]
    HTM --> MULR
    XT --> CONC2["concat rt ht-1, xt"]
    MULR --> CONC2
    CONC2 --> HT["htilde t = tanh W concat + b candidate"]
    ZT --> MULZ1["zt elementwise htilde t"]
    HT --> MULZ1
    ZT --> SUB["1 minus zt"]
    HTM --> MULZ2["1 minus zt elementwise ht-1"]
    SUB --> MULZ2
    MULZ1 --> ADD["ht = 1-zt ht-1 + zt htilde t"]
    MULZ2 --> ADD
    ADD --> OUT["ht new hidden state"]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Vanishing vs Exploding Gradient Flow

This block diagram maps the conceptual difference between vanishing and exploding gradient flow during BPTT.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOSS["L total loss"] --> G1["gradient at t"]
    G1 --> G2["gradient at t-1"]
    G2 --> G3["gradient at t-2"]
    G3 --> G4["gradient at t-3"]
    G4 --> G5["gradient at t-4"]
    G5 --> G6["gradient at t-5"]
    G6 --> G7["gradient at t-6"]
    G7 --> G8["gradient at t-7"]
    G8 --> G9["gradient at t-8"]
    G9 --> G10["gradient at t-9"]
    G10 --> G11["gradient at t-10"]

    G1 -. vanishing case .-> VNOTE["values shrink exponentially to 0"]
    G11 -. exploding case .-> ENOTE["values grow exponentially to infinity"]
```

> [!TIP]
> The same chain behaves **differently** depending on whether the largest singular value of the recurrent Jacobian is less than or greater than 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer:**
Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) is the training algorithm used for Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs). Since RNNs contain cyclic connections (the hidden state at time $t$ depends on the hidden state at time $t-1$), standard backpropagation cannot be applied directly. BPTT overcomes this by **unrolling** the RNN through all $T$ time steps, converting the cyclic graph into a deep acyclic feedforward graph. The total loss $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$ is computed at the output, and then gradients are propagated **backward** through the unrolled graph using the chain rule. The same weight matrices $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are **shared** across all time steps, and their gradients are **summed** across time.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Distinguish between the vanishing and exploding gradient problems in RNNs.**

**Model Answer:**
Both problems arise during BPTT when gradients are propagated backward through many time steps. The key difference is in the **direction** of divergence:

- **Vanishing Gradient:** Gradients shrink **exponentially** to zero. Occurs when the spectral radius of the recurrent Jacobian $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$. Effect: earlier time steps receive negligible weight updates; the network cannot learn long-term dependencies.
- **Exploding Gradient:** Gradients grow **exponentially** to infinity. Occurs when $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$. Effect: numerical overflow, `NaN` losses, unstable training.

Vanishing is harder to detect (silent failure) while exploding is easier to detect (sudden `NaN`). Solutions include LSTM/GRU architectures (for vanishing) and **gradient clipping** (for exploding).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the gradient of the total loss with respect to the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$ during BPTT, clearly showing the chain of dependencies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with equations how LSTM mitigates the vanishing gradient problem. Compare it with GRU. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Forward Pass Equations (1 mark)**
For a vanilla RNN cell at time $t$:

$$
h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b_h)
$$

$$
\hat{y}_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}_{total} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Dependency Chain (1 mark)**
The hidden state $h_t$ depends on $h_{t-1}$, which depends on $h_{t-2}$, ..., which depends on $h_0$. So $W_{hh}$ influences $\mathcal{L}_t$ through **every** intermediate hidden state.

**Step 3 — Apply Chain Rule (2 marks)**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

Using recursive chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

**Step 4 — Final BPTT Equation (2 marks)**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

Total gradient:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{total}}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
$$

**Step 5 — Jacobian Computation (1 mark)**

$$
\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(a_j)\right) \cdot W_{hh}^T
$$

The product of these Jacobians over many time steps causes vanishing/exploding behavior.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**LSTM Equations (3 marks)**
The LSTM cell state update is **additive** rather than purely multiplicative with a nonlinearity:

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

The gradient of the loss with respect to $C_{t-1}$ becomes:

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t)
$$

Since $f_t \in [0, 1]$, the gradient flows backward as a product of forget gate activations, but the **additive structure** prevents the repeated squashing by $\tanh$ derivatives that occurs in vanilla RNNs. This allows gradients to propagate over hundreds of time steps.

**Comparison with GRU (2 marks)**

| Aspect | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| Gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Cell State | Separate $C_t$ and $h_t$ | Single $h_t$ |
| Equation | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | $h_t = (1-z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ |
| Parameters | $4 \times (n_h^2 + n_h \cdot d)$ | $3 \times (n_h^2 + n_h \cdot d)$ |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Memory | Stronger (separate cell) | Slightly less expressive |

**Why Both Solve Vanishing Gradients (2 marks)**
Both architectures use **gating mechanisms** to control information flow and use **additive updates** (linear interpolation) for the recurrent state, which preserves the gradient's magnitude over long sequences. GRU achieves this more compactly by merging the cell and hidden states and combining the forget/input gates into a single update gate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an LSTM cell. List all gate equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the architecture and equations of a GRU. Why is GRU considered computationally lighter than LSTM? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Diagram Description (3 marks)** — A neat block diagram should show:
- Inputs $x_t$ and previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ concatenated and fed into **three** sigmoid layers (forget, input, output gates).
- A $\tanh$ layer producing the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$.
- The cell state $C_{t-1}$ multiplied element-wise with forget gate $f_t$.
- The candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ multiplied element-wise with input gate $i_t$.
- The two products summed to form new cell state $C_t$.
- The new cell state passed through $\tanh$ and multiplied element-wise with output gate $o_t$ to produce the new hidden state $h_t$.

**Gate Equations List (3 marks)**

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) \quad \text{(forget gate)}
$$

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) \quad \text{(input gate)}
$$

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C) \quad \text{(candidate)}
$$

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \quad \text{(cell state update)}
$$

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) \quad \text{(output gate)}
$$

$$
h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t) \quad \text{(hidden state output)}
$$

**Purpose of Each Gate (1 mark)**
- Forget gate: controls retention of past cell state.
- Input gate: controls addition of new information.
- Output gate: controls exposure of cell state to next layer/time.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**GRU Architecture (3 marks)**
GRU has only two gates and does not maintain a separate cell state. Inputs $x_t$ and $h_{t-1}$ are concatenated and fed into:
- An **update gate** $z_t$ (sigmoid).
- A **reset gate** $r_t$ (sigmoid).
- The reset gate modulates $h_{t-1}$ before forming the candidate $\tilde{h}_t$ (tanh).
- The final hidden state is a **convex combination** of $h_{t-1}$ and $\tilde{h}_t$, weighted by $z_t$.

**GRU Equations (3 marks)**

$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z) \quad \text{(update gate)}
$$

$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r) \quad \text{(reset gate)}
$$

$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b) \quad \text{(candidate)}
$$

$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t \quad \text{(hidden state)}
$$

**Why GRU is Lighter (1 mark)**
GRU has **3 weight matrices** ($W_z, W_r, W$) compared to LSTM's **4 weight matrices** ($W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$). It also has no separate cell state. This reduces parameter count by approximately **25%**, making training faster and inference cheaper, especially for smaller datasets or edge deployment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write $\odot$ as matrix multiplication. KTU examiners deduct marks for this. Always state: "$\odot$ denotes **Hadamard (element-wise) product**".
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that BPTT **sums** gradients across all time steps because the weights are **shared** across the unrolled graph. The total gradient is $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial W}$, not just the last time step.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the LSTM cell state $C_t$ with the hidden state $h_t$. They are **two different vectors**. $C_t$ is the long-term memory, $h_t$ is the output exposed to the next layer.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing LSTM cell state as $C_t = \sigma(W \cdot [\cdot])$ — the cell state update has **no** activation function, only the candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ uses $\tanh$ and the gates use $\sigma$. This is a very common and **heavily penalized** error.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** For Part B derivations, students often skip the chain rule step showing $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{k}} = \prod \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$. Examiners award marks for **explicitly** writing the product of Jacobians.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BPTT** is backpropagation applied to the **unrolled** version of an RNN; it sums gradients across all time steps because weights are shared.
- The **vanishing gradient** problem occurs when $\lambda_{\max} < 1$, causing gradients to shrink exponentially with sequence length.
- The **exploding gradient** problem occurs when $\lambda_{\max} > 1$, causing gradients to grow exponentially; the standard fix is **gradient clipping** (rescale gradient when its norm exceeds a threshold).
- **LSTM** uses **three gates** (forget, input, output) and a **separate cell state** with an **additive update** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$.
- **GRU** uses **two gates** (update, reset) and **merges** the cell and hidden states with a **linear interpolation** $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$.
- The $\odot$ symbol means **element-wise (Hadamard) product**, not matrix multiplication.
- Sigmoid $\sigma$ squashes to $[0, 1]$ (used in gates); $\tanh$ squashes to $[-1, 1]$ (used in candidate and cell state exposure).
- LSTM is more **expressive but slower**; GRU is **faster and lighter** but slightly less expressive.
- The **constant error carousel** (additive update of cell/hidden state) is the mathematical reason gated RNNs solve vanishing gradients.
- BPTT is **computationally expensive** for very long sequences — alternatives include **Truncated BPTT** (limit backprop to a window of $k$ steps) and modern **Transformer** architectures with self-attention.
- Practical remedies for vanishing: gated architectures (LSTM/GRU), better initialization (orthogonal $W_{hh}$), layer normalization, residual connections.
- Practical remedies for exploding: **gradient clipping** (most common), penalty on large weights, careful initialization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Generative Models, GANs - Adversarial Training, Discriminator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET865 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Generative Models, GANs - Adversarial Training, Discriminator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Generative Models

A **Generative Model** is a class of machine learning models that learn the underlying probability distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ of a training dataset so that it can produce **new, synthetic data samples** that resemble the original data. Unlike discriminative models, which model the conditional probability $p(y \mid x)$ for classification, generative models attempt to capture how the data is generated in the first place.

Mathematically, given a dataset $X = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(m)}\}$ sampled from an unknown distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$, a generative model aims to learn a parametric distribution $p_{\theta}(x)$ that approximates $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ as closely as possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Generative models are unsupervised (or self-supervised) learning frameworks. They form the backbone of modern AI systems used in image synthesis, text-to-image conversion, drug discovery, and audio generation.

### Common Families of Generative Models

- **Autoregressive Models** – PixelCNN, WaveNet (model joint distribution as product of conditionals).
- **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)** – Learn a latent probabilistic encoder-decoder.
- **Flow-Based Models** – RealNVP, Glow (use invertible transformations).
- **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** – Learn via a two-player adversarial game.
- **Diffusion Models** – DDPM, Stable Diffusion (learn to reverse a noise process).
- **Energy-Based Models (EBMs)** – Assign an unnormalized probability via an energy function.

## 1.2 Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)

A **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** is a deep learning framework introduced by **Ian Goodfellow and colleagues in 2014** that learns to generate realistic synthetic data by pitting two neural networks against each other in a **minimax game**.

The two networks are:

| Network | Role | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Generator** ($G$) | Maps random noise $z \sim p_z(z)$ to a fake data sample $G(z)$ that mimics real data. | $G$ |
| **Discriminator** ($D$) | A binary classifier that distinguishes real samples from generated (fake) samples. | $D$ |

## 1.3 The Discriminator (D)

The **Discriminator** $D(x; \theta_d)$ is a neural network (typically a deep CNN for image data) that outputs the **probability** that its input $x$ is a real sample drawn from the true data distribution $p_{\text{data}}(x)$, rather than a fake sample produced by the generator.

$$D(x) = P(x \text{ is real} \mid x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The discriminator is essentially a **deep binary classifier** with a sigmoid output, trained using standard supervised learning techniques on two classes: **real (label 1)** and **fake (label 0)**.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Forger and the Art Detective

Imagine a **counterfeit currency forger** (Generator) trying to produce fake banknotes, and a **police detective** (Discriminator) trying to identify which notes are fake.

- Initially, the forger produces poor-quality notes — the detective easily catches them.
- As the forger improves, the detective must sharpen his detection skills to keep up.
- This **adversarial competition** continues until the forger's notes are so realistic that the detective can only guess randomly (50/50 accuracy).

In this analogy:
- **Counterfeit notes** $\longleftrightarrow$ Generated samples $G(z)$
- **Genuine notes** $\longleftrightarrow$ Real samples $x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)$
- **Detective's verdict** $\longleftrightarrow$ Discriminator output $D(x)$
- **50/50 accuracy** $\longleftrightarrow$ Nash equilibrium where $p_g(x) = p_{\text{data}}(x)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The GAN Adversarial Game — Formal Setup

Let us define the key distributions and players:

- $p_z(z)$: Prior noise distribution, typically $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ or $z \sim \mathcal{U}(-1, 1)$.
- $p_{\text{data}}(x)$: True (unknown) data distribution.
- $p_g(x)$: Implicit distribution induced by the generator, where $x = G(z), \; z \sim p_z$.
- $G(z; \theta_g)$: Generator network with parameters $\theta_g$.
- $D(x; \theta_d)$: Discriminator network with parameters $\theta_d$, with output in $[0,1]$.

The two networks play a **two-player zero-sum minimax game** with the value function $V(D, G)$.

## 2.2 Adversarial Training — Step-by-Step Logic

The training procedure alternates between two phases per iteration:

### Phase 1 — Train the Discriminator (freeze $G$)
1. Sample a mini-batch of $m$ real samples $\{x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(m)}\}$ from the training data.
2. Sample a mini-batch of $m$ noise vectors $\{z^{(1)}, \ldots, z^{(m)}\}$ from $p_z(z)$.
3. Generate fake samples: $x_{\text{fake}}^{(i)} = G(z^{(i)})$.
4. Train $D$ to output **1** for real and **0** for fake (standard binary cross-entropy with two classes).
5. **Discriminator update rule** (gradient ascent on $V(D, G)$):
$$\nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ \log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))) \right]$$

### Phase 2 — Train the Generator (freeze $D$)
1. Sample a fresh mini-batch of $m$ noise vectors from $p_z(z)$.
2. Train $G$ to **fool** the discriminator — i.e., to maximize $\log D(G(z))$ (or equivalently minimize $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$).
3. **Generator update rule** (gradient descent on $V(D, G)$):
$$\nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip (Goodfellow et al., 2014):** Early in training, $D$ can easily reject fake samples, causing $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ to saturate (gradient ≈ 0). In practice, $G$ is trained to **maximize** $\log D(G(z))$ instead, which provides stronger gradients early on.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Value Function (Minimax Objective)** | $\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Core GAN training objective |
| 2 | **Discriminator Output** | $D(x) \in [0, 1]$ — probability of $x$ being real | Binary classification output |
| 3 | **Optimal Discriminator (analytical form)** | $D^*(x) = \dfrac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$ | Theoretical reference for $D$ at equilibrium |
| 4 | **Discriminator Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy form)** | $\mathcal{L}_D = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i} \left[ \log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))) \right]$ | Practical loss for training $D$ |
| 5 | **Generator Loss (original form)** | $\mathcal{L}_G = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i} \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))$ | Minimized by $G$ |
| 6 | **Generator Loss (non-saturating heuristic)** | $\mathcal{L}_G^{\text{NS}} = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i} \log D(G(z^{(i)}))$ | Used in practice to avoid saturation |
| 7 | **Value at Global Optimum** | $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4$ | Convergence diagnostic |
| 8 | **Jensen-Shannon Divergence form** | $V(D^*, G) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\Vert\, p_g) - \log 4$ | Distance minimized by $G$ |
| 9 | **Generator Induced Distribution** | $p_g(x) = p_{\text{data}}(x)$ at convergence | Equilibrium condition |
| 10 | **Generator Mapping** | $x_{\text{fake}} = G(z), \; z \sim p_z$ | Latent-to-data mapping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Ranges:**
> - $D(x) \in [0, 1]$ (sigmoid output, dimensionless probability)
> - $V(D, G) \in (-\infty, 0]$ (logarithmic, dimensionless)
> - Loss values are in **nats** (natural log) by convention; convert to bits by dividing by $\ln 2$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of GANs

- **Computer Vision:** Image super-resolution (SRGAN), image inpainting, style transfer (CycleGAN).
- **Medical Imaging:** Synthesizing training data for rare disease classification.
- **Audio & Speech:** Voice cloning, music generation (WaveGAN).
- **Industry & Design:** Generating product prototypes, architectural layouts, fashion design.
- **Data Augmentation:** Creating synthetic samples to balance skewed datasets.
- **Scientific Discovery:** Simulating particle physics events, drug molecule generation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Discriminator $D^*(x)$

For a fixed generator $G$, we want to find the discriminator $D$ that **maximizes** the value function. Recall the GAN value function:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz$$

**Step 1 — Change of variable.**
Since $x = G(z)$ and $G$ is a deterministic mapping, we can substitute the noise integral over $z$ into an integral over the generator-induced distribution $p_g(x)$:

$$\int_z p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz = \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Combine integrals.**
The objective becomes a single integral over the data space $x$:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx$$

**Step 3 — Pointwise optimization.**
For any fixed $x$, the integrand is a function of $D(x)$ only. To find the optimal $D(x)$, we take the derivative of the integrand with respect to $D(x)$ and set it to zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial D(x)} \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] = 0$$

Computing the derivative:

$$\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D(x)$.**
Cross-multiplying:

$$p_{\text{data}}(x) \cdot (1 - D(x)) = p_g(x) \cdot D(x)$$

Expanding:

$$p_{\text{data}}(x) - p_{\text{data}}(x) D(x) = p_g(x) D(x)$$

Collecting $D(x)$ terms:

$$p_{\text{data}}(x) = D(x) \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x) \right]$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify it is a maximum.**
The second derivative of the integrand with respect to $D(x)$ is:

$$\frac{\partial^2}{\partial D(x)^2} \left[ \cdot \right] = -\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)^2} - \frac{p_g(x)}{(1 - D(x))^2} < 0$$

Since the second derivative is **strictly negative** for any non-zero $D(x) \in (0,1)$, the critical point is indeed a **global maximum**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the GAN Value at the Optimum

**Step 1 — Substitute $D^*(x)$ back into the value function.**

$$V(D^*, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g} \left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Introduce an auxiliary mixed distribution $p_m$.**
Define $p_m(x) = \frac{1}{2} p_{\text{data}}(x) + \frac{1}{2} p_g(x)$. Then we can rewrite the expression. First, add and subtract $\log 2$ inside each expectation:

$$V(D^*, G) = \mathbb{E}_{p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{2 p_m(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{p_g} \left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{2 p_m(x)} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Separate the constant terms.**

$$V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + \mathbb{E}_{p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_m(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{p_g} \left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_m(x)} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the KL divergences.**

Recall the Kullback–Leibler divergence:
$$\text{KL}(P \,\Vert\, Q) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P} \left[ \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right]$$

Therefore, the two expectations are exactly the KL divergences of $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_g$ with respect to the mixture $p_m$:

$$V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + \text{KL}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\Big\Vert\, p_m\right) + \text{KL}\!\left(p_g \,\Big\Vert\, p_m\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Jensen–Shannon Divergence.**

By definition, the **Jensen–Shannon Divergence** is:

$$\text{JSD}(P \,\Vert\, Q) = \frac{1}{2} \text{KL}(P \,\Vert\, M) + \frac{1}{2} \text{KL}(Q \,\Vert\, M), \quad M = \tfrac{1}{2}(P+Q)$$

Multiplying our expression by 2 and dividing by 2:

$$V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\Vert\, p_g)$$

$$\boxed{V(D^*, G) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\Vert\, p_g) - \log 4}$$

**Step 6 — The global minimum of the value function.**

Since JSD $\geq 0$ with equality **iff** $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, the minimum value of $V(D^*, G)$ is achieved exactly when the two distributions are equal, and equals:

$$V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4 \approx -1.3863$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When the generator perfectly mimics the data distribution, the discriminator can do no better than random guessing ($D^*(x) = 0.5$ everywhere), and the value function settles at $-\log 4$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Discriminator Behaviour Across Training

| Training Stage | $p_g$ vs $p_{\text{data}}$ | $D(x)$ behaviour | Value of $V(D^*, G)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial (G weak)** | $p_g \ll p_{\text{data}}$ (modes separated) | $D(x) \approx 1$ on real, $\approx 0$ on fake | $V \approx 0$ (since $\log(1) = 0$, $\log(0) = -\infty$ but integral bounded) |
| **Intermediate** | Distributions partially overlap | $D$ shows soft transitions between 0 and 1 | $V < 0$ (JSD $> 0$) |
| **Converged (Nash Eq.)** | $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$ | $D(x) = 0.5$ everywhere (random guess) | $V = -\log 4 \approx -1.386$ |
| **Mode Collapse** | $p_g$ covers only a few modes of $p_{\text{data}}$ | $D$ easily distinguishes modes | $V$ plateaus; JSD remains large |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Nash Equilibrium?** A pure minimax optimum is achieved when neither player can improve by unilaterally changing their strategy. In GANs, this means $D$ cannot distinguish $p_g$ from $p_{\text{data}}$ and $G$ cannot improve further. The minimax theorem guarantees such an equilibrium exists under convexity, but in practice for non-convex deep networks, we only approximate it.

## 3.4 Generator Loss Gradient Insight (Non-Saturating Heuristic)

The original minimax formulation gives $G$ a weak gradient near $D(G(z)) = 0$ because:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_g} \log(1 - D(G(z))) = \frac{1}{1 - D(G(z))} \cdot \frac{\partial D}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{\partial G}{\partial \theta_g}$$

When $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ (early training), the prefactor $\frac{1}{1 - 0} = 1$ is small compared to the alternative:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_g} \log D(G(z)) = \frac{1}{D(G(z))} \cdot \frac{\partial D}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{\partial G}{\partial \theta_g}$$

When $D(G(z)) \approx 0$, this prefactor $\frac{1}{D(G(z))} \to \infty$, providing **strong gradients** that push $G$ to produce more realistic samples.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GAN High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z["Noise Vector z ~ pz"] -->|"Sample z"| GEN["Generator G"]
    REAL["Real Data x ~ pdata"] -->|"Sample x"| MIX["Combine into Batch"]
    GEN -->|"Fake sample G(z)"| MIX
    MIX -->|"Forward Pass"| DISC["Discriminator D"]
    DISC -->|"Probability"| OUT["Output: D(x) in 0 to 1"]
    OUT -->|"Compute Loss"| LOSS["BCE Loss"]
    LOSS -->|"Backprop"| DOPT["Update D params"]
    LOSS -->|"Backprop"| GOPT["Update G params"]
```

## 4.2 Discriminator Internal Architecture (Typical for Images)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INP["Input Image 3x64x64"] --> C1["Conv2D + LeakyReLU"]
    C1 --> C2["Conv2D + LeakyReLU + Dropout"]
    C2 --> C3["Conv2D + LeakyReLU + Dropout"]
    C3 --> F["Flatten"]
    F --> LIN["Linear Layer"]
    LIN --> SIG["Sigmoid Activation"]
    SIG --> P["Probability D(x)"]
```

## 4.3 Adversarial Training Loop Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["Start Training Epoch"]) --> SAMPLE_R["Sample real batch xr from pdata"]
    SAMPLE_R --> SAMPLE_Z["Sample noise batch zr from pz"]
    SAMPLE_Z --> GEN_F["Generate fake batch Gzr"]
    GEN_F --> D_FWD["Forward pass through D for both batches"]
    D_FWD --> D_LOSS["Compute Discriminator BCE Loss"]
    D_LOSS --> D_BACK["Backprop and update D parameters"]
    D_BACK --> SAMPLE_Z2["Sample fresh noise batch zg from pz"]
    SAMPLE_Z2 --> G_FWD["Forward G then D on Gzg"]
    G_FWD --> G_LOSS["Compute Generator Loss non-saturating"]
    G_LOSS --> G_BACK["Backprop and update G parameters"]
    G_BACK --> CHECK["Convergence check"]
    CHECK -->|"Not converged"| SAMPLE_R
    CHECK -->|"Converged D x approx 0.5"| STOP(["Stop Training"])
```

## 4.4 Nash Equilibrium Convergence Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph E1["Initial Stage"]
        A1["pg far from pdata"] --> A2["D easily separates real and fake"]
    end
    subgraph E2["Mid Training"]
        B1["pg moving toward pdata"] --> B2["D has harder time separating"]
    end
    subgraph E3["Nash Equilibrium"]
        C1["pg equals pdata"] --> C2["D x equals 0.5 everywhere"]
    end
    E1 -->|"Adversarial updates"| E2
    E2 -->|"Adversarial updates"| E3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows represent data flow during the **forward pass**, while the implicit gradient arrows (during backpropagation) flow in the **opposite direction**. In the training loop diagram, observe that $D$ is updated **first** with both real and fake samples (so it has a meaningful gradient signal), and only **then** is $G$ updated using the freshly-updated $D$'s feedback.

## 4.5 Discriminator Decision Boundary Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DB["Discriminator Decision Boundary in Feature Space"]
        REGION_R["Region A: D x greater than 0.5 — Real data manifold"]
        REGION_F["Region B: D x less than 0.5 — Fake generated manifold"]
        BOUND["Decision Boundary: D x equals 0.5 — Optimal classifier"]
    end
    REGION_R --- BOUND
    BOUND --- REGION_F
```

At the **Nash equilibrium**, the decision boundary collapses because the real and fake manifolds perfectly overlap, forcing $D(x) = 0.5$ everywhere.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**Define a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN). List its two main components and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN), introduced by Goodfellow et al. (2014), is a deep learning framework composed of two neural networks trained in opposition to perform unsupervised learning of complex data distributions.

The two main components are:

1. **Generator ($G$):** A neural network that takes a random noise vector $z$ sampled from a prior distribution $p_z(z)$ (e.g., Gaussian) and maps it to a synthetic data sample $G(z)$ intended to resemble real data drawn from $p_{\text{data}}(x)$.

2. **Discriminator ($D$):** A neural network (typically a binary classifier) that receives either a real sample $x$ or a generated sample $G(z)$ and outputs the probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$ that the input is real.

**Role of the Discriminator:** To distinguish real samples from fake ones, thereby providing a learning signal that pushes the generator to produce increasingly realistic outputs.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**Write the minimax value function for a GAN. Explain the significance of $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4$.**

**Model Answer:**

The minimax value function of a GAN is:

$$\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Significance of $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4$:**

- This is the **global optimum** value of the GAN objective.
- It is achieved when $p_g(x) = p_{\text{data}}(x)$, meaning the generator's distribution perfectly matches the true data distribution.
- At this point, the optimal discriminator outputs $D^*(x) = 0.5$ for every $x$, indicating it cannot distinguish real from fake.
- The value $-\log 4 \approx -1.386$ corresponds to a Jensen–Shannon Divergence of zero between the two distributions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the optimal discriminator $D^*(x)$ analytically for a fixed generator $G$, starting from the GAN value function. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the complete adversarial training algorithm for GANs, including the alternating optimization of $D$ and $G$. Discuss the role of the non-saturating heuristic for the generator loss. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** $V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$

**Step 1:** Change variable $z \to x = G(z)$. The second expectation becomes an integral over the induced distribution $p_g(x)$:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_x p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \, dx$$

**Step 2:** Combine into a single integral:

$$V(D, G) = \int_x \left[ p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D(x) + p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \right] dx$$

**Step 3:** For any $x$, take the derivative of the integrand $f(D) = p_{\text{data}}(x) \log D + p_g(x) \log(1 - D)$ with respect to $D$:

$$\frac{df}{dD} = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solving:

$$p_{\text{data}}(x) (1 - D(x)) = p_g(x) D(x)$$
$$D(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

**Step 5:** Second derivative check:
$$\frac{d^2 f}{dD^2} = -\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)^2} - \frac{p_g(x)}{(1 - D(x))^2} < 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (maximum)}$$

$$\boxed{D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Adversarial Training Algorithm (Goodfellow et al., 2014):**

For each training iteration:
1. **Train the Discriminator for $k$ steps** (typically $k = 1$):
   - Sample mini-batch of $m$ noise samples $\{z^{(1)}, \ldots, z^{(m)}\}$ from $p_z(z)$.
   - Sample mini-batch of $m$ real samples $\{x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(m)}\}$ from $p_{\text{data}}$.
   - Update $D$ by **ascending** the stochastic gradient:
$$\nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ \log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)}))) \right]$$

2. **Train the Generator for 1 step**:
   - Sample mini-batch of $m$ noise samples from $p_z(z)$.
   - Update $G$ by **descending** the stochastic gradient (non-saturating form):
$$\nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ -\log D(G(z^{(i)})) \right]$$

3. Repeat for a fixed number of epochs or until convergence.

**Role of the Non-Saturating Heuristic:**

The original minimax objective minimizes $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$. Early in training, when $G$ is weak, $D$ easily rejects fakes, making $D(G(z)) \approx 0$. The gradient of $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ with respect to $G$'s parameters then becomes very small (saturates), slowing learning.

By instead **maximizing** $\log D(G(z))$ (i.e., minimizing $-\log D(G(z))$), the gradient becomes large when $D(G(z)) \approx 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_g} \log D(G(z)) = \frac{1}{D(G(z))} \cdot \frac{\partial D}{\partial G} \cdot \frac{\partial G}{\partial \theta_g}$$

This provides strong early-training signal, leading to faster and more stable convergence. The two formulations share the same fixed points but differ in gradient dynamics.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the Nash Equilibrium in the context of GAN training. Why is reaching it difficult in practice? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the optimal discriminator, derive the expression showing that the generator's objective is equivalent to minimizing the Jensen–Shannon Divergence between $p_{\text{data}}$ and $p_g$. Hence show that $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Nash Equilibrium in GANs:**

A Nash Equilibrium is a state in a multi-player game where no player can improve their outcome by unilaterally changing their strategy, assuming the other players' strategies remain fixed.

In GANs:
- The **Generator's equilibrium strategy** is one where $p_g(x) = p_{\text{data}}(x)$.
- The **Discriminator's equilibrium strategy** is one where $D(x) = 0.5$ for all $x$ (i.e., it can only randomly guess).

At this joint point $(D^*, G^*)$, neither network can reduce its loss by changing parameters alone, so neither has incentive to deviate — this is the Nash Equilibrium.

**Why is it hard to reach in practice?**

1. **Non-convexity of deep networks:** The minimax theorem guarantees equilibrium under convexity, but deep neural networks are highly non-convex.
2. **Alternating optimization, not joint:** We update $D$ and $G$ sequentially, not simultaneously, so the system may oscillate rather than converge.
3. **Mode collapse:** $G$ may produce samples from only a subset of $p_{\text{data}}$, trapping the system in a local equilibrium.
4. **Vanishing gradients:** If $D$ becomes too strong, $G$'s gradient vanishes; if $D$ is too weak, $G$ receives no useful signal.
5. **No explicit stopping criterion:** Equilibrium corresponds to $D(x) = 0.5$ everywhere, but in practice it is hard to verify this globally.
6. **Cyclic behaviour:** The loss of $D$ and $G$ can oscillate without converging, rather than settling.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Substitute $D^*(x)$ into the value function.**

$$V(D^*, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_g} \left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x)} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the mixture distribution.**
Define $p_m(x) = \frac{1}{2}(p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x))$.

Rewrite each term by introducing and subtracting $\log 2$:

$$V(D^*, G) = \mathbb{E}_{p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \log \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{2 p_m(x)} \right] + \mathbb{E}_{p_g} \left[ \log \frac{p_g(x)}{2 p_m(x)} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Separate constants and identify KL terms.**

$$V(D^*, G) = -\log 4 + \text{KL}(p_{\text{data}} \,\Vert\, p_m) + \text{KL}(p_g \,\Vert\, p_m)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of JSD.**

By definition, the Jensen–Shannon Divergence is:

$$\text{JSD}(P \,\Vert\, Q) = \frac{1}{2} \text{KL}(P \,\Vert\, M) + \frac{1}{2} \text{KL}(Q \,\Vert\, M)$$

Therefore:

$$V(D^*, G) = 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\Vert\, p_g) - \log 4$$

**Step 5 — Minimum value.**

Since JSD $\geq 0$ and equals 0 **iff** $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, the global minimum of $V(D^*, G)$ is:

$$V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4 \approx -1.3863$$

$$\boxed{V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not skip the change of variable** $z \to x$ in the derivation. Many students forget this, and examiners specifically look for the justification of the substitution.
> 2. **Mixing up maximizer vs minimizer:** Clearly state that $D$ maximizes $V$ and $G$ minimizes $V$. Mixing the two is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the second-derivative check** in $D^*$ derivation costs marks. The optimum must be verified as a maximum, not a minimum or saddle point.
> 4. **Units/Logs:** GAN losses use natural log ($\ln$, in nats). Do not write $\log_2$ or $\log_{10}$ unless explicitly stated.
> 5. **Confusing KL with JSD:** KL divergence is asymmetric and unbounded; JSD is symmetric and bounded by $\log 2$. The GAN objective uses **JSD** after substitution, not KL directly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Generative Models & GANs**

- ✅ **Generative Model Definition:** Learns $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ to synthesize new samples; unsupervised paradigm.
- ✅ **GAN Components:** Generator $G$ (noise $\to$ fake data) and Discriminator $D$ (binary classifier of real vs. fake).
- ✅ **Discriminator Output:** $D(x) \in [0, 1]$ via sigmoid; trained with binary cross-entropy.
- ✅ **Value Function:** $\min_G \max_D V = \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
- ✅ **Optimal Discriminator:** $D^*(x) = p_{\text{data}}(x) / (p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_g(x))$.
- ✅ **At Nash Equilibrium:** $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, $D^*(x) = 0.5$, and $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4$.
- ✅ **Connection to JSD:** Generator implicitly minimizes $2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{\text{data}} \,\Vert\, p_g) - \log 4$.
- ✅ **Non-Saturating Heuristic:** Replace $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ with $-\log D(G(z))$ for stronger early gradients.
- ✅ **Training is Alternating:** Update $D$ first (with both real and fake), then update $G$ using the fresh $D$.
- ✅ **Common Failure Modes:** Mode collapse, vanishing gradients, training instability, non-convergence to equilibrium.
- ✅ **Practical Hints:** Use LeakyReLU in $D$, BatchNorm in $G$, Adam optimizer with low learning rate ($\sim 2 \times 10^{-4}$), and label smoothing for stability.
- ✅ **GAN vs VAE vs Diffusion:** GANs use adversarial loss (implicit density); VAEs use reconstruction + KL (variational bound); Diffusion models learn to reverse a noise process step by step.
- ✅ **Discriminator is NOT a probability density estimator** — it is a binary classifier, and its output is **not** $p_{\text{data}}(x)$ directly.
- ✅ **Remember:** The minimax theorem's assumptions (convexity) do **not** hold for deep networks — equilibrium is only approximated in practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Generator (in Deep Learning)

### Formal Definition
A **Generator** is a differentiable neural network $G_\theta : \mathcal{Z} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$, parameterized by weights $\theta$, that maps samples from a simple prior latent distribution $p_z(z)$ (commonly a standard Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$) into samples from a learned data distribution $p_g(x)$ intended to approximate the true data distribution $p_{data}(x)$. The generator is the *synthesis half* of generative models, prominently used in **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**, **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)**, and **autoregressive Transformer decoders**.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Counterfeit Artist:** Imagine a counterfeiter (the Generator) trying to forge paintings in the style of Van Gogh. He starts with random noise (the latent vector $z$) and produces canvases. An art detective (the Discriminator) examines both real Van Goghs and the counterfeits, telling them apart. As the detective improves, the counterfeiter is forced to improve too. Eventually, the counterfeit's forgeries become indistinguishable from the originals — this adversarial dance is the essence of generative modeling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** A Generator's *only* job is **synthesis**, not classification. Its quality is judged not by an accuracy metric, but by how closely its output distribution matches $p_{data}(x)$, typically quantified via the **Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD)**, **Wasserstein distance**, or the **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)**.

### Generator Loss (Original GAN Formulation, Goodfellow et al., 2014)

$$\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

The generator minimizes the second term, attempting to push $D(G(z)) \rightarrow 1$.

---

## 1.2 Introduction to Transformer Models — Architecture

### Formal Definition
The **Transformer** is a sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) deep learning architecture introduced in the landmark 2017 paper *"Attention Is All You Need"* by Vaswani et al. (Google Brain/Research). It **entirely eliminates recurrence and convolutions**, relying exclusively on a mechanism called **Self-Attention** to model global dependencies between all positions in a sequence. The architecture consists of a symmetric **Encoder stack** and **Decoder stack**, each built from $N$ identical layers containing *Multi-Head Attention*, *Position-wise Feed-Forward Networks*, *Residual Connections*, and *Layer Normalization*.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Conference Room:** Imagine reading a paragraph. A recurrent network (RNN/LSTM) reads it **one word at a time**, remembering what came before — like a tired translator. A **Transformer** instead places every word in a conference room, lets every word "look at" every other word simultaneously, and asks each word: *"How much should I pay attention to that other word?"* The result is **parallelism, long-range context capture, and faster training**. This is *self-attention*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Transformers Matter:** They form the backbone of **BERT, GPT, T5, ViT (Vision Transformer), Stable Diffusion's UNet+Transformer blocks**, and most modern Large Language Models (LLMs). Understanding the architecture is **non-negotiable** for any Deep Learning engineer.

### Core Architectural Components (One-Liners)

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Input Embedding** | Converts tokens $\rightarrow$ dense vectors of dimension $d_{model}$ |
| **Positional Encoding** | Injects sequence-order information (since attention is permutation-invariant) |
| **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | Computes pairwise token relevance scores |
| **Multi-Head Attention** | Runs attention in $h$ parallel subspaces for richer representations |
| **Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** | Position-wise non-linear transformation: $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ |
| **Residual Connection + LayerNorm** | Stabilizes deep stacks: $\text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$ |
| **Encoder Stack** | $N=6$ identical layers, processes input bidirectionally |
| **Decoder Stack** | $N=6$ identical layers, generates output autoregressively with **masked** self-attention |
| **Linear + Softmax Head** | Projects decoder outputs to vocabulary probabilities |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Hyperparameters (Base Model):** $N=6$, $d_{model}=512$, $h=8$ attention heads, $d_{ff}=2048$, $d_k = d_v = 64$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How the Generator Works — Operational Logic

1. **Sample noise** $z \sim p_z(z)$ from a latent prior (typically $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$).
2. **Forward pass** through $G_\theta$: $\tilde{x} = G_\theta(z)$.
3. **Pass** $\tilde{x}$ to the Discriminator $D_\phi$ to obtain $D(\tilde{x}) \in [0,1]$.
4. **Update** $\theta$ via backprop to **maximize** $D(G(z))$ (non-saturating GAN loss).
5. **Repeat** until $p_g \approx p_{data}$.

### Why the Generator "Works" — The Math

- At Nash equilibrium ($p_g = p_{data}$), the optimal discriminator is $D^*(x) = \frac{1}{2}$ for all $x$.
- The global minimum of the value function corresponds to $C(G) = -\log 4$, with $p_g = p_{data}$.
- Generator gradients vanish when $D$ is too strong (saturating $\log(1-D(G(z)))$), hence the **non-saturating heuristic**: minimize $-\log D(G(z))$ instead.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Generator networks power image synthesis (StyleGAN, BigGAN), text generation (GPT decoders), drug discovery (molecular generation), audio synthesis (WaveGAN), and 3D asset creation.

---

## 2.2 Transformer Self-Attention — Operational Logic

1. Each input embedding $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model}}$ is projected into three vectors via learned matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$:
   - **Query** $q_i = x_i W^Q$
   - **Key**   $k_i = x_i W^K$
   - **Value** $v_i = x_i W^V$
2. **Compatibility score** between positions $i$ and $j$: $q_i \cdot k_j^T$.
3. **Scale** by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to keep gradients stable (prevents softmax saturation when $d_k$ is large).
4. **Softmax** over all keys $j$ to get attention weights $\alpha_{ij}$.
5. **Weighted sum** of value vectors: $\text{head}_i = \sum_j \alpha_{ij} v_j$.

### Why the $\sqrt{d_k}$ Scale? (Critical Intuition)

If $q, k$ components have variance $1$, then $q \cdot k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$ has variance $d_k$. For large $d_k$, the dot product magnitudes explode, pushing softmax into saturated regions (gradient $\approx 0$). Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ **normalizes variance back to 1**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the equations the examiner expects you to reproduce.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GAN Value Function | $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Min-max game between D and G |
| 2 | Optimal Discriminator | $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$ | At convergence $D^*=1/2$ |
| 3 | Generator Loss (Non-Sat.) | $\mathcal{L}_G = -\mathbb{E}_{z}[\log D(G(z))]$ | Avoids vanishing gradients |
| 4 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Core attention operation |
| 5 | Single Attention Head | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$ | One of $h$ parallel heads |
| 6 | Multi-Head Attention | $\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)W^O$ | $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{hd_v \times d_{model}}$ |
| 7 | Position-wise FFN | $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$ | Applied independently per position |
| 8 | Positional Encoding (sin) | $PE_{(pos,2i)} = \sin\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$ | Even indices |
| 9 | Positional Encoding (cos) | $PE_{(pos,2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$ | Odd indices |
| 10 | Residual + LayerNorm | $\text{output} = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$ | Stabilizes $N$-stack training |
| 11 | Total Params (Base) | $\approx 65\text{M}$ for $N=6$, $d_{model}=512$ | Trainable weights in encoder+decoder |
| 12 | Complexity per Layer | $O(n^2 \cdot d + n \cdot d^2)$ | $n$ = sequence length, $d$ = $d_{model}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Transformer Architecture:** Forms the foundation of modern NLP (BERT/GPT), computer vision (ViT, Swin), speech (Whisper), protein folding (AlphaFold), and multimodal models (CLIP, Flamingo).

---

## 2.4 Multi-Head Attention vs. Single-Head — Why $h > 1$?

> [!TIP]
> Single-head attention forces one attention distribution over the sequence. Multi-head attention projects queries/keys/values into $h$ different learned subspaces, allowing the model to **jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions**. Empirically, even with $h=8$ and $d_k = d_{model}/8 = 64$, total compute is comparable to single-head with full $d_{model}$, but representation capacity is much richer.
