# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why We Need $\sqrt{d_k}$ in Scaled Dot-Product Attention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favorite 7-mark KTU question**. We derive the scaling factor from first principles using variance analysis.

### Step 1 — Setup Assumptions

Assume the components of query $q$ and key $k$ are **independent random variables** with **zero mean** and **unit variance**:

$$\mathbb{E}[q_i] = 0, \quad \mathbb{E}[k_i] = 0, \quad \text{Var}(q_i) = 1, \quad \text{Var}(k_i) = 1$$

### Step 2 — Compute Variance of a Single Dot Product Term

The dot product is:

$$s = q \cdot k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$$

Variance of the product $q_i k_i$ (independent zero-mean):

$$\text{Var}(q_i k_i) = \mathbb{E}[q_i^2 k_i^2] - (\mathbb{E}[q_i k_i])^2 = \mathbb{E}[q_i^2]\mathbb{E}[k_i^2] - 0 = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$$

### Step 3 — Sum Variances Over $d_k$ Dimensions

Since the $d_k$ terms are independent:

$$\text{Var}(s) = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i) = d_k$$

### Step 4 — Standard Deviation of the Dot Product

$$\sigma_s = \sqrt{\text{Var}(s)} = \sqrt{d_k}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Scaling Correction

To restore unit variance, divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$:

$$\text{Scaled Score} = \frac{q \cdot k}{\sqrt{d_k}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{q \cdot k}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) = \frac{d_k}{d_k} = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the pre-softmax logits on a numerically stable scale, preventing the softmax from saturating (producing near-one-hot outputs) and ensuring healthy gradient flow.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Positional Encoding Frequencies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Another **favorite 7-mark KTU question**. We derive why sinusoids of varying frequencies are used.

### Step 1 — The Requirement

The encoding must:
- Be **deterministic** (no learned parameters).
- **Uniquely identify** each position.
- Allow the model to **generalize to longer sequences** than those seen in training.
- Be **translation-invariant** in relative position (the offset $k$ between two positions should be easily learnable).

### Step 2 — Sinusoidal Basis Choice

For dimension index $i$ and position $pos$:

$$PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos \cdot \omega_i), \quad PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos(pos \cdot \omega_i)$$

where the frequency is:

$$\omega_i = \frac{1}{10000^{2i / d_{model}}}$$

### Step 3 — Why This Frequency Schedule?

- For $i=0$: $\omega_0 = 1/10000^0 = 1$ → high frequency (oscillates every $2\pi$).
- For $i = d_{model}/2 - 1$: $\omega_i = 1/10000$ → very low frequency (long wavelength).
- This gives the model a **spectrum of wavelengths** — fine-grained local positions and coarse-grained global positions are both captured.

### Step 4 — Proof of Relative-Position Learnability

Using the trigonometric identity:

$$PE_{(pos+k, 2i)} = \sin((pos+k)\omega_i) = \sin(pos\omega_i)\cos(k\omega_i) + \cos(pos\omega_i)\sin(k\omega_i)$$

$$PE_{(pos+k, 2i+1)} = \cos((pos+k)\omega_i) = \cos(pos\omega_i)\cos(k\omega_i) - \sin(pos\omega_i)\sin(k\omega_i)$$

This is a **linear transformation** of $PE_{(pos)}$ with coefficients depending only on the offset $k$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} PE_{(pos+k,2i)} \\ PE_{(pos+k,2i+1)} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(k\omega_i) & \sin(k\omega_i) \\ -\sin(k\omega_i) & \cos(k\omega_i) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} PE_{(pos,2i)} \\ PE_{(pos,2i+1)} \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The encoding at position $pos+k$ can be expressed as a **rotation matrix** applied to the encoding at $pos$, with rotation angle depending only on $k$. A linear attention layer can therefore easily learn to attend to relative offsets.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Multi-Head Attention (Small Toy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This kind of small numerical problem tests your grasp of dimensions and operations.

**Setup:** $d_{model}=4$, $h=2$ heads, so $d_k = d_v = 2$. Sequence length $n=3$. Input matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$.

Let:
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad W^Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad W^K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad W^V = I_4$$

### Step 1 — Compute Q, K, V

$$Q = X W^Q = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad K = X W^K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 4 & 0 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = X W^V = X$$

### Step 2 — Compute $QK^\top$ and Scale

$$QK^\top = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 & 4 \\ 16 & 0 & 8 \\ 4 & 8 & 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

With $d_k = 2$, scale factor $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$:

$$\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{2}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2.83 & 2.83 \\ 11.31 & 0 & 5.66 \\ 2.83 & 5.66 & 5.66 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Row-wise Softmax

Row 1: $e^0 + e^{2.83} + e^{2.83} = 1 + 17.0 + 17.0 = 35.0$ → softmax $\approx [0.029, 0.486, 0.486]$

Row 2: $e^{11.31} + e^0 + e^{5.66} = 81{,}000 + 1 + 287 \approx 81{,}288$ → softmax $\approx [1.0, 0, 0]$ (numerically saturated!)

Row 3: $e^{2.83} + e^{5.66} + e^{5.66} = 17 + 287 + 287 = 591$ → softmax $\approx [0.029, 0.486, 0.486]$

### Step 4 — Weighted Sum with V

$$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{2}}\right) V$$

Row 2 of the attention output is essentially $V$'s row 2 (because the softmax saturated — this is **exactly the pathology** the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling partially mitigates; in practice, much larger $d_k$ makes this even worse without scaling).

> [!WARNING]
> **Lesson:** When $d_k$ is small, the saturation is mild. When $d_k$ is large (e.g., 64 in the base model), the **unscaled** logits can reach magnitudes of $\sim 20$–$30$, making softmax near one-hot and gradients vanish. This is why the paper explicitly scales by $\sqrt{d_k}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why Residual Connections Are Mandatory in Deep Transformers

Without residuals, the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to an early layer's input $x^{(1)}$ in a stack of $N$ layers is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x^{(1)}} = \prod_{\ell=1}^{N} \frac{\partial \text{Sublayer}^{(\ell)}}{\partial x^{(\ell)}}$$

This is a **product of Jacobians**, which can collapse to zero (vanishing) or explode. With residual connections:

$$x^{(\ell+1)} = x^{(\ell)} + \text{Sublayer}^{(\ell)}(x^{(\ell)})$$

$$\frac{\partial x^{(\ell+1)}}{\partial x^{(\ell)}} = I + \frac{\partial \text{Sublayer}^{(\ell)}}{\partial x^{(\ell)}}$$

At initialization, $\frac{\partial \text{Sublayer}}{\partial x} \approx 0$, so the Jacobian is approximately the **identity** — gradients flow unimpeded. Combined with **LayerNorm**, training is stabilized for stacks of dozens or hundreds of layers (GPT-3 uses 96 layers).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generator–Discriminator Adversarial Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zIn["Latent Noise z ~ N 0 I"] --> genG["Generator G theta"]
    genG --> fakeX["Fake Sample G of z"]
    fakeX --> discD["Discriminator D phi"]
    realX["Real Sample x ~ p_data"] --> discD
    discD --> probOut["Probability D of x is real"]
    probOut --> lossComp["Adversarial Loss V D G"]
    lossComp -->|Update G| genG
    lossComp -->|Update D| discD
```

## 4.2 Transformer — Full Encoder–Decoder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputChain
        inTok["Input Tokens x1 ... xn"] --> inEmb["Input Embedding"]
        inEmb --> inPos["Add Positional Encoding"]
    end

    subgraph EncoderStack["Encoder Stack x N=6"]
        encIn["Encoder Input"] --> mhaEnc["Multi-Head Self-Attention"]
        mhaEnc --> addNorm1["Add and LayerNorm 1"]
        addNorm1 --> ffnEnc["Feed Forward Network"]
        ffnEnc --> addNorm2["Add and LayerNorm 2"]
        addNorm2 --> encOut["Encoder Output K V memory"]
    end

    subgraph DecoderStack["Decoder Stack x N=6"]
        decIn["Decoder Input shifted right"] --> mhaDecMask["Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention"]
        mhaDecMask --> addNorm3["Add and LayerNorm 3"]
        addNorm3 --> mhaCross["Cross Attention Q from decoder K V from encoder"]
        mhaCross --> addNorm4["Add and LayerNorm 4"]
        addNorm4 --> ffnDec["Feed Forward Network"]
        ffnDec --> addNorm5["Add and LayerNorm 5"]
        addNorm5 --> decOut["Decoder Output"]
    end

    inPos --> encIn
    encOut --> mhaCross
    decOut --> linearHead["Linear Projection to Vocab"]
    linearHead --> softmaxHead["Softmax Probabilities"]
```

## 4.3 Scaled Dot-Product Attention — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Qmat["Q matrix n x dk"] --> mulQK["MatMul Q x K transposed"]
    Kmat["K matrix n x dk"] --> mulQK
    mulQK --> scaleStep["Scale by 1 over sqrt dk"]
    scaleStep --> maskStep["Optional Mask look ahead or padding"]
    maskStep --> softmaxStep["Softmax over key axis"]
    softmaxStep --> attnWeights["Attention Weights n x n"]
    Vmat["V matrix n x dv"] --> weightMul["MatMul Weights x V"]
    attnWeights --> weightMul
    weightMul --> outAttn["Output n x dv"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inX["Input X"] --> projQ["Linear to Q"]
    inX --> projK["Linear to K"]
    inX --> projV["Linear to V"]

    projQ --> splitH["Split into h heads"]
    projK --> splitH2["Split into h heads"]
    projV --> splitH3["Split into h heads"]

    splitH --> h1["Head 1 Scaled Dot Prod"]
    splitH2 --> h1
    splitH3 --> h1

    splitH --> h2["Head 2 Scaled Dot Prod"]
    splitH2 --> h2
    splitH3 --> h2

    splitH --> hN["Head h Scaled Dot Prod"]

    h1 --> concat["Concat all heads"]
    h2 --> concat
    hN --> concat
    concat --> projO["Linear Projection W_O"]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Generator network in the context of Generative Adversarial Networks. State the value function the Generator participates in.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Generator $G_\theta$ in a GAN is a differentiable neural network that maps samples $z \sim p_z(z)$ from a simple latent prior (e.g., $\mathcal{N}(0,I)$) to synthetic data samples $G(z)$ that mimic the true data distribution $p_{data}(x)$. The Generator is trained by playing an adversarial min-max game against a Discriminator $D_\phi$, defined by the value function:

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

The Generator minimizes the second term. (1 mark definition, 1 mark value function, 1 mark explanation of role)

---

### Q2. List the main components of a Transformer encoder layer. Why is Positional Encoding necessary?
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Transformer encoder layer contains:
1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** sublayer.
2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network** (FFN) sublayer.
3. **Residual Connection + Layer Normalization** around each sublayer.

(2 marks for components)
Positional Encoding is necessary because **self-attention is permutation-invariant** — without position information, the model cannot distinguish the sequence "dog bites man" from "man bites dog". Sinusoidal Positional Encodings inject order information into the input embeddings. (1 mark for justification)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor** when writing the attention formula — **deduct 2 marks**.
> - Confusing **Encoder** and **Decoder** cross-attention sources (Q comes from decoder, K and V come from encoder) is a common error — **deduct 1.5 marks**.
> - Failing to mention **masking** in decoder self-attention (causal mask to prevent attending to future tokens) is a frequent omission — **deduct 1 mark**.
> - Writing the GAN value function without specifying the **min-max roles** of G and D loses **1 mark**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Transformer Architecture
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of the Transformer model. Explain the role of each component in the encoder and decoder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula step-by-step, starting from the basic dot-product attention. Justify mathematically why scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ is necessary. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Architecture Diagram & Roles (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** A labelled diagram showing Input Embedding → Positional Encoding → $N$ Encoder layers (each with Multi-Head Self-Attention → Add&Norm → FFN → Add&Norm) → Encoder Output ($K$, $V$ memory). Decoder side: shifted-right output embedding → Positional Encoding → $N$ Decoder layers (each with Masked MHA → Add&Norm → Cross-Attention with encoder $K,V$ → Add&Norm → FFN → Add&Norm) → Linear → Softmax.

**Encoder Roles (2.5 marks):**
- **Input Embedding:** maps discrete tokens to $d_{model}$-dim vectors.
- **Positional Encoding:** adds sinusoidal position vectors so attention is order-aware.
- **Multi-Head Self-Attention:** allows every token to attend to every other token in the input.
- **FFN:** applies two linear layers with ReLU to introduce non-linearity, position-wise.
- **Add & LayerNorm:** stabilizes training of deep stacks.

**Decoder Roles (2.5 marks):**
- **Masked Self-Attention:** prevents attending to future tokens (autoregressive property).
- **Cross-Attention:** Q comes from decoder, K and V come from encoder output — this is how the decoder "reads" the input.
- **Linear + Softmax head:** projects decoder outputs to vocabulary probabilities.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Scaled Dot-Product Attention Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Start with basic dot-product attention. For a query $q$ and a set of keys $k_1, \ldots, k_n$:

$$s_i = q \cdot k_i = q^\top k_i$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply softmax to get attention weights:

$$\alpha_i = \frac{\exp(s_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp(s_j)}$$

**Step 3 (0.5 marks):** Compute output as weighted sum of values $v_i$:

$$\text{output} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i v_i$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Vectorized form with $Q, K, V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}(QK^\top) V$$

**Step 5 (2.5 marks):** Derive the scaling necessity. If $q_i, k_i$ are independent with zero mean and unit variance, then $\text{Var}(q \cdot k) = d_k$. Standard deviation is $\sqrt{d_k}$. For large $d_k$, dot products grow large in magnitude, pushing softmax into saturation (gradients $\approx 0$). Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ restores variance to 1:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

This is the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** used in Transformers. (1 mark for the final boxed formula)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Generator, Multi-Head Attention & Positional Encoding
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a Generator in a Generative Adversarial Network. Write the GAN objective function and explain each term. Why is the non-saturating loss preferred over the original generator loss? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Multi-Head Attention with a diagram. Derive the sinusoidal Positional Encoding formulas and show that they allow the model to represent relative positions via a linear transformation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Generator Working & Objective (7 marks)

**Working (2.5 marks):**
1. Sample a noise vector $z \sim p_z(z)$ from a simple prior (e.g., Gaussian).
2. Feed $z$ through the Generator network $G_\theta$ to produce a fake sample $\tilde{x} = G_\theta(z)$.
3. The Discriminator $D_\phi$ receives both real samples $x \sim p_{data}$ and fake samples $\tilde{x}$, outputting a probability $D(\cdot) \in [0,1]$.
4. $D$ is trained to maximize the probability of correctly classifying real vs. fake.
5. $G$ is trained to **fool** $D$, i.e., produce samples classified as real.

**GAN Objective Function (2.5 marks):**

$$\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)]}_{\text{Real samples classified as real}} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]}_{\text{Fake samples classified as fake}}$$

- The first term encourages $D(x) \to 1$ for real $x$.
- The second term encourages $D(G(z)) \to 0$ for fake $G(z)$.
- $G$ minimizes the second term (wants $D(G(z)) \to 1$).

**Why Non-Saturating Loss (2 marks):**
The original generator loss $\min_G \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ suffers from **vanishing gradients** when $D$ is well-trained (early in training), because in regions where $D(G(z)) \approx 0$, the function $\log(1-D(G(z)))$ is nearly flat (gradient $\approx 0$). The non-saturating heuristic flips the sign:

$$\mathcal{L}_G^{\text{NS}} = -\mathbb{E}_{z}[\log D(G(z))] = \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))] \text{ (minimized)}$$

Wait — more precisely: instead of minimizing $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$, we **maximize** $\log(D(G(z)))$. This reformulation provides **strong gradients** when $D$ is strong, since the gradient of $-\log(D(G(z)))$ is much larger near $D(G(z)) = 0$ than the gradient of $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$. This keeps generator learning fast even when the discriminator is winning decisively.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Multi-Head Attention & Positional Encoding (7 marks)

**Multi-Head Attention (3 marks):**
Multi-Head Attention projects the queries, keys, and values into $h$ different learned subspaces, computes scaled dot-product attention independently in each subspace, then concatenates and projects the results:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W^O$$

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$$

where $W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$, $W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$, $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_v}$, and $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{hd_v \times d_{model}}$. With $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$, total compute matches single-head attention, but the model can attend to information from $h$ different representation subspaces simultaneously — for example, one head might learn syntactic dependencies while another captures semantic similarity. (Diagram: 1 mark included; explanation: 2 marks)

**Positional Encoding Derivation (4 marks):**

**Formulas (2 marks):**
$$PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right), \quad PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{model}}}\right)$$

**Relative-Position Linear Transformation (2 marks):** Using angle addition formulas:

$$PE_{(pos+k, 2i)} = \sin(pos\omega_i)\cos(k\omega_i) + \cos(pos\omega_i)\sin(k\omega_i)$$
$$PE_{(pos+k, 2i+1)} = \cos(pos\omega_i)\cos(k\omega_i) - \sin(pos\omega_i)\sin(k\omega_i)$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} PE_{(pos+k,2i)} \\ PE_{(pos+k,2i+1)} \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \cos(k\omega_i) & \sin(k\omega_i) \\ -\sin(k\omega_i) & \cos(k\omega_i) \end{bmatrix}}_{\text{Rotation by } k\omega_i} \begin{bmatrix} PE_{(pos,2i)} \\ PE_{(pos,2i+1)} \end{bmatrix}$$

This is a **linear transformation** (specifically a 2D rotation) depending only on the relative offset $k$. A linear attention layer can therefore easily learn to attend based on relative positions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This to Your Wall):**

- **Generator** = neural net $G_\theta$ that maps latent noise $z \sim p_z(z)$ to synthetic data $G(z)$ mimicking $p_{data}(x)$.
- **GAN value function** = $\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}_x[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_z[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
- **Non-saturating generator loss** = $-\log D(G(z))$ — preferred to avoid vanishing gradients early in training.
- **Optimal discriminator** = $D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$; at Nash equilibrium, $D^*=1/2$.
- **Transformer** = encoder-decoder architecture relying **only on attention** (no recurrence, no convolution).
- **Three projections** per token: Query $Q$, Key $K$, Value $V$ via learned matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V$.
- **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** = $\text{softmax}(QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k}) V$.
- **Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$** to keep dot-product variance at 1; without it, softmax saturates and gradients vanish.
- **Multi-Head Attention** = $h$ parallel attention heads, each in its own $d_k$-dim subspace, concatenated and projected.
- **Positional Encoding** is **mandatory** because attention is permutation-invariant; sinusoidal $PE$ provides unique position vectors at all lengths.
- **$PE$ relative-offset property**: $PE_{pos+k}$ is a 2D rotation of $PE_{pos}$ by angle $k\omega_i$ — linearly learnable.
- **Encoder layer** = Multi-Head Self-Attention + FFN, with residual + LayerNorm around each.
- **Decoder layer** = Masked Self-Attention + Cross-Attention (Q from decoder, K,V from encoder) + FFN.
- **Masking** in decoder self-attention ensures **causal/autoregressive** generation (no peeking at future tokens).
- **Base model hyperparameters**: $N=6$, $d_{model}=512$, $h=8$, $d_k=d_v=64$, $d_{ff}=2048$, $\approx 65\text{M}$ parameters.
- **Residual + LayerNorm** are critical — they make training deep stacks (12, 96, even 175B-param models) feasible.
- **Transformers dominate** modern NLP (BERT, GPT, T5), vision (ViT, Swin), audio (Whisper), and multimodal (CLIP, Flamingo).
- **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance)** = standard metric to evaluate generator quality; lower is better.
- **Cycle consistency, conditional GANs, StyleGAN, BigGAN, diffusion models** all build on the Generator concept.
- **The "Attention Is All You Need" paper** (Vaswani et al., 2017) is the **single most-cited deep learning paper of the decade** — know the citation for viva.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Word Embedding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET865 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Word Embedding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Word Embedding** is a class of representation learning techniques in Natural Language Processing (NLP) where individual words, phrases, or documents from a given vocabulary are mapped to dense, low-dimensional, continuous-valued vectors of real numbers (typically of dimension $d \in [50, 300]$). The defining property of a well-constructed word embedding is that the **semantic and syntactic similarity between linguistic units is encoded as geometric proximity (e.g., cosine similarity or Euclidean distance) in the resulting vector space**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *word embedding* is a parameterized function $f: \mathcal{W} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{d}$ that maps each word $w \in \mathcal{W}$ (from a finite vocabulary) to a dense vector $\mathbf{v}_w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$, learned from large unlabeled text corpora such that words appearing in similar contexts occupy nearby regions of the embedding space.

### 1.2 Why Traditional Representations Fail: The One-Hot Limitation

Before the advent of embeddings, words were represented using **one-hot encoding**. If the vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ contains $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ unique tokens, each word $w_i$ is represented as a vector $\mathbf{e}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$ that is **1** at index $i$ and **0** everywhere else.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Failures of One-Hot Vectors:**
> 1. **High Dimensionality:** If $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 100{,}000$, each word is a $100{,}000$-dimensional vector.
> 2. **Orthogonality Curse:** $\mathbf{e}_i \cdot \mathbf{e}_j = 0$ for all $i \neq j$, meaning the model cannot perceive that *"king"* is more related to *"queen"* than to *"banana"*.
> 3. **No Semantic Encoding:** Semantic relationships (analogies, synonymy, hypernymy) are entirely lost.
> 4. **Curse of Dimensionality:** Feeding such sparse vectors into neural networks causes overfitting and vanishing-gradient pathologies.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Cartography of Meaning"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a vast, magical library** (the corpus). Every book in this library is written using the same 50,000 unique words (the vocabulary). A brilliant librarian (the embedding algorithm) decides to plot every word as a single point on a giant 2D map. He places *"king"* near *"queen"*, near *"royalty"*, near *"crown"*. He places *"apple"* near *"fruit"*, near *"banana"*, but far away from *"king"*. He even notices that the vector difference from *"man"* to *"woman"* is roughly the same as the vector difference from *"king"* to *"queen"*. This magical map is exactly what a **word embedding** is — a continuous geometric landscape where **distance = meaning** and **direction = relationship**.

### 1.4 The Distributional Hypothesis (Theoretical Foundation)

The entire field of word embedding rests on a single linguistic principle, formalized by **J. R. Firth (1957)**:

> *"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."*

Mathematically, this hypothesis states that two words $w_i$ and $w_j$ are semantically similar if and only if their **contextual distributions** $\mathcal{C}(w_i)$ and $\mathcal{C}(w_j)$ within a sliding window of the corpus are statistically similar.

$$\text{sim}_{\text{semantic}}(w_i, w_j) \;\propto\; \text{sim}_{\text{distributional}}\big(\mathcal{C}(w_i),\, \mathcal{C}(w_j)\big)$$

### 1.5 Historical Evolution (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Era | Technique | Year | Vector Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Classical | One-Hot / Bag-of-Words | Pre-2000 | Sparse, $\mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$ |
| Statistical | TF-IDF, LSA / SVD | 2000s | Sparse / Low-rank |
| Neural Static | **Word2Vec (Skip-gram, CBOW)** | **2013** | Dense, $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| Neural Static | **GloVe** | **2014** | Dense, $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| Neural Static | **fastText** | **2016** | Dense with subword info |
| Contextual | **ELMo, BERT, GPT** | 2018+ | Dense, dynamic per context |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars: Static vs. Contextual Embeddings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction (Board Exam Favorite):**
> - **Static Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe):** Each word has exactly **ONE** fixed vector regardless of context. The vector for *"bank"* is the same in *"river bank"* and *"money bank"*.
> - **Contextual Embeddings (ELMo, BERT):** Each word receives a **different** vector depending on the surrounding sentence. The model uses bidirectional LSTMs or Transformers to inject context.

### 2.2 Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) — Deep Architecture Analysis

Word2Vec is a shallow two-layer neural network trained to reconstruct linguistic contexts of words. It exists in two flavors.

#### 2.2.1 Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW)

- **Goal:** Predict the **center (target) word** from a symmetric window of surrounding **context words**.
- **Input:** $2c$ context one-hot vectors (window of size $c$ to the left and right).
- **Architecture:** Input $\rightarrow$ Projection (Embedding) Layer $\rightarrow$ Average $\rightarrow$ Output Softmax.
- **Best For:** Small datasets, faster training, frequent words.

#### 2.2.2 Skip-gram with Negative Sampling (SGNS)

- **Goal:** Predict the **surrounding context words** given a single center word.
- **Input:** One center word.
- **Output:** Probability distribution over the vocabulary for each context position.
- **Negative Sampling Trick:** Instead of computing the expensive softmax over the entire vocabulary, SGNS approximates the objective by sampling $k$ "negative" words from a unigram distribution raised to the $\frac{3}{4}$ power.

**Why raise to the power $\frac{3}{4}$?** Empirically discovered by Mikolov: it **down-weights** the probability of extremely frequent words (like *"the"*, *"a"*, *"of"*) and **up-weights** rare words, leading to a better-balanced negative sampling distribution.

### 2.3 GloVe (Global Vectors, Pennington et al., 2014)

GloVe combines the strengths of **global matrix factorization** (like LSA) and **local context window methods** (like Word2Vec).

- It constructs a global **co-occurrence matrix** $X$ where entry $X_{ij}$ counts how often word $j$ appears in the context of word $i$ across the entire corpus.
- The objective is to learn vectors whose dot-product equals the **logarithm** of the co-occurrence probability.

### 2.4 The Famous King – Queen – Man – Woman Analogy

One of the most celebrated results in NLP is the linear algebraic structure of embedding spaces:

$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{king}} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{man}} + \mathbf{v}_{\text{woman}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{v}_{\text{queen}}$$

This works because Word2Vec/GloVe embeddings **preserve linear translations of semantic relations**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every high-yield equation a KTU 2024 examiner can ask in a Part A or Part B question.

| # | Concept | Equation / Formula | Key Terms & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **One-Hot Vector** | $\mathbf{e}_i \in \{0,1\}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$ | Sparse, dimension = $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ |
| 2 | **Embedding Lookup** | $\mathbf{v}_w = \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{e}_w$ | $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times \vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$ is the embedding matrix |
| 3 | **CBOW Objective** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(w_t \mid w_{t-c} \dots w_{t+c})$ | Predicts center from context |
| 4 | **Skip-gram Objective** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \le j \le c,\, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$ | Predicts context from center |
| 5 | **Full Softmax** | $P(w_O \mid w_I) = \dfrac{\exp(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}{\sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp(\mathbf{v}_w^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})}$ | $W = \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$, expensive |
| 6 | **Negative Sampling Loss** | $\log \sigma(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)}\big[\log \sigma(-\mathbf{v}_{w_i}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I})\big]$ | $k$ = 5–20 negative samples |
| 7 | **Negative Sampling Distribution** | $P_n(w) = \dfrac{f(w)^{3/4}}{\sum_{w'} f(w')^{3/4}}$ | $f(w)$ = word frequency |
| 8 | **GloVe Loss Function** | $J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij})\big(\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \tilde{\mathbf{v}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij}\big)^{2}$ | $X_{ij}$ = co-occurrence count |
| 9 | **GloVe Weighting Function** | $f(x) = \begin{cases} (x/x_{\max})^{\alpha} & \text{if } x < x_{\max} \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | $\alpha = 0.75$, $x_{\max} = 100$ |
| 10 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{cos\_sim}(w_i, w_j) = \dfrac{\mathbf{v}_i \cdot \mathbf{v}_j}{\vert \mathbf{v}_i \vert \cdot \vert \mathbf{v}_j \vert}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$ |
| 11 | **Euclidean Distance** | $d(w_i, w_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d}(v_{i,k} - v_{j,k})^{2}}$ | Lower = more similar |
| 12 | **Analogy Equation** | $\mathbf{v}_a - \mathbf{v}_b \approx \mathbf{v}_c - \mathbf{v}_d$ | Solved as $\arg\max_d \text{cos\_sim}(b-a+c, d)$ |
| 13 | **Softmax Gradient W.r.t. Embedding** | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{w_I}} = \sum_{w=1}^{W}\big(P(w \mid w_I) - \mathbb{1}_{w=w_O}\big)\mathbf{v}_w$ | Used in backprop |
| 14 | **Perplexity (Embedding Eval)** | $\text{PPL} = 2^{H(p)}$ where $H(p) = -\sum p \log_2 p$ | Lower PPL = better LM |
| 15 | **t-SNE Dimensionality** | Project $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{300} \rightarrow \tilde{\mathbf{v}} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ | Used for visualization only |

### 2.6 Engineering Applications

- **Machine Translation:** Embeddings are the input layer of sequence-to-sequence models.
- **Search Engines & Information Retrieval:** Semantic search using cosine similarity.
- **Recommender Systems:** Item embeddings (e.g., YouTube, Spotify).
- **Sentiment Analysis & Chatbots:** Downstream NLP tasks.
- **Bio-informatics & Cheminformatics:** Embedding DNA subsequences, molecular SMILES strings.
- **Knowledge Graph Completion:** Entity embeddings in TransE, RotatE.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Skip-gram with Negative Sampling (SGNS) Loss Function

The **full softmax** of the Skip-gram model is computationally intractable for large vocabularies (e.g., $W = 10^6$). Negative Sampling reformulates the problem as a **binary classification** task.

**Step 1 — Start with the Skip-gram Maximum Likelihood Objective.**

We want to maximize the probability of observing the actual context words $w_O$ given a center word $w_I$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{softmax}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-c \le j \le c,\, j \neq 0} \log P(w_{t+j} \mid w_t)$$

**Step 2 — Express the Softmax Probability.**

The probability of observing output word $w_O$ given input word $w_I$ uses the inner product of their embedding vectors (with bias terms omitted for clarity):

$$P(w_O \mid w_I) = \frac{\exp\!\big(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)}{\sum_{w=1}^{W} \exp\!\big(\mathbf{v}_w^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)}$$

**Step 3 — Reframe as Binary Classification (Sigmoid).**

For each $(w_I, w_O)$ pair, we define a sigmoid:

$$\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-x)}$$

The probability that the pair $(w_I, w_O)$ is a **true** context pair is:

$$P(D=1 \mid w_I, w_O) = \sigma\!\big(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)$$

The probability that it is a **negative** (false) pair is:

$$P(D=0 \mid w_I, w_O) = 1 - \sigma\!\big(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big) = \sigma\!\big(-\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)$$

**Step 4 — Construct the New Objective.**

For each positive pair, we draw $k$ negative samples $w_1, \dots, w_k$ from the noise distribution $P_n(w)$. The new loss for a single positive pair is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{SGNS}} = \log \sigma\!\big(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n(w)}\Big[\log \sigma\!\big(-\mathbf{v}_{w_i}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)\Big]$$

**Step 5 — The Final Negative Sampling Distribution.**

The noise distribution that empirically works best is the unigram distribution raised to the $\frac{3}{4}$ power:

$$P_n(w) = \frac{\big(f(w)\big)^{3/4}}{\sum_{w' \in \mathcal{V}} \big(f(w')\big)^{3/4}}$$

**Reasoning for $\frac{3}{4}$:** Highly frequent words like *"the"* would otherwise overwhelm the negative samples. The exponent $0.75$ dampens the difference between rare and frequent words, ensuring more balanced learning across the vocabulary.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: GloVe's Quadratic Loss from Co-occurrence Ratios

GloVe is rooted in the observation that **ratios of co-occurrence probabilities** carry semantic information, not raw probabilities.

**Step 1 — Define the Co-occurrence Matrix.**

Let $X_{ij}$ be the number of times word $j$ occurs in the context of word $i$.

**Step 2 — Define the Conditional Probability.**

$$P_{ik} = P(w_k \mid w_i) = \frac{X_{ik}}{X_i} \quad \text{where} \quad X_i = \sum_k X_{ik}$$

**Step 3 — Examine the Probability Ratio.**

For probe words $i, j$ and a third word $k$:

$$F(w_i, w_j, \tilde{w}_k) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}$$

This ratio is large if $k$ is related to $i$ (not $j$), small if related to $j$ (not $i$), and $\approx 1$ if $k$ is unrelated to both.

**Step 4 — Encode this Ratio in Vector Space.**

Since the ratio is a scalar, the most general symmetric model that preserves it is:

$$F\!\big((\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k\big) = \frac{P_{ik}}{P_{jk}}$$

**Step 5 — Restore Symmetry.**

For the left-hand side to depend only on $(\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k$, the function $F$ must be exponential:

$$F = \exp \;\Rightarrow\; (\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j)^{\top} \tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k = \log P_{ik} - \log P_{jk}$$

**Step 6 — Decompose and Add Biases.**

Rearranging and adding word-specific bias terms $b_i, \tilde{b}_j$:

$$\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \tilde{\mathbf{v}}_k + b_i + \tilde{b}_k = \log X_{ik}$$

**Step 7 — Define the Weighted Least-Squares Loss.**

The final GloVe objective is a weighted squared error against the log-co-occurrence:

$$J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij})\Big(\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \tilde{\mathbf{v}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij}\Big)^{2}$$

where $f(X_{ij})$ is the weighting function that down-weights very rare co-occurrences and prevents $\log 0$:

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} (x / x_{\max})^{\alpha} & \text{if } x < x_{\max} \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \quad \text{with } \alpha = 0.75,\; x_{\max} = 100$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Skip-gram vs. CBOW vs. GloVe

| Property | **CBOW** | **Skip-gram** | **GloVe** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Prediction Direction | Context $\rightarrow$ Center | Center $\rightarrow$ Context | Counts $\rightarrow$ Vectors |
| Context Aggregation | Averages context vectors | Treats each context position independently | Uses global co-occurrence matrix |
| Training Speed | Faster (one prediction per window) | Slower (multiple predictions per window) | Slower on small corpora |
| Performance on Rare Words | Worse | **Better** | Better |
| Performance on Frequent Words | **Better** | Worse | Good |
| Uses Global Statistics? | No (local) | No (local) | **Yes (global)** |
| Math Foundation | Neural probabilistic LM | Neural probabilistic LM | Matrix factorization |
| KTU Exam Likelihood | High | High | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Word2Vec CBOW Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Context W t-2"] --> P["Projection Layer Embedding Matrix E"]
    A2["Context W t-1"] --> P
    A3["Context W t+1"] --> P
    A4["Context W t+2"] --> P
    P --> S["Sum and Average Layer"]
    S --> H["Hidden Layer Dimensionality d"]
    H --> O["Output Softmax Layer Vocabulary Size V"]
    O --> OUT["Predicted Center Word W t"]
    O -.Backprop.-> P
```

### 4.2 Word2Vec Skip-gram Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Input Layer One Hot Center Word"] --> E["Embedding Matrix E d x V"]
    E --> H["Hidden Layer d dimensional vector"]
    H --> O1["Output Softmax Context 1"]
    H --> O2["Output Softmax Context 2"]
    H --> O3["Output Softmax Context 3"]
    H --> ON["Output Softmax Context N"]
    O1 -.Update.-> E
    O2 -.Update.-> E
    O3 -.Update.-> E
    ON -.Update.-> E
```

### 4.3 Negative Sampling Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CW["Input Center Word wI"] --> EMB["Embedding Lookup v_wI"]
    EMB --> DP["Dot Product with v_wO"]
    P1["Positive Sample wO from True Context"] --> EMB2["v_wO"]
    EMB2 --> DP
    DP --> SIG1["Sigmoid Layer"]
    SIG1 --> L1["Loss Positive log sigma"]
    N1["Negative Sample w1"] --> EN1["v_w1"]
    N2["Negative Sample w2"] --> EN2["v_w2"]
    Nk["Negative Sample wk"] --> ENk["v_wk"]
    EN1 --> DN1["Dot Product with v_wI"]
    EN2 --> DN2["Dot Product with v_wI"]
    ENk --> DNk["Dot Product with v_wI"]
    DN1 --> SN1["Sigmoid"]
    DN2 --> SN2["Sigmoid"]
    DNk --> SNk["Sigmoid"]
    SN1 --> L2["Loss Negative sum log sigma"]
    SN2 --> L2
    SNk --> L2
    L1 --> TOT["Total SGNS Loss"]
    L2 --> TOT
```

### 4.4 End-to-End Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Word Embedding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["Raw Text Corpus Wikipedia News Articles"] --> TP["Text Preprocessing Tokenization Lowercasing Stopword Removal"]
    TP --> VS["Vocabulary Construction Frequency Filtering min count threshold"]
    VS --> WM["Windowed Pair Generation Context Window Size c"]
    WM --> M1["Model Training Word2Vec or GloVe"]
    M1 --> EM["Learned Embedding Matrix E of shape d x V"]
    EM --> APP["Downstream Applications Sentiment Analysis Machine Translation Question Answering"]
    EM --> VIZ["Visualization via t-SNE or UMAP 2D scatter plot of semantically related word clusters"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define word embedding. How does it overcome the limitations of one-hot encoded vectors?

**Model Answer:**

A **word embedding** is a dense, low-dimensional, real-valued vector representation of words learned from a large text corpus such that semantically and syntactically similar words are mapped to nearby points in the continuous vector space.

It overcomes the limitations of one-hot encoding in the following ways:

1. **Dimensionality Reduction:** One-hot vectors have dimension $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ (often millions), whereas embeddings have dimension $d \approx 50\text{–}300$.
2. **Captures Semantic Similarity:** One-hot vectors are mutually orthogonal, so all words are equidistant. Embeddings preserve relationships — cosine similarity between *"king"* and *"queen"* is high, but low between *"king"* and *"banana"*.
3. **Generalization:** Embeddings allow neural networks to share statistical strength across similar words.
4. **Analogies & Algebra:** Embeddings support linear operations like $\mathbf{v}_{\text{king}} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{man}} + \mathbf{v}_{\text{woman}} \approx \mathbf{v}_{\text{queen}}$, which is impossible with one-hot vectors.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Distinguish between **CBOW** and **Skip-gram** architectures of Word2Vec.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **CBOW** | **Skip-gram** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Prediction Direction** | Predicts the target (center) word from surrounding context words. | Predicts surrounding context words from a single center word. |
| **Input** | Multiple context words within window $c$. | A single center word. |
| **Output** | One word (the center). | $2c$ context words. |
| **Speed** | Faster — fewer training samples per window. | Slower — multiple outputs per window. |
| **Quality on Frequent Words** | Better. | Worse. |
| **Quality on Rare Words** | Worse. | **Better — produces higher-quality embeddings for infrequent words.** |
| **Use Case** | Large corpora, when speed matters. | Small corpora, when semantic precision matters. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Part B question offers an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both questions are provided below with full sub-parts and model solutions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the **Skip-gram model** with a neat diagram. Derive the loss function used in **Skip-gram with Negative Sampling (SGNS)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **GloVe** model? Explain its objective function and the weighting function $f(X_{ij})$. How does GloVe differ from Word2Vec? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The **Skip-gram model** is a shallow neural network designed to learn word embeddings by predicting the surrounding context words given a single center word. The architecture consists of:

- **Input Layer:** A one-hot vector of the center word $w_I$ of dimension $V = \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$.
- **Projection (Embedding) Layer:** Multiplied by the embedding matrix $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times V}$, producing the dense center embedding $\mathbf{v}_{w_I} \in \mathbb{R}^d$. There is no non-linear activation here.
- **Output Layer:** A softmax classifier producing a probability distribution over the entire vocabulary for each of the $2c$ context positions.

**Derivation of SGNS Loss:**

The full softmax is intractable for large $V$. Negative Sampling reformulates the problem as binary classification. For a true pair $(w_I, w_O)$, define:

$$P(D=1 \mid w_I, w_O) = \sigma\!\big(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)$$

For $k$ negative samples $w_1, \dots, w_k$ drawn from $P_n(w)$:

$$P(D=0 \mid w_I, w_i) = \sigma\!\big(-\mathbf{v}_{w_i}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)$$

The final SGNS loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{SGNS}} = \log \sigma\!\big(\mathbf{v}_{w_O}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big) + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{w_i \sim P_n}\Big[\log \sigma\!\big(-\mathbf{v}_{w_i}^{\top} \mathbf{v}_{w_I}\big)\Big]$$

The noise distribution is $P_n(w) = \dfrac{f(w)^{3/4}}{\sum_{w'} f(w')^{3/4}}$, where $f(w)$ is the word frequency. The exponent $\frac{3}{4}$ empirically down-weights the dominance of frequent words like *"the"*.

**(b)** **GloVe (Global Vectors for Word Representation)** is a hybrid model that combines global matrix factorization with local context-window methods. It is built on the insight that **ratios of word-word co-occurrence probabilities** carry semantic meaning.

**Objective Function:**

$$J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij})\Big(\mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \tilde{\mathbf{v}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij}\Big)^{2}$$

Here, $X_{ij}$ is the number of times word $j$ appears in the context of word $i$, $\mathbf{v}_i$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{v}}_j$ are the word and context embedding vectors, and $b_i, \tilde{b}_j$ are bias terms.

**Weighting Function:**

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} (x / x_{\max})^{\alpha} & \text{if } x < x_{\max} \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \quad \text{with } \alpha = 0.75,\; x_{\max} = 100$$

This function: (i) prevents $\log(0)$ for zero co-occurrences (returns $f(0) = 0$), (ii) prevents overweighting of very frequent co-occurrences, and (iii) is continuous.

**Differences from Word2Vec:**

| Aspect | **Word2Vec** | **GloVe** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Uses global co-occurrence statistics? | No (only local windows) | **Yes** |
| Training method | Stochastic gradient descent on local pairs | Adagrad on global co-occurrence matrix |
| Parallelization | Sequential word-by-word | Highly parallelizable |
| Performance on small corpora | Worse | Better |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **CBOW architecture** with diagram. Discuss its training procedure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the **distributional hypothesis** in learning word embeddings. Explain the **king-queen-man-woman analogy** with mathematical formulation. Also, list and briefly explain any **four applications** of word embeddings. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The **Continuous Bag-of-Words (CBOW)** model is the mirror image of Skip-gram: it predicts the **center (target) word** given a symmetric window of surrounding context words.

**Architecture Layers:**

1. **Input Layer:** $2c$ one-hot vectors representing context words $w_{t-c}, \dots, w_{t-1}, w_{t+1}, \dots, w_{t+c}$.
2. **Projection Layer:** Each context word is multiplied by the shared embedding matrix $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times V}$ to produce $2c$ dense vectors of dimension $d$.
3. **Summation/Average Layer:** These $2c$ vectors are averaged element-wise to produce a single context vector $\bar{\mathbf{v}} \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
4. **Output Layer:** A softmax classifier produces a probability distribution over the vocabulary, and the word with the maximum probability is predicted as the center word $\hat{w}_t$.

**Training Procedure:**

- Loss function: Categorical cross-entropy between the predicted softmax $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ and the true one-hot of the center word.
- Optimizer: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) with backpropagation, updating $\mathbf{E}$ and the output weight matrix.
- Once training converges, the embedding matrix $\mathbf{E}$ is the **final embedding dictionary**.
- CBOW converges faster than Skip-gram on large corpora because each training sample produces only one prediction, not $2c$.

**(b)** **Distributional Hypothesis** (J. R. Firth, 1957):

> *"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."*

This hypothesis asserts that **words appearing in similar linguistic contexts tend to have similar meanings**. In embedding learning, this is operationalized as: if two words $w_i$ and $w_j$ share many neighboring words in the corpus, their embedding vectors $\mathbf{v}_i$ and $\mathbf{v}_j$ will be close in vector space (high cosine similarity).

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$\text{sim}(\mathbf{v}_i, \mathbf{v}_j) = \frac{\mathbf{v}_i \cdot \mathbf{v}_j}{\vert \mathbf{v}_i \vert \cdot \vert \mathbf{v}_j \vert} \;\approx\; \text{correlation of context distributions}$$

**The Famous King–Queen Analogy:**

A celebrated property of well-trained embeddings is that semantic relations are encoded as **vector translations**:

$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{king}} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{man}} + \mathbf{v}_{\text{woman}} \approx \mathbf{v}_{\text{queen}}$$

Intuitively, the vector $\mathbf{v}_{\text{king}} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{man}}$ captures the concept of *"royalty + male"*, and adding $\mathbf{v}_{\text{woman}}$ shifts it to *"royalty + female"*. The answer is found by:

$$\hat{w} = \arg\max_{w \in \mathcal{V}} \text{cos\_sim}\!\big(\mathbf{v}_w,\; \mathbf{v}_{\text{king}} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{man}} + \mathbf{v}_{\text{woman}}\big)$$

**Four Applications of Word Embeddings:**

1. **Sentiment Classification:** Embeddings feed into LSTMs/Transformers to classify reviews as positive or negative.
2. **Machine Translation:** Sequence-to-sequence NMT models use source-language embeddings as input to a recurrent encoder.
3. **Information Retrieval & Semantic Search:** Documents are ranked by the cosine similarity of their average word embeddings to the query embedding, going beyond simple keyword matching.
4. **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** Each token's embedding is fed to a BiLSTM-CRF tagger to identify persons, organizations, and locations.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in KTU 2024 Word Embedding Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing one-hot dimension with embedding dimension** — One-hot has dimension $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$; embedding has dimension $d \ll \vert \mathcal{V} \vert$. Examiners strictly deduct if you mix these up.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{3}{4}$ exponent** in the negative sampling distribution $P_n(w) \propto f(w)^{3/4}$. This is a 1-mark "free point" if mentioned, and 1 mark lost if missed.
> 3. **Writing the GloVe loss without the weighting function $f(X_{ij})$** — full credit requires both the loss AND $f(x)$ definition.
> 4. **Drawing diagrams without labels** — a Skip-gram or CBOW diagram must show **input → embedding/projection → hidden → softmax output** with dimensions annotated.
> 5. **Mixing static and contextual embeddings** — In Q1 definitions, clearly mention whether the embedding is fixed (Word2Vec/GloVe) or dynamic (BERT/ELMo).
> 6. **Sign error in the analogy equation** — Use $\mathbf{v}_a - \mathbf{v}_b + \mathbf{v}_c \approx \mathbf{v}_d$, not $\mathbf{v}_a + \mathbf{v}_b - \mathbf{v}_c$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Word Embedding Module:**
>
> - **Word Embedding** = dense, low-dimensional, real-valued vector $\mathbf{v}_w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ learned from text corpora.
> - Solves the **curse of dimensionality** and **semantic blindness** of one-hot encoding.
> - Theoretical foundation: **Distributional Hypothesis** (Firth, 1957) — *"a word is known by the company it keeps."*
> - **Word2Vec (Mikolov, 2013)** has two architectures:
>   - **CBOW:** Predicts center from context. Faster. Good for frequent words.
>   - **Skip-gram:** Predicts context from center. Better for rare words. Higher semantic quality.
> - **Negative Sampling (SGNS)** replaces the expensive full softmax with binary classification on $k$ negative samples.
> - **Noise distribution** uses the $\frac{3}{4}$ power of unigram frequency: $P_n(w) \propto f(w)^{3/4}$.
> - **GloVe (2014)** uses **global co-occurrence statistics** via a weighted least-squares loss against $\log X_{ij}$.
> - **GloVe weighting** $f(x) = (x/x_{\max})^{\alpha}$ for $x < x_{\max}$ (with $\alpha = 0.75$, $x_{\max} = 100$) prevents $\log 0$ and over-weighting.
> - **King–Queen Analogy:** $\mathbf{v}_{\text{king}} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{man}} + \mathbf{v}_{\text{woman}} \approx \mathbf{v}_{\text{queen}}$ — linear translations encode semantic relations.
> - **Cosine similarity** $\text{cos\_sim}(i,j) = \frac{\mathbf{v}_i \cdot \mathbf{v}_j}{\vert \mathbf{v}_i \vert \vert \mathbf{v}_j \vert}$ is the standard metric; range $[-1, 1]$.
> - **Static embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe): one fixed vector per word.
> - **Contextual embeddings** (ELMo, BERT): vector depends on sentence context.
> - Typical embedding dimensions: $d \in [50, 300]$; vocabulary size $V \in [10^4, 10^6]$.
> - Visualize high-dimensional embeddings using **t-SNE** or **UMAP** projections to 2D/3D.
> - Applications: **Sentiment Analysis, Machine Translation, NER, Information Retrieval, Recommender Systems, Chatbots, Bio-informatics.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position Encoding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET865 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Position Encoding # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Position Encoding (PE)** is a mechanism used in sequence-processing neural networks (most notably the **Transformer architecture**) to inject information about the *order* or *relative/absolute position* of tokens in a sequence, because the core **self-attention** operation is mathematically **permutation-invariant** — it treats the input as an unordered set. In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology: $$\text{Input to Attention Layer} = \text{TokenEmbedding}(x_t) \;+\; \text{PositionEncoding}(t)$$ where $t \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1\}$ is the position index, and $x_t$ is the $t$-th input token. Without Position Encoding, the sentence *"The dog bit the man"* and *"The man bit the dog"* would produce **identical attention distributions** — clearly unacceptable for natural language understanding. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Cinema Ticket Counter** > > Imagine you walk into a cinema where there are 100 people standing in a random order. The usher wants to seat them, but the usher is **blind** and can only recognize people by their *clothing (token content)* — not where they are standing. If person A wears a red shirt and person B wears a blue shirt, the usher can never reconstruct the original queue order. > > Now, the manager solves this by giving each person a **numbered badge (1, 2, 3, …)** before they enter. Even though the usher still only sees clothing + badge, the badge now carries **positional information**. The same trick is what Position Encoding does for the Transformer: it attaches a position-aware vector to each token so the model can distinguish *"1st word"* from *"5th word."* ## 1.3 Why Position Encoding is Necessary (The Core Problem) The **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** mechanism is defined as: $$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$ Key observation: the matrix multiplication $QK^{\top}$ is invariant to the order of rows. If we permute the input sequence, the attention output is permuted in exactly the same way — but the **pairwise similarity scores** remain identical. The Transformer has **no built-in notion of sequence order**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** Any exam question on "Why Transformers need Position Encoding?" expects the answer: *Self-attention is permutation-invariant, so without explicit positional signals the model cannot distinguish token order, breaking tasks like translation, parsing, and language modeling.* ## 1.4 Three Families of Position Encoding (Syllabus Scope) The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 4 emphasizes the following families: | Family | Type | Learnable? | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | **Sinusoidal PE** | Absolute, Fixed | No | Original Vaswani et al. (2017) Transformer | | **Learned PE** | Absolute, Learned | Yes (Embedding layer) | BERT, GPT-2 | | **Relative PE** | Relative, Fixed/Learned | Either | T5, Transformer-XL | | **Rotary (RoPE)** | Relative, Fixed | No | LLaMA, Mistral, GPT-NeoX | | **ALiBi** | Relative, Fixed (bias) | No | BLOOM, MPT | We will analyze all of these in detail in subsequent sections. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline — Where PE Fits In The standard Transformer encoder/decoder block processes input through these stages: 1. **Token Embedding**: Each discrete token $x_t$ is mapped to a dense vector $e_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ via an embedding matrix $W_E$. 2. **Position Encoding Injection**: A position vector $p_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is added (or concatenated) to $e_t$. 3. **Self-Attention**: The combined vector $z_t = e_t + p_t$ is projected into Query, Key, and Value spaces. 4. **Feed-Forward + Residual + LayerNorm**: Standard transformer sub-layers. > [!NOTE] > In the **original Transformer** and most modern LLMs, position encoding is **added** to token embeddings, not concatenated. Concatenation would inflate dimensionality. ## 2.2 Sinusoidal Position Encoding (Vaswani et al., 2017) This is the **canonical** method asked in KTU examinations. The encoding for position $t$ and dimension index $i$ is defined as: $$\text{PE}(t, 2i) = \sin\!\left(\frac{t}{10000^{\,2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)$$ $$\text{PE}(t, 2i+1) = \cos\!\left(\frac{t}{10000^{\,2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)$$ where: - $t$ = position index (0, 1, 2, …) - $i$ = dimension-pair index, ranging $0 \le i < d_{\text{model}}/2$ - $d_{\text{model}}$ = embedding dimension (e.g., **512** in the original paper, **4096** in LLaMA-7B) - $10000$ = the base wavelength constant (a hyperparameter) ### Why these specific frequencies? The frequency for dimension $2i$ is: $$\omega_i = \frac{1}{10000^{\,2i/d_{\text{model}}}}$$ This produces a **geometric progression** of wavelengths from $2\pi$ (highest frequency at $i=0$) to $2\pi \cdot 10000$ (lowest frequency at $i = d_{\text{model}}/2 - 1$). This multi-scale structure allows the model to attend to both **local** (short-range) and **global** (long-range) positional relationships. ## 2.3 Key Property: Linear Translation via Trigonometric Identity > [!IMPORTANT] > The most elegant property of sinusoidal PE — and a **favourite KTU exam question** — is that a position offset $k$ can be expressed as a **linear transformation** of the original encoding. Using the identity $\sin(a+b) = \sin a \cos b + \cos a \sin b$: $$\text{PE}(t+k, 2i) = \sin(\omega_i t + \omega_i k) = \sin(\omega_i t)\cos(\omega_i k) + \cos(\omega_i t)\sin(\omega_i k)$$ $$\text{PE}(t+k, 2i+1) = \cos(\omega_i t + \omega_i k) = \cos(\omega_i t)\cos(\omega_i k) - \sin(\omega_i t)\sin(\omega_i k)$$ Stacking these into a 2D vector and expressing in matrix form: $$\begin{bmatrix} \text{PE}(t+k, 2i) \\ \text{PE}(t+k, 2i+1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\omega_i k) & \sin(\omega_i k) \\ -\sin(\omega_i k) & \cos(\omega_i k) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \text{PE}(t, 2i) \\ \text{PE}(t, 2i+1) \end{bmatrix}$$ This is a **rotation matrix** $R_{\omega_i}(k)$ acting on the 2D sub-vector. The model can therefore learn to attend to **relative positions** $k$ even though the encoding itself is absolute. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Input vector to attention | $z_t = e_t + p_t$ | $p_t$ = PE for position $t$ | | 2 | Sinusoidal PE (even dim) | $\text{PE}(t, 2i) = \sin(t / 10000^{2i/d})$ | $d = d_{\text{model}}$ | | 3 | Sinusoidal PE (odd dim) | $\text{PE}(t, 2i+1) = \cos(t / 10000^{2i/d})$ | Companion to (2) | | 4 | Frequency | $\omega_i = 10000^{-2i/d}$ | Geometric progression | | 5 | Wavelength | $\lambda_i = 2\pi / \omega_i = 2\pi \cdot 10000^{2i/d}$ | Range: $2\pi$ to $2\pi \cdot 10000$ | | 6 | RoPE rotation (Query) | $q'_t = R_{\Theta,t} \, q_t$ | $R_{\Theta,t}$ is block-diagonal rotation | | 7 | RoPE rotation (Key) | $k'_s = R_{\Theta,s} \, k_s$ | At source position $s$ | | 8 | RoPE attention dot-product | $q_t^{\top} k_s = (R_{\Theta,t} q_t)^{\top} (R_{\Theta,s} k_s) = q_t^{\top} R_{\Theta,t-s} k_s$ | Encodes relative offset $t-s$ | | 9 | ALiBi attention bias | $\text{score}_{ts} = q_t^{\top} k_s - m \cdot \vert t - s \vert$ | $m$ = per-head slope | | 10 | ALiBi slope (standard) | $m_h = 2^{-8 \cdot h/H}$ | $h$ = head index, $H$ = total heads | > [!NOTE] > The `\vert` symbol is used in the table above (e.g., `\vert t - s \vert`) to denote absolute value, in compliance with KTU markdown rendering rules. The actual LaTeX output remains $\vert t-s \vert$ in math mode. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Position encoding is the cornerstone of: - **Large Language Models** (GPT-4, LLaMA, Mistral): All use RoPE or ALiBi. - **Machine Translation** (Google Translate, MarianMT): Original sinusoidal PE. - **Time-Series Forecasting** (Informer, Autoformer): Modified PE for continuous time. - **Vision Transformers (ViT)**: 2D-aware PE for image patches. - **Protein Structure Prediction (AlphaFold)**: PE over residue positions. - **Code Generation (Codex, Copilot)**: PE over token positions in source code. - **Audio Models (Whisper)**: PE over 30-second spectrogram chunks. The choice of PE scheme directly affects **length generalization** (can the model handle sequences longer than those seen during training?) and **training stability**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Full Derivation: Sinusoidal Position Encoding ### Step 1 — Define the encoding dimensions We want a position vector $p_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ for each position $t$. We will pair up dimensions into $\frac{d}{2}$ pairs, indexed by $i = 0, 1, \ldots, \frac{d}{2} - 1$. ### Step 2 — Assign a unique frequency to each pair For pair $i$, we choose a base angular frequency: $$\omega_i = \frac{1}{10000^{\,2i/d}}$$ **Reasoning:** When $i = 0$, $\omega_0 = 1$ (highest frequency, shortest wavelength $2\pi$). When $i = d/2 - 1$, $\omega_{d/2-1} = 10000^{-(d-2)/d} \approx 1/10000$ (lowest frequency, longest wavelength $2\pi \cdot 10000$). The geometric spacing ensures broad coverage of frequency bands. ### Step 3 — Define sine and cosine components For position $t$ and pair index $i$: $$p_t[2i] = \sin(\omega_i \cdot t)$$ $$p_t[2i+1] = \cos(\omega_i \cdot t)$$ ### Step 4 — Verify dimensionality A full position vector concatenates all $d/2$ pairs: $$p_t = \big[\sin(\omega_0 t),\; \cos(\omega_0 t),\; \sin(\omega_1 t),\; \cos(\omega_1 t),\; \ldots,\; \sin(\omega_{d/2-1} t),\; \cos(\omega_{d/2-1} t)\big]$$ Length = $2 \cdot (d/2) = d$. ✓ ### Step 5 — Prove relative-position property (full derivation) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU exams. The goal is to show that $\text{PE}(t+k)$ can be written as a linear function of $\text{PE}(t)$, with coefficients depending only on $k$ (not on $t$). Starting from the angle-addition formulas: $$\sin(\omega_i(t+k)) = \sin(\omega_i t)\cos(\omega_i k) + \cos(\omega_i t)\sin(\omega_i k)$$ $$\cos(\omega_i(t+k)) = \cos(\omega_i t)\cos(\omega_i k) - \sin(\omega_i t)\sin(\omega_i k)$$ Substituting $\text{PE}(t, 2i) = \sin(\omega_i t)$ and $\text{PE}(t, 2i+1) = \cos(\omega_i t)$: $$\text{PE}(t+k, 2i) = \text{PE}(t, 2i)\cos(\omega_i k) + \text{PE}(t, 2i+1)\sin(\omega_i k)$$ $$\text{PE}(t+k, 2i+1) = \text{PE}(t, 2i+1)\cos(\omega_i k) - \text{PE}(t, 2i)\sin(\omega_i k)$$ Rearranging into matrix form for the 2D sub-vector at pair $i$: $$\begin{bmatrix} \text{PE}(t+k, 2i) \\ \text{PE}(t+k, 2i+1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\omega_i k) & \sin(\omega_i k) \\ -\sin(\omega_i k) & \cos(\omega_i k) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \text{PE}(t, 2i) \\ \text{PE}(t, 2i+1) \end{bmatrix}$$ The matrix on the right is a **2D rotation matrix** $R(\omega_i k)$ with angle $\theta = \omega_i k$. Therefore: $$\boxed{\;\text{PE}(t+k) = M(k) \cdot \text{PE}(t)\;}$$ where $M(k)$ is a **block-diagonal matrix** of $d/2$ rotation matrices, each depending only on the offset $k$. **Implication:** The model can learn to compute relative offsets $k$ via simple linear projections, even though the encoding is added in *absolute* form. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example Let $d_{\text{model}} = 4$ (so $d/2 = 2$ pairs), and $t = 1$. - Pair 0: $i = 0$, $\omega_0 = 10000^{0} = 1$ - Pair 1: $i = 1$, $\omega_1 = 10000^{-2/4} = 10000^{-0.5} = 1/100 = 0.01$ Then: $$\text{PE}(1) = \big[\sin(1 \cdot 1),\; \cos(1 \cdot 1),\; \sin(0.01 \cdot 1),\; \cos(0.01 \cdot 1)\big]$$ $$= \big[\sin(1),\; \cos(1),\; \sin(0.01),\; \cos(0.01)\big]$$ $$= \big[0.8415,\; 0.5403,\; 0.01000,\; 0.99995\big]$$ (Values use radians. $\sin(1) \approx 0.8415$, $\cos(1) \approx 0.5403$, $\sin(0.01) \approx 0.0100$, $\cos(0.01) \approx 0.99995$.) Similarly for $t = 2$: $$\text{PE}(2) = \big[\sin(2),\; \cos(2),\; \sin(0.02),\; \cos(0.02)\big]$$ $$= \big[0.9093,\; -0.4161,\; 0.02000,\; 0.99980\big]$$ > [!NOTE] > Observe the pattern: low-index dimensions (pair 0) change rapidly with $t$, capturing fine positional distinctions. High-index dimensions (pair 1) change slowly, acting as a coarse-grained "epoch counter." This multi-resolution property is why sinusoidal PE generalizes well to longer sequences. ## 3.3 Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) — Modern Standard **RoPE** (Su et al., 2021), used in LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon, and others, rotates the Query and Key vectors by position-dependent angles in 2D subspaces. For position $t$ and dimension-pair $i$: $$\text{RoPE}(q, t, 2i) = q_{2i} \cos(t\theta_i) - q_{2i+1} \sin(t\theta_i)$$ $$\text{RoPE}(q, t, 2i+1) = q_{2i} \sin(t\theta_i) + q_{2i+1} \cos(t\theta_i)$$ with base frequency: $$\theta_i = 10000^{-2i/d}$$ The **key invariant** of RoPE is that the dot-product $q_t^{\top} k_s$ depends *only* on the original vectors and the **relative offset** $t - s$: $$(R_{\Theta,t} q_t)^{\top} (R_{\Theta,s} k_s) = q_t^{\top} R_{\Theta,t-s} k_s$$ This is proven by substituting the rotation matrix and using $R_{\Theta,t}^{\top} R_{\Theta,s} = R_{\Theta,s-t}$: $$\begin{aligned} (R_{\Theta,t} q_t)^{\top} (R_{\Theta,s} k_s) &= q_t^{\top} R_{\Theta,t}^{\top} R_{\Theta,s} k_s \\ &= q_t^{\top} R_{\Theta,s-t} k_s \\ &= q_t^{\top} R_{\Theta,-(t-s)} k_s \\ &= (R_{\Theta,t} q_t)^{\top} (R_{\Theta,s} k_s) \end{aligned}$$ Since rotation matrices are orthogonal, $R_{\Theta,t}^{\top} = R_{\Theta,-t}$. Hence the result depends only on $t - s$. ## 3.4 ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases) ALiBi (Press et al., 2022) takes a minimalist approach: it **removes** position encoding from the embeddings and instead adds a **static, non-learned bias** to each attention score proportional to the pairwise distance: $$\text{score}_{ts} = q_t^{\top} k_s - m_h \cdot \vert t - s \vert$$ where $m_h$ is a per-head slope chosen via geometric schedule: $$m_h = 2^{-8 \cdot h / H}$$ **Why this works:** Nearby tokens receive a small (or zero) penalty; distant tokens receive a linearly increasing penalty, biasing each attention head toward a specific locality window depending on its slope. ## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix | Property | Sinusoidal | Learned Absolute | RoPE | ALiBi | |---|---|---|---|---| | Trainable parameters | **0** | $N \cdot d$ | **0** | **0** | | Extrapolation to longer sequences | **Good** | Poor (limited to training $N$) | **Very Good** | **Excellent** | | Encodes absolute position? | **Yes** | **Yes** | Encodes relative via rotation | No (only relative bias) | | Encodes relative position? | **Yes** (via linearity) | Not directly | **Yes** (built into dot-product) | **Yes** (linear bias) | | Computational overhead | Negligible | Small | Negligible | Negligible | | Used in production LLMs | NMT systems | BERT, GPT-2 | **LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon** | **BLOOM, MPT** | | Storage cost | $O(1)$ | $O(Nd)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | | Sensitivity to $N$ at inference | Low | High | Low | Very Low | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Position Encoding Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Token Sequence x1, x2, x3, ..., xN] --> B[Token Embedding Layer W_E] B --> C[Token Vectors e1, e2, ..., eN in R^d] P[Position Index t = 0, 1, 2, ..., N-1] --> Q[Position Encoding Function PE] Q --> R[Position Vectors p0, p1, ..., pN-1 in R^d] C --> S[Element-wise Addition] R --> S S --> T[Combined Vectors z1, z2, ..., zN] T --> U[Multi-Head Self-Attention Block] U --> V[Feed-Forward Network] V --> W[Output Representations] ``` ## 4.2 Sinusoidal Frequency Spectrum ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Dimension i = 0] -->|Highest Frequency| B[omega_0 = 1
Wavelength = 2pi] A --> C[Dimension i = 1] C --> D[omega_1 = 0.01
Wavelength approx 628] C --> E[Dimension i = 2] E --> F[omega_2 = 0.0001
Wavelength approx 62832] E --> G[Dimension i = d/2 - 1] G --> H[omega approx 0.0001
Wavelength approx 62832] B --> I[Geometric Progression of Frequencies] D --> I F --> I H --> I I --> J[Multi-Resolution Position Signal] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Choosing a Position Encoding Scheme ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Need Position Encoding] --> B{Domain} B -->|NLP / LLMs| C{Need length extrapolation?} B -->|Vision / Time-Series| D[2D or Temporal PE] B -->|Protein / Audio| E[Custom Continuous PE] C -->|Yes| F{Compute budget?} C -->|No| G[Learned PE or Sinusoidal] F -->|Tight| H[RoPE or ALiBi] F -->|Flexible| I[RoPE with longer training context] G --> J[BERT-style Learned PE] H --> K[RoPE: LLaMA family] H --> L[ALiBi: BLOOM family] D --> M[2D Sinusoidal for ViT] E --> N[ALiBi for sequences, Fourier Features for continuous] ``` ## 4.4 RoPE Rotation Mechanism (2D Pair Subspace) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Original Query Vector q at Position t] --> B[Split into 2D Pairs] B --> C[Pair i: q_2i, q_2i+1] C --> D[Apply 2D Rotation by Angle t times theta_i] D --> E[Rotated Query q_prime at Position t] F[Original Key Vector k at Position s] --> G[Split into 2D Pairs] G --> H[Pair i: k_2i, k_2i+1] H --> I[Apply 2D Rotation by Angle s times theta_i] I --> J[Rotated Key k_prime at Position s] E --> K[Dot Product q_prime Transpose times k_prime] J --> K K --> L[Result Depends Only on t minus s] ``` ## 4.5 Comparison Block: Major PE Families ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Position Encoding Families] --> B[Absolute PE] A --> C[Relative PE] A --> D[Rotary PE] A --> E[Bias-based PE] B --> B1[Sinusoidal Fixed] B --> B2[Learned Embedding] C --> C1[Shaw et al. 2018] C --> C2[T5 Relative Bias] C --> C3[Transformer-XL] D --> D1[RoPE LLaMA Mistral] D --> D2[xPos Extended RoPE] E --> E1[ALiBi BLOOM MPT] E --> E2[NoPE No Positional Encoding] B1 --> F1[Fixed Frequency Multiscale] B2 --> F2[Trainable N times d Matrix] C1 --> F3[Edge Features on Attention Graph] D1 --> F4[Rotation in Complex Plane] E1 --> F5[Linear Distance Penalty] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Explain in brief why the Transformer architecture requires explicit position encoding, while RNNs do not.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Transformer relies entirely on the **self-attention mechanism** to model token interactions. Self-attention computes pairwise similarities via $QK^{\top}$ followed by softmax and weighted summation with $V$. Critically, this operation is **permutation-invariant**: if the input token order is shuffled, the output representations are shuffled identically, but the underlying attention scores remain unchanged. Hence, the Transformer has **no inductive bias for sequence order**. In contrast, **RNNs** process tokens one at a time through a recurrent hidden state $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$. This sequential structure inherently encodes position: the hidden state at time $t$ is a function of all previous inputs in order. Therefore, RNNs do not require explicit position encoding. Since the Transformer is a feed-forward, parallel architecture with no sequential bias, position must be injected **manually** via position encoding vectors added to token embeddings. *(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for stating permutation invariance, 1 mark for contrast with RNN recurrence, 1 mark for the conclusion.)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **List and briefly define any two families of position encoding used in modern Transformer models.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Sinusoidal Position Encoding** (Vaswani et al., 2017): A fixed, non-learned scheme that uses sine and cosine functions of varying frequencies to encode each position. The formula is $\text{PE}(t, 2i) = \sin(t / 10000^{2i/d})$ and $\text{PE}(t, 2i+1) = \cos(t / 10000^{2i/d})$. It offers good length extrapolation. 2. **Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE)** (Su et al., 2021): Encodes position by rotating the Query and Key vectors in 2D subspaces by an angle proportional to the position. The key property is that the dot-product $q_t^{\top} k_s$ depends only on the **relative offset** $t - s$, making it the de-facto standard in modern LLMs like LLaMA and Mistral. 3. **ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases)** (Press et al., 2022): Adds a **static linear penalty** to attention scores based on token distance, with a different slope per head. Used in BLOOM and MPT. Excellent length extrapolation and zero additional parameters. *(Examiner's note: 1.5 marks each for definition + example.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With suitable mathematical formulation, explain the **Sinusoidal Position Encoding** used in the original Transformer. Derive the property that allows a relative position $k$ to be expressed as a linear transformation of absolute positions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **Sinusoidal PE**, **Learned PE**, and **RoPE** with respect to: (i) number of trainable parameters, (ii) length extrapolation capability, (iii) representation of relative position, and (iv) computational cost. Conclude with a recommendation for a production LLM scenario. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Definition (1 mark):** $$\text{PE}(t, 2i) = \sin(t / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$$ $$\text{PE}(t, 2i+1) = \cos(t / 10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}})$$ **Explanation of frequency choice (1 mark):** The frequency for pair $i$ is $\omega_i = 1 / 10000^{2i/d}$. At $i=0$, $\omega_0 = 1$ (highest). At $i = d/2 - 1$, the wavelength grows to $2\pi \cdot 10000$. This geometric progression creates a multi-resolution signal. **Derivation of relative-position linearity (5 marks):** Using the angle-addition identity: $$\sin(\omega_i(t+k)) = \sin(\omega_i t)\cos(\omega_i k) + \cos(\omega_i t)\sin(\omega_i k)$$ $$\cos(\omega_i(t+k)) = \cos(\omega_i t)\cos(\omega_i k) - \sin(\omega_i t)\sin(\omega_i k)$$ Substituting $\text{PE}(t, 2i) = \sin(\omega_i t)$, etc.: $$\text{PE}(t+k, 2i) = \text{PE}(t, 2i)\cos(\omega_i k) + \text{PE}(t, 2i+1)\sin(\omega_i k)$$ $$\text{PE}(t+k, 2i+1) = -\text{PE}(t, 2i)\sin(\omega_i k) + \text{PE}(t, 2i+1)\cos(\omega_i k)$$ In matrix form: $$\begin{bmatrix} \text{PE}(t+k, 2i) \\ \text{PE}(t+k, 2i+1) \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \cos(\omega_i k) & \sin(\omega_i k) \\ -\sin(\omega_i k) & \cos(\omega_i k) \end{bmatrix}}_{R(\omega_i k) \; \text{rotation matrix}} \begin{bmatrix} \text{PE}(t, 2i) \\ \text{PE}(t, 2i+1) \end{bmatrix}$$ Therefore $\text{PE}(t+k) = M(k) \cdot \text{PE}(t)$, where $M(k)$ is block-diagonal with rotation sub-matrices. **The relative offset $k$ is captured by a fixed linear operator independent of $t$.** ✓ #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks | Criterion | Sinusoidal | Learned PE | RoPE | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Trainable parameters | **0** | $N \cdot d$ | **0** | | (ii) Length extrapolation | **Good** (smooth analytic formula) | **Poor** (clip at training $N$) | **Very Good** (continuous rotation) | | (iii) Relative position | Implicit (via rotation matrices) | Not directly encoded | **Explicit** (built into $QK^{\top}$) | | (iv) Compute cost | Negligible (one-time computation) | Embedding lookup | Negligible (2D rotations) | **Recommendation (1 mark):** For a production LLM handling long contexts (e.g., 32K–128K tokens), **RoPE** is the recommended choice because it (a) has zero extra parameters, (b) inherently captures relative position in attention scores, and (c) extrapolates gracefully to longer sequences when combined with techniques like NTK-aware scaling or YaRN. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the **Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE)** mechanism. Prove mathematically that the dot-product of rotated queries and keys depends only on the relative position $t - s$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe **ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases)**. Show the formula for the attention score with ALiBi and explain the geometric slope schedule $m_h = 2^{-8h/H}$. Discuss its advantages and limitations compared to RoPE. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Mechanism (2 marks):** RoPE rotates the Query and Key vectors in each 2D subspace by an angle proportional to their absolute positions. For Query at position $t$ and dimension-pair $i$: $$q'_t[2i] = q_t[2i]\cos(t\theta_i) - q_t[2i+1]\sin(t\theta_i)$$ $$q'_t[2i+1] = q_t[2i]\sin(t\theta_i) + q_t[2i+1]\cos(t\theta_i)$$ where $\theta_i = 10000^{-2i/d}$. Key $k_s$ is rotated analogously at position $s$. **Proof that dot-product encodes relative position (5 marks):** The rotation in pair $i$ can be written as $R_{\Theta,t}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$, and the full rotation is a block-diagonal matrix $R_{\Theta,t}$. The attention contribution from pair $i$ is: $$(R_{\Theta,t}^{(i)} q_t^{(i)})^{\top} (R_{\Theta,s}^{(i)} k_s^{(i)}) = q_t^{(i)\top} R_{\Theta,t}^{(i)\top} R_{\Theta,s}^{(i)} k_s^{(i)}$$ Since each $R^{(i)}$ is a 2D rotation, $R_{\Theta,t}^{(i)\top} = R_{\Theta,-t}^{(i)}$. Therefore: $$= q_t^{(i)\top} R_{\Theta,s-t}^{(i)} k_s^{(i)} = q_t^{(i)\top} R_{\Theta,-(t-s)}^{(i)} k_s^{(i)}$$ Summing over all pairs, the total dot-product is: $$(R_{\Theta,t} q_t)^{\top} (R_{\Theta,s} k_s) = q_t^{\top} R_{\Theta,t-s} k_s$$ This is a function of $q_t$, $k_s$, and the **relative offset $t-s$** only — independent of absolute positions. ✓ #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **ALiBi description (2 marks):** ALiBi modifies the attention score by subtracting a linear penalty proportional to the distance between tokens: $$\text{score}_{ts} = q_t^{\top} k_s - m_h \cdot \vert t - s \vert$$ No position vectors are added to embeddings. The penalty is a **static bias** depending only on the head index $h$ and distance $\vert t - s \vert$. **Geometric slope schedule (2 marks):** Each attention head is assigned a slope: $$m_h = 2^{-8 \cdot h / H}, \quad h = 0, 1, \ldots, H-1$$ For $h=0$: $m_0 = 1$ (steepest penalty, head attends locally). For $h = H-1$: $m_{H-1} = 2^{-8} = 1/256$ (gentlest penalty, head attends globally). This geometric schedule spans **four orders of magnitude** of locality windows across heads. **Comparison with RoPE (3 marks):** | Aspect | ALiBi | RoPE | |---|---|---| | Length extrapolation | **Excellent** (trivially extends) | Good (needs NTK-aware scaling) | | Captures fine-grained relative offsets? | **No** (only linear) | **Yes** (full rotation) | | Bias addition vs. vector rotation | Bias (additive on scores) | Rotation (multiplicative on Q/K) | | Trainable parameters | **0** | **0** | | Standard in production | BLOOM, MPT | LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon | ALiBi is **simpler** and more robust to extreme length extrapolation, but RoPE preserves richer relative-position structure for moderate sequence lengths. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the $+1$ in $10000^{2i/d}$:** Many students write the formula as $10000^{i/d}$ instead of $10000^{2i/d}$.
